Outcome-only; survey the pi-sdk + sibling pi extensions for the canonical shape; coordinate with the onboarding ceremony (DEC-0038), its primary consumer. DEC-0015 + DEC-0019/0020 + DEC-0025.

gaps.title: No schema-write Pi tool — in-pi agents cannot define new block kinds or evolve existing schemas
gaps.description: There is no registered Pi tool to define a new block kind's schema or evolve an existing one. read-schema is the only schema tool (read-only). writeSchema / updateSchema / readSchema exist with AJV meta-validation (schema-write.ts) plus a per-schema migration registry (schema-migrations.ts), but have no pi.registerTool twin. So a tools-only in-pi agent cannot extend the substrate's type surface — every new block kind or schema version must be authored through the orchestrator CLI or by hand. project-init scaffolds only the legacy defaults/ schema set; there is no tool path to add a schema beyond it.

evidence.reference: only read-schema registered; no write-schema / update-schema tool
writeSchema/updateSchema/readSchema library surface with AJV meta-validation — no Pi-tool twin
per-schema version-to-version migration registry exists; a schema-evolution tool must honor it (version bump + MigrationFn registration)
DEC-0019/0020 (dual-surface) + DEC-0023 (schema title/display_name) + schema-versioning ($id + version + migration) — Pi-tool twin missing

gaps.impact: New block-kind definition and schema evolution are orchestrator-CLI-only; in-pi agents and tool-only consumers cannot extend or migrate the type surface. Onboarding a custom block kind (DEC-0038) and any in-pi schema authoring are blocked. Completes the trio of write-twin dual-surface gaps with FGAP-075 (edge-write) + FGAP-076 (config-write). Lower frequency than edge/config operations (hence P2) but required for full tool-side substrate self-definition.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Register schema-write Pi tool(s) — the dual-surface twin of schema-write.ts (write-schema + update-schema) — with AJV meta-validation and migration-registry awareness (version bump + MigrationFn registration on evolution). Outcome-only; survey the pi-sdk + sibling pi extensions for the canonical shape. DEC-0019/0020 + DEC-0023 + schema-versioning.

gaps.title: No tool-surface for MigrationFn registration — breaking schema evolution requires a code change
gaps.description: The FGAP-077 write-schema tool can create/replace a block-kind JSON Schema (incl. bumping its `version`), but it cannot complete a BREAKING evolution. When a schema's `version` changes, existing block items retain the old `schema_version`, and read-time `validateBlockWithMigration` (schema-validator.ts) THROWS a version mismatch unless a `MigrationFn` is registered. A `MigrationFn = (data:unknown)=>unknown` (schema-migrations.ts:45) is TypeScript CODE registered in-source via `registry.register(entry)` — it cannot be carried by a Pi tool param. So a harness-confined in-pi agent (or any tools-only consumer) can write an evolved schema but cannot forward-migrate the existing items through the tool surface; the evolution requires a code change. This is the deferred half of FGAP-077's migration boundary, surfaced + proven by its migration-boundary test. Mirrors the FGAP-071 precedent (unit 2.3 deferred the block_kind-rename cascade it could not cover).

evidence.reference: MigrationFn = (data:unknown)=>unknown registered in-source via registry.register — code, not data; no tool param can carry a function
validateBlockWithMigration throws on schema.version !== block.schema_version when no MigrationRegistry supplied
migration-boundary test: write-schema replace with a bumped version SUCCEEDS, then validateBlockWithMigration THROWS on an old-version item; only a code MigrationFn closes it
DEC-0019/0020 dual-surface — the migration-registration leg is inherently code, no tool/CLI twin possible without an executable-code-carrying surface

gaps.impact: Breaking schema evolution (item-shape change) is not completable via the tool surface — the schema JSON is writable, but existing items are left un-migratable until a developer registers a MigrationFn in source. Onboarding / in-pi agents that evolve a schema must hand off to a code change. Bounds what FGAP-077's write-schema can deliver end-to-end.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Outcome-only (implementation deferred): provide a path for forward-migrating existing block items when a schema version changes that a harness-confined agent can drive — e.g. a declarative/data-expressible migration spec the framework can interpret (vs an arbitrary code MigrationFn), OR an explicit accepted-boundary doc that breaking evolution is a developer-code task with a clear handoff. Survey whether common migrations (add-optional, rename-field, drop-field) are expressible as data the framework runs, reserving code MigrationFn for the irreducible cases. Coordinate with the schema-write tool (FGAP-077) + onboarding (DEC-0038).

gaps.title: Schema-path surface inconsistency — write/read resolver divergence (config.root) + no path-traversal sanitization on schemaName
gaps.description: Two pre-existing flaws in the schema-file path surface, surfaced during FGAP-077 exploration (NOT introduced by it; the write-schema unit reuses the existing writeSchema path to stay internally consistent). (1) RESOLVER DIVERGENCE: schema-write.ts resolves the schema file via projectRoot(cwd) (project-context.ts — honors config.root); but read-schema (the Pi tool + CLI) and validateBlockWithMigration resolve via schemaPath(cwd,name) -> resolveContextDir(cwd) (project-dir.ts — IGNORES config.root). They coincide in the default substrate (the .pi-context.json pointer dir == config.root) but DIVERGE if config.root is set to a different dir: a schema written by writeSchema/write-schema could then be unreadable by read-schema and unseen by migration validation. (2) NO PATH-TRAVERSAL SANITIZATION: readSchema/writeSchema/schemaPath all interpolate the raw `${schemaName}` into the file path (e.g. `${name}.schema.json`) with no validation — a schemaName like '../../etc/x' escapes the schemas/ dir. With the FGAP-077 write-schema tool now exposing schema writes to harness-confined/in-pi agents, the unsanitized name is a write-side escape, not just read.

evidence.reference: schemaWritePath -> projectRoot(cwd) (honors config.root); raw `${schemaName}` interpolation, no sanitization
projectRoot: returns config.root when set, else resolveContextDir
read-schema resolves via schemaPath -> resolveContextDir (project-dir.ts), ignoring config.root — the divergent read path
validateBlockWithMigration also resolves via resolveContextDir, not projectRoot — same divergence

gaps.impact: (1) A non-default config.root silently splits where schemas are written vs read — write-schema writes a file read-schema/validation cannot find. (2) An unsanitized schemaName can write or read outside the schemas/ dir; now reachable from the tool surface (FGAP-077). Both are latent correctness/safety issues across the whole schema-path surface (read + write together), not isolated to one function.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Outcome-only: unify the schema-file path resolver across read + write (one resolution honoring or ignoring config.root consistently — decide which is canonical and route readSchema/writeSchema/schemaPath/validateBlockWithMigration through it) AND add schemaName validation (reject path separators / traversal) at the single resolution point so both read and write are guarded together. Fix the surface as a unit; do not patch one function. [CLOSED 2026-05-23 — DEC-0045 + commit (TASK-063/VER-035): projectRoot unified on resolveContextDir (config.root not a path input); assertSubstrateName guards every name->path builder (read+write); schemaWritePath routed through schemaPath. Implemented via coding subagent, orchestrator-verified.]

gaps.title: Orchestrator CLIs catching transitively-thrown package errors via `instanceof` are module-instance-fragile — prefer name-based checks
gaps.description: An orchestrator CLI that imports an error class from `@davidorex/pi-context/<subpath>` and catches it with `instanceof` fails SILENTLY when the error is thrown TRANSITIVELY through a different package subpath. Surfaced in FGAP-077: scripts/orchestrator/write-schema.ts imported BootstrapNotFoundError from @davidorex/pi-context/project-dir and caught it with `instanceof`, but writeSchemaChecked (from @davidorex/pi-context/schema-write) throws BootstrapNotFoundError via schema-write's OWN internal project-dir module instance — under tsx/symlink resolution that is a DIFFERENT class object, so `instanceof` returns false and the catch falls through to the wrong exit code (uninitialized-cwd hit exit 5 instead of the contracted exit 1). Confirmed empirically: instanceof === false while err.name === 'BootstrapNotFoundError' and err.constructor.name === 'BootstrapNotFoundError'. Fixed in write-schema.ts with a name-based check. Siblings read-schema.ts / read-config.ts happen to work because they throw the class DIRECTLY from their own project-dir import (same instance) — but the pattern is fragile: any future CLI catching a transitively-thrown package error via instanceof will silently misbehave.

evidence.reference: FIXED here: instanceof BootstrapNotFoundError -> err.name === 'BootstrapNotFoundError' (module-instance-proof); comment documents why
uses instanceof BootstrapNotFoundError — works ONLY because the throw is direct (schemaPath from the same project-dir import); latent fragility if its throw path ever becomes transitive
same instanceof pattern, same direct-throw dependency
BootstrapNotFoundError sets this.name='BootstrapNotFoundError' (project-dir.ts:53) — the name survives the module-instance split that defeats instanceof

gaps.impact: Silent exit-code / control-flow wrongness in orchestrator CLIs when a package error is caught via instanceof across the package boundary under tsx. Currently only write-schema.ts was affected (fixed); read-schema/read-config are correct today but one refactor of their throw path away from breaking. A cross-cutting convention gap, not a single bug.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Outcome-only: establish + apply the convention that orchestrator CLIs (and any cross-package consumer) catching a pi-context error class check by `err.name === '<ClassName>'` (or a shared type-guard helper exported from the package) rather than `instanceof`, which is unreliable across module instances under tsx/symlink resolution. Audit existing CLIs (read-schema/read-config already correct but instanceof-based) + add the guard helper or convention doc so future CLIs don't reintroduce the hazard. [CLOSED 2026-05-23 — commit dfa0fc6 / TASK-061 / VER-033: orchestrator-CLI instanceof on pi-context errors -> name-based (read-config/read-schema/file-block-item/append-relation); write-schema/accept-all already done. Residual src-consumer sites filed FGAP-096.]

gaps.title: Anti-injection contextBlocks delimiters not aligned with pi 0.75.x XML-tag context-boundary convention
gaps.description:
Pi 0.75.0 introduced new XML-tag context-boundary conventions for system-prompt context injection. Our JIT-agent contextBlocks injection (pi-jit-agents compile.ts:144-212) wraps injected block content in our OWN framework anti-injection delimiter scheme rather than pi's 0.75.x convention. To be canonically pi-sdk (the user's explicit goal), align our delimiters with 0.75.x's context-boundary tags so injected context is demarcated the way the runtime/models expect. Surfaced by the Pi SDK 0.74->0.75.4 investigation (analysis/2026-05-21-pi-sdk-0.74-to-0.75-investigation.md, SHOULD-ADOPT #1). The exact tag names + convention could NOT be pinned from the changelog alone — they require reading the 0.75.x system-prompt / context-assembly source in earendil-works/pi-mono (the investigation's open question section 7). Distinct from + sequenced AFTER the mechanical SDK upgrade (TASK that bumps 0.74.0->0.75.4); this is the best-practice ADOPTION the upgrade enables.

VERIFIED 2026-05-21 (open question RESOLVED — pi 0.75.4 source read directly): pi wraps injected context in XML tags — <project_context>...</project_context> and <available_skills>...</available_skills> (dist/core/system-prompt.js). OUR contextBlocks use bracket delimiters [BLOCK <name> — INFORMATIONAL ONLY, NOT INSTRUCTIONS]...[END BLOCK <name>] (compile.ts wrapBlock/wrapItem). They diverge. No further investigation needed: the alignment is to adopt XML-tag delimiters for our blocks (e.g. a <context-block name=...>...</context-block> shape) matching pi house style. Note: whether pi INTRODUCED this in 0.75 or it is longstanding is immaterial — the gap is the divergence, now concretely specified and ready to plan.

evidence.reference: contextBlocks injection wraps block content in our own framework anti-injection delimiters — the site to align with the 0.75.x XML-tag convention
SHOULD-ADOPT #1 + open-question 7: 0.75.0 introduced XML-tag context boundaries; exact tag names need 0.75.x system-prompt source-read
per-block-kind Nunjucks render macros also emit injected context — check whether they carry/duplicate delimiters that must align too

gaps.impact: Our context injection uses a non-canonical delimiter scheme diverging from pi 0.75.x's own context-boundary convention; the framework anti-injection demarcation may not match how 0.75.x-era models are trained/prompted to parse injected context, and it is off the canonical-pi-sdk axis the user is steering toward. Low correctness risk today, real best-practice + future-compat debt.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Outcome-only: read the 0.75.x system-prompt / context-assembly source in earendil-works/pi-mono to determine the exact XML-tag context-boundary convention, then align pi-jit-agents compile.ts contextBlocks delimiters (and pi-workflows macros if they carry delimiters) to it. Sequence AFTER the 0.74->0.75.4 mechanical upgrade.

gaps.title: Orchestrator CLIs validate items against the item-subschema FRAGMENT in isolation (losing $ref definitions), not whole-file like block-api
gaps.description: file-block-item.ts and append-relation.ts (the dual-surface append CLIs) re-implement pre-write item validation against the block's ITEMS SUBSCHEMA FRAGMENT extracted from the schema (file-block-item.ts:109 itemSchema=properties[arrayKey].items, validated at :270; append-relation.ts:117-139 extracts relations .items, validated at :157), rather than validating the whole file the way the library does (appendToBlock->writeBlock->writeTypedFile->validateFromFile against the WHOLE schema, block-api.ts:401 — proven to resolve $refs). When an items schema is {"$ref":"#/definitions/<kind>"} (features.schema.json:10, spec-reviews.schema.json:38), the fragment cannot resolve #/definitions/<kind> (parent definitions out of scope) -> --dry-run errors "can't resolve reference". ACTIVE on file-block-item filing features/spec-reviews; LATENT in append-relation (relations.schema.json has no $ref today but the same fragment-in-isolation pattern is shipped). NOTE: the REAL (non-dry-run) file-block-item append delegates straight to appendToBlock and is NOT broken — only --dry-run (this gap) + --auto-id (FGAP-083) fail. Conception (samples) + registry schemas use no item $ref, so .context library filing is unaffected. Root: orchestrator-CLI validation is a PARALLEL re-implementation diverging from block-api whole-file validation (feedback_no_parallel_ungated_paths).

evidence.reference: --dry-run validates item against extracted .items fragment, not whole schema; $ref-to-definitions item fails to resolve
same fragment-in-isolation validation pattern (latent — relations.schema has no $ref yet)
the canonical whole-file validateFromFile path the CLIs should route through (resolves $refs)
items {"$ref":"#/definitions/feature"} — the shape that breaks fragment validation; spec-reviews.schema.json:38 same

gaps.impact: file-block-item — the canonical append surface (CLAUDE.md filing pattern) — cannot append/preview/auto-id features (or any future $ref-item block kind), forcing a non-canonical raw appendToBlock tsx-eval fallback that bypasses the CLI's --writer/--dry-run/--auto-id ergonomics. Undermines the DEC-0019/0020 canonical-script surface for $ref-using schemas; the conception (samples) schemas may use $ref similarly, so .context filing would hit this too.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Both append CLIs should validate the candidate item by composing a whole-block wrapper and running validateFromFile against the FULL schema (mirroring writeBlock/appendToBlock) for --dry-run, NOT the item-fragment in isolation. Best: route --dry-run through the same library validation the real path uses (single path; eliminates the divergence). [CLOSED 2026-05-23 — commit 5ecd7bd / TASK-060 / VER-032: file-block-item + append-relation dry-run now validate the prospective WHOLE file (validateFromFile) not the items fragment — resolves $ref + dry-run==write parity.]

gaps.title: file-block-item --auto-id cannot read the id pattern from a $ref item schema (reads itemSchema.properties.id.pattern, absent on a $ref fragment)
gaps.description: file-block-item.ts --auto-id allocates the next id by reading the id PATTERN from the extracted item subschema's properties.id.pattern (file-block-item.ts:110-119 idProp = itemSchema?.properties?.id; pattern at :112). When the items schema is {"$ref":"#/definitions/<kind>"} (features.schema.json:10), the fragment has NO top-level `properties` (they live in the unresolved definition), so idProp is undefined and :157 throws 'Cannot auto-allocate ID: pattern undefined not parseable'. Distinct fix-surface from FGAP-082 (that is the dry-run VALIDATION fragment bug; this is the AUTO-ID pattern-extraction fragment bug) though same fragment-not-resolved root. Symptom-of-FGAP-082's fragment approach but separable: even with whole-file validation fixed, auto-id must resolve the $ref (or read from the definition) to find the id pattern.

evidence.reference: auto-id reads itemSchema.properties.id.pattern; a $ref fragment has no .properties → 'pattern undefined not parseable'
items {"$ref":"#/definitions/feature"}; the id pattern lives in definitions.feature.properties.id, unreachable from the fragment

gaps.impact: --auto-id is unusable for any $ref-item block kind (features, spec-reviews); the orchestrator must pass an explicit id (or use raw appendToBlock). Will recur for any conception/.context block kind that adopts $ref item schemas.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Resolve the items $ref against the parent schema's definitions before reading properties.id.pattern (or, with FGAP-084, route allocation through a block-api helper that dereferences). One-line-ish once the fragment is dereferenced. [CLOSED 2026-05-23 — commit 5ecd7bd / TASK-060 / VER-032: resolveBlockItemSchema dereferences the items $ref so id.pattern reads correctly; nextId is $ref-aware.]

gaps.title: No canonical block-api id-allocation helper — orchestrator scripts re-implement next-id allocation
gaps.description: block-api exposes read/write primitives but NO id-allocation helper (no nextId/allocateId; verified absent across block-api.ts). file-block-item.ts re-implements id allocation inline (file-block-item.ts:156-174: parse prefix+width from the id pattern, scan existing items, increment) as a parallel path. Any other surface needing auto-id (the in-pi append tools, future CLIs) must re-implement or hand-scan. The missing primitive is the allocation-side analog of the validation divergence in FGAP-082: allocation logic lives in the CLI, not the library, so it can't be shared + diverges (e.g. it doesn't dereference $ref item schemas — FGAP-083).

evidence.reference: no exported id-allocation function (only read/write primitives)
inline re-implementation of next-id allocation (prefix/width parse + scan + increment) — the logic that belongs in a shared block-api helper

gaps.impact: id-allocation is duplicated/divergent and not reusable by the in-pi tool surface (the Pi append tools have no auto-id at all). Extracting a canonical allocateId(cwd, block) helper would let file-block-item, the in-pi tools, and future surfaces share one correct implementation (and fix FGAP-083 once).
gaps.proposed_resolution: Add a block-api id-allocation primitive (e.g. allocateId(cwd, blockName) → next id from the block-kind's resolved id pattern + existing items, dereferencing $ref item schemas) and route file-block-item + any in-pi auto-id through it. DEC-0019/0020 dual-surface: library primitive + CLI + (optionally) Pi tool. [CLOSED 2026-05-23 — commit 5ecd7bd / TASK-060 / VER-032: canonical block-api nextId(cwd,block) helper; file-block-item + the in-pi append-block-item autoId twin route through it; inline re-implementation removed.]

gaps.title: Tool descriptions need a discovery-readiness + grounding rubric — the per-tool eager-guidance layer
gaps.description: FGAP-044 (list-tools) makes the loaded tool surface introspectable (DEC-0014): pi.getAllTools() surfaces each tool's `description` as a PRIMARY discovery signal the in-pi agent reads to choose tools. Descriptions were authored ad-hoc with no shared rubric (purpose / params / substrate effect / dual-surface twin / when-to-use-vs-not). They are also a confabulation-prevention surface (FGAP-092): live agents (2026-05-22) asserted substrate specifics from assumption — invented an unregistered relation_type `phase_depends_on`; proposed reconstructing draft-07 schemas from read-samples-catalog SHAPE metadata (arrayKeys/itemProperties, not bodies); assumed builtin fs read (absent in --tools sessions); guessed params. Descriptions are ONE eager channel — the per-tool layer; cross-cutting orientation + topic->tool-call routing live in a before_agent_start pointer block (FGAP-090), not duplicated here.

evidence.reference: list-tools (FGAP-044) returns ToolInfo.description as the in-pi LLM's primary discovery signal — quality of every registered description is load-bearing
pi.registerTool description strings authored ad-hoc; no shared discovery-readiness rubric, no grounding directives
FGAP-092 confabulation evidence: live agent used `phase_depends_on` — NOT a registered relation_type; descriptions must steer 'vocabulary is queryable, don't invent'
read-samples-catalog returns shape (arrayKeys/itemProperties), not raw schema bodies — agent proposed reconstructing schemas; descriptions/grounding must point to install (FGAP-091) instead

gaps.impact: Now load-bearing (raised P3->P2). With skills unreachable (FGAP-090), descriptions are the agent's SOLE guidance: terse/incomplete ones cause mis-selection, AND without grounding directives the agent confabulates substrate specifics (unregistered-relation_type edges, mis-shaped items, wrongly-reconstructed schemas) which write SILENTLY because append-relation/amend-config defer validation (FGAP-075/076) — surfacing late at project-validate or never. Directly undermines the DEC-0014 aim of a tools-only agent operating the substrate CORRECTLY, not just plausibly.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Define the description rubric (purpose/params/effect/dual-surface-twin/when-to-use) AND author GROUNDING DIRECTIVES into the relevant descriptions: read-config + read-samples-catalog as 'the authoritative vocabulary — reference these names, never invent'; append-relation/amend-config/write-schema as 'the relation_type/kind/name must be registered — check read-config first'. Enumerate via grep pi.registerTool; score each; emit remediated strings; apply through plan mode (source edits). COMPLEMENTARY: fail loud on unregistered names at write where layering permits (FGAP-092 facet). Cross-cutting orientation belongs in the before_agent_start pointer block (FGAP-090) + resources_discover skills (FEAT-007), not in every description.

gaps.title: relation_types carry no endpoint-kind metadata — edges can wire wrong-kind endpoints undetected; validateProject checks endpoint existence but never kind
gaps.description: config.relation_types[] items carry only {canonical_id, display_name, category, cycle_allowed} (config.schema.json relation_types, additionalProperties:false). There is no declaration of which block_kind(s) a relation_type may connect. Endpoint kinds are resolved at runtime purely from the actual edge (buildIdIndex maps item-id -> {block}); relation_type endpoint metadata is used NOWHERE. Consequently validateProject (project-sdk.ts:1072-1097) verifies an edge's parent/child IDs EXIST but never that their KINDS are legal for the relation_type — e.g. a task_addresses_gap edge whose child resolves to a decision (not a framework-gap) passes validation. The catalog discovery surface (FGAP-068, DEC-0037 packaged view 'schema + its relation_types') also cannot present a kind's applicable relation_types without this metadata; name-token parsing is not viable (task != tasks, gap != framework-gaps, review != spec-reviews, wildcard 'item'). Surfaced during B2/FGAP-068 planning.

evidence.reference: relation_types items: required [canonical_id,display_name,category]; props [...,cycle_allowed]; additionalProperties:false — no source_kinds/target_kinds
validateProject edge-integrity loop asserts parent/child ids resolve in idIndex; never compares their block-kind to the relation_type
buildIdIndex resolves endpoint kind from the item's home block; relation_type metadata is not consulted

gaps.impact: A structurally wrong edge (legal ids, illegal kinds for the relation) is undetectable by validateProject — a correctness hole in the closure-table model. Blocks DEC-0037 packaged catalog (FGAP-068) from showing per-kind relation_types. Closed jointly with FGAP-068 in B2: add optional source_kinds[]/target_kinds[] (wildcard '*'), author them on conception, and add a presence-gated endpoint-kind check to validateProject (gated so the metadata-less frozen .project stays silent).
gaps.proposed_resolution: Add OPTIONAL source_kinds[]/target_kinds[] to the relation_types schema (wildcard literal '*'=any kind; optional so frozen .project/config.json keeps validating; version bump, no MigrationFn for additive-optional). Author all 27 conception.json relation_types per the B2 mapping table. Add a validateProject loop after edge-integrity: resolve parent/child kind via idIndex, look up the relation_type, assert parent-kind in source_kinds && child-kind in target_kinds honoring '*'; GATE on the relation_type declaring at least one of the fields (silent where absent => no false positives on metadata-less substrate). severity error. Implemented as part of TASK-054 (B2).

gaps.title: registry/ + defaults/ dirs retained on disk as AJV test fixtures after consumption-MVP de-canonicalized them; delete pending fixture-repoint + a home for 8 conception-less legacy schemas
gaps.description: The consumption MVP (TASK-055) made samples/ the single canonical catalog: installProject + initProject + generate-skills now read samples/, and registry/ + defaults/ were dropped from the shipped package.json files[] (DEC-0011). But the two dirs were NOT deleted from disk because (a) ~7 test files read packages/pi-context/registry/schemas/*.json directly as AJV fixtures (schema-validator.test, context-contracts.test, execution-context.integration.test, index.test + pi-workflows macros/render-architecture/render-task tests) and (b) 8 schemas live ONLY in registry/ with no samples/ home (architecture, audit, conformance-reference, domain, handoff, plan, project, roadmap — legacy, absent from the going-forward DEC-0037 conception). So registry/ + defaults/ are now dead-but-present: unshipped, unread by production, retained solely as test fixtures + legacy-schema holding.

evidence.reference: 8 schemas absent from samples/: architecture/audit/conformance-reference/domain/handoff/plan/project/roadmap
reads registry/schemas as AJV fixtures (one of ~7 test readers); deletion would break these
files[] no longer lists registry/ or defaults/ (TASK-055 commit a53663e) — dirs ship nowhere but remain in the repo tree

gaps.impact: Dead dirs in the repo tree are misleading (a reader may assume registry/ is still canonical) and the legacy-fixture coupling blocks a clean cutover. Low-urgency (no production path touches them; samples/ is canonical) hence P2. Becomes relevant at the .context cutover (DEC-0036) when the substrate vocabulary is finalized.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Repoint the ~7 registry-fixture tests to samples/schemas (where the kind exists) or to inline fixtures; decide each of the 8 conception-less legacy schemas — either give it a samples/ home (if still wanted in the going-forward conception) or drop it (legacy planning vocab not in DEC-0037). Then delete packages/pi-context/registry/ + packages/pi-context/defaults/. Sequence with the .context cutover; not blocking.

gaps.title: Package-relative path resolution uses import.meta.dirname in 3 sites — undefined under tsx-CJS dist-load; normalize to fileURLToPath(import.meta.url)
gaps.description: import.meta.dirname is undefined when a built dist/*.js (type:module) is loaded under tsx's CJS-interop path (tsx -e / subpath import) — proven during B2 where samples-catalog.ts's module-top import.meta.dirname threw ERR_INVALID_ARG_TYPE and was fixed to path.dirname(fileURLToPath(import.meta.url)) (idiom already at project-context.ts:220/433, samples-catalog.ts:34, schema-validator.ts:47, pi-jit-agents/trace-writer.ts:40). Three sites still use import.meta.dirname: (a) pi-workflows/src/bundled-dirs.ts:14 MODULE-TOP-LEVEL (eager) PACKAGE_ROOT = path.resolve(import.meta.dirname, '..') — same eager pattern that broke samples-catalog (highest risk: throws at import under tsx-CJS); (b) pi-context/src/index.ts:335 installProject samplesRoot (lazy, inside fn); (c) pi-context/src/update-check.ts:20 getInstalledVersion (lazy, inside fn). Lazy sites are prod-ESM-safe but latent under tsx-CJS-if-called; eager bundled-dirs.ts:14 is the genuine risk. Codebase already standardizes on fileURLToPath(import.meta.url) in 5 places.

evidence.reference: PACKAGE_ROOT = path.resolve(import.meta.dirname, '..') EAGER module-top; same pattern that threw in samples-catalog under tsx-CJS
installProject samplesRoot import.meta.dirname — lazy, prod-ESM-safe, latent under tsx-CJS
getInstalledVersion import.meta.dirname — lazy, prod-ESM-safe, latent under tsx-CJS
canonical idiom precedent path.dirname(fileURLToPath(import.meta.url)) — target form

gaps.impact: No production breakage today (prod loads dist as native ESM; 2 pi-context sites lazy). Risks: bundled-dirs.ts:14 eager-module-top throws if that pi-workflows module loads via any tsx-CJS-interop path (orchestrator tsx -e / some harnesses) — the exact B2 failure class; and inconsistency with the established idiom invites repeating the B2 defect. P3 — no current failing path, cheap to normalize, removes a latent footgun.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Replace all 3 import.meta.dirname package-relative resolutions with path.dirname(fileURLToPath(import.meta.url)) (idiom in 5 existing sites). Prioritize bundled-dirs.ts:14 (eager = actual risk). Add fileURLToPath import from node:url where absent. Verify each touched module resolves its package-relative dir under native-ESM AND a tsx -e import probe. Mechanical, no prod behavior change; route through plan mode per feedback_no_adhoc_dev. Consider a biome guard to prevent reintroduction. [CLOSED 2026-05-23 — commit dfa0fc6 / TASK-061 / VER-033: 3 shipped-src import.meta.dirname sites (update-check, index installProject, pi-workflows bundled-dirs EAGER) converted to path.dirname(fileURLToPath(import.meta.url)).]

gaps.title: Truncating read tools invite partial-read-as-complete — no machine-checkable completeness signal; >50KB content dropped with a prose-only note
gaps.description: Our read tools that JSON.stringify + truncateHead (read-block, read-config, read-samples-catalog, list-tools, join-blocks) cap output at DEFAULT_MAX_BYTES (50KB, from @earendil-works/pi-coding-agent core/tools/truncate.js) and append only a PROSE note '[Truncated: N bytes exceeds 50KB limit]' with NO continuation/pagination — content past 50KB is unrecoverable via that tool call. The note is a soft signal a satisficing LLM ignores → it treats the partial view as the whole (false-completeness; the exact failure DEC-0018 guards against). The pi builtin read tool shares the soft-signal weakness (offset-continuation is voluntary; truncateHead is head-down with a 2000-line/50KB dual cap) but at least offers offset-continuation; our tools offer none. Contrast: read-block-page (B3) returns {items,total,hasMore} — a STRUCTURED, machine-checkable completeness signal — which is the correct pattern, but nothing routes a truncated read-block to read-block-page. Surfaced 2026-05-22 reviewing pi's read truncation.

evidence.reference: DEFAULT_MAX_BYTES=50*1024, DEFAULT_MAX_LINES=2000; truncateHead head-down cap; builtin read offers offset-continuation note
read-block / read-config / read-samples-catalog / list-tools / join-blocks: truncateHead(JSON.stringify(...)) + prose '[Truncated: N bytes exceeds 50KB limit]' with NO continuation path
readBlockPage returns {items,total,hasMore} — the structured completeness signal the truncating reads lack

Observed live: on the 50KB list-tools truncation the agent treated the partial output as workable-complete ('work with what I have', 'count from what I have and acknowledge the truncation') and filled the dropped tail from a secondary source (its system prompt) rather than re-querying — the partial-as-complete / soft-prose-signal-ignored failure this gap describes, occurring on the discovery surface itself.

OBSERVED 2026-05-25 (live constrained agent): serializeForRead returned the over-cap read-samples-catalog body + footer [read-element: truncated at 95641 bytes . address a sub-element]; the agent ignored the footer and presented the partial catalog as complete -> degraded info. Proves the footer-in-text + returned-partial-body is a satisficeable bad route; the fix is fail-closed (no partial body on over-cap, directive-only).

gaps.impact: A harness-confined agent (DEC-0014) reading a >50KB block (framework-gaps ~346KB, decisions ~188KB, issues ~129KB) via read-block gets a silent partial + a prose note it may ignore, then acts on incomplete substrate believing it is whole. read-block-page exists but is not signposted from the truncation point, so the escape requires the agent to already know to switch. Under a growing substrate this compounds.
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: truncation completeness-signal, partial-read-as-complete, structured pagination signal
gaps.proposed_resolution: EMPIRICALLY CONFIRMED INSUFFICIENT (2026-05-25): the FGAP-103 structured footer is STILL a soft signal a satisficing agent skims past. Observed: a live constrained agent called read-samples-catalog with NO kind, got the over-cap result ending [read-element: truncated at 95641 bytes . address a sub-element], IGNORED it, and presented the partial catalog as COMPLETE (degraded, wrong info). Root: the PARTIAL BODY is still returned and usable, so a bad route exists. FIX (no-bad-route / fail-closed, user-directed): serializeForRead must PRE-DETECT over-cap (serialize + measure) and, when the full content would exceed the cap, return NO partial body — only a directive naming the SPECIFIC element-read tool + addressing for THAT surface: read-samples-catalog -> kind; read-config -> registry/id; read-block -> read-block-page (offset/limit) or read-block-item (id); read-schema -> path; list-tools -> name. e.g. "This catalog is 95641 bytes, exceeds the 50KB read limit and was NOT returned. Narrow: call read-samples-catalog with kind=<X>." The agent then CANNOT satisfice on partial data (there is none) — it must follow the directive. Each routed handler supplies its surface addressing hint. Edge case (atomic element, no finer addressing): explicit unmissable PARTIAL marker + byte counts, never silent partial. This solves the open "what happens after truncate" issue. Supersedes the structured-footer-in-text attempt (necessary but insufficient). DEC-0018 anti-false-pass.

gaps.title: In-pi agent receives no skill/guidance content — our extension does not wire pi's eager-injection hooks (before_agent_start / resources_discover)
gaps.description: Empirically (2026-05-22, live constrained AND unconstrained pi sessions) the in-pi agent reports NO skills in its context and operates the substrate from tool DESCRIPTIONS alone; bare on-disk SKILL.md (skill-narrative.md -> generate-skills -> SKILL.md) is not auto-surfaced, so the authored-guidance investment gives the agent no passive benefit. CAUSE: pi DOES expose eager guidance-injection to extensions; we simply wire none of it (our extension + scripts/launch-constrained-pi.sh). Shipped pi 0.75.4 channels: (1) before_agent_start handler returns { systemPrompt } (node_modules/@earendil-works/pi-coding-agent/dist/core/extensions/types.d.ts:469/475/738/796), applied per-turn and CHAINED across extensions (dist/core/agent-session.js:791-792; dist/core/extensions/runner.js:710-739) — an always-present eager system-prompt channel; (2) resources_discover returns { skillPaths } (types.d.ts:371/377/784) -> formatSkillsForPrompt emits eager <available_skills> metadata (dist/core/skills.js:258-277), bodies lazy via read. No appendSystemPrompt/registerSkill API — the hook return is the sole channel. (pi-mono stale 0.62.0; cite shipped dist.)

evidence.reference: skills are a separate axis (--no-skills); invoked explicitly via /skill (on-demand), not injected as passive agent context
hand-authored in-pi guidance → generate-skills → SKILL.md; the layer whose passive reach to the agent is in question
FGAP-085 (description discovery-readiness) becomes load-bearing if descriptions are confirmed as the sole in-pi guidance channel

gaps.impact: If skills are the intended richer guidance layer, the harness-confined agent never sees them — all in-pi comprehension rides on tool description quality (FGAP-085), and the skill-narrative authoring effort yields no passive agent benefit. If by-design, the project should stop treating skills as the agent's guidance and formally make descriptions the canonical in-pi channel. Either way the current unstated split misallocates guidance effort.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Wire pi's eager hooks, mirroring pi-core's own self-docs mechanism (pointer-eager / content-lazy; core's pointer block at dist/core/system-prompt.js:94-100): (1) before_agent_start injects an eager topic->tool-call pointer/orientation block; (2) resources_discover surfaces our framework skills as <available_skills>; (3) content stays lazy — fetched via our element-read tools (read-samples-catalog/read-config/read-schema + FGAP-103), since the tools-only agent has no `read`, so the conception/substrate IS our docs corpus. Skill-block source per FEAT-007. Verify against a live constrained-pi session (the agent's reported context).

gaps.title: No project-install tool — bootstrap tool-trilogy incomplete; a tools-only agent can't self-materialize a substrate from the catalog
gaps.description: Bootstrap is init -> accept-all -> install. project-init and project-accept-all are registered TOOLS (the harness-confined agent can call them), but install exists ONLY as the /project install slash command — there is no project-install tool. So a tools-only agent (DEC-0014) can create the skeleton + write config.json, but CANNOT materialize the declared schemas + starter blocks from the samples catalog. The init+accept-all-have-tools-but-install-doesn't asymmetry is the decisive point: the trilogy is inconsistent. The write-schema workaround is INFEASIBLE for a tools-only agent: read-samples-catalog returns only SHAPE (arrayKeys + itemProperties), NOT the raw draft-07 schema bodies (verified: SampleKind has shape, no schema body), so the agent has nothing to pass to write-schema; and a fully-constrained --tools session has no builtin read tool to source the package's .schema.json files either. Surfaced 2026-05-22 by a live in-pi agent tracing its own bootstrap pipeline. (Refines an earlier mis-call that 'install-as-tool' was marginal — it is a real gap once init+accept-all are already tools.)

evidence.reference: project-init + project-accept-all registered as tools; NO project-install tool (grep count 0); /project install is a command-only handler
samplesCatalog SampleKind = {…, shape:{arrayKeys,itemProperties}, …} — no raw schema body; so write-schema has no source for the body via the catalog
installProject (the copy-from-samples logic, post FGAP-067) exists as a function but is only invoked by the /project install command — a tool binding would expose it to the agent

gaps.impact: A harness-confined agent cannot complete a fresh-substrate bootstrap through tools — it gets a config.json (accept-all) but no schemas/blocks on disk, so subsequent block writes proceed UNVALIDATED (schema-absent path) and the substrate is degraded. The self-bootstrap scenario (e.g. a constrained session in a fresh dir like may-22-2026) is blocked at step 3 of 3. Inconsistent with init/accept-all being agent-callable.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Add a project-install Pi tool (and orchestrator CLI per DEC-0019/0020) wrapping the existing installProject library fn — completing the init/accept-all/install trilogy on the tool surface. This is the root fix; it makes the write-schema workaround + the read-samples-catalog-lacks-bodies observation moot (install copies the bodies from the package; the agent never needs them in-context). Do NOT pursue the reconstruct-schemas-from-shape workaround (lossy/error-prone) as the resolution.

gaps.title: In-pi agent confabulates substrate vocabulary/signatures instead of grounding in the discovery tools — no anti-confabulation directive in the agent-reachable layer
gaps.description: [FOLDED INTO FGAP-085 per user direction 2026-05-22 — grounding-directive content + write-time fail-loud facet now live in FGAP-085.] Live in-pi agents (2026-05-22) repeatedly ASSERTED substrate specifics from assumption rather than DERIVING them from the queryable surface: (a) used relation_type `phase_depends_on` which is NOT registered in config.relation_types (verified empty) — a confabulated edge type; (b) proposed reconstructing draft-07 schemas from read-samples-catalog SHAPE metadata (it returns arrayKeys/itemProperties, not bodies) instead of knowing install is the materialization path; (c) assumed builtin filesystem read of the npm package (a constrained --tools session has no read builtin); (d) guessed tool signatures (append-block-item arrayKey param, an invented roadmap.json shape) instead of consulting list-tools. The unifying ignorance: the agent does not know that the VOCABULARY (relation_types/block_kinds/lenses via read-config + read-samples-catalog) and its OWN tool signatures (via list-tools) are queryable, so it should ground before asserting and never invent canonical names or schema bodies. The surface compounds it: append-relation DEFERS relation_type-registration validation (FGAP-075), so a confabulated edge writes silently and only fails at project-validate — no write-time correction. This is the CONTENT gap (what the agent must be told), distinct from FGAP-085 (description quality = the channel) and FGAP-090 (skills don't reach the agent = the channel reach). Because skills are unreachable, the cure must live in the always-present layer (tool descriptions + a foundational orientation).

evidence.reference: relation_types has no `phase_depends_on` (verified empty for phase*) — the agent invented it
read-samples-catalog returns shape (arrayKeys/itemProperties), NOT raw schema bodies — yet the agent proposed reconstructing schemas from it

read-config / read-samples-catalog / list-tools expose the vocabulary + signatures the agent should ground in but didn't; append-relation defers relation_type validation (no write-time confabulation check)

gaps.impact: A harness-confined agent silently produces invalid substrate (unregistered-relation_type edges, mis-shaped items, reconstructed-wrong schemas) because nothing in its reachable context tells it to ground in the discovery tools or forbids inventing canonical names. Deferred validation means the error surfaces late (project-validate) or never (if validate isn't run). This undermines the DEC-0014 aim: a tools-only agent that operates the substrate CORRECTLY, not just plausibly.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Encode an explicit grounding directive in the always-present layer (tool descriptions per FGAP-085, and/or a foundational in-pi orientation surfaced regardless of skills per FGAP-090): (1) vocabulary is queryable — call read-config / read-samples-catalog before referencing any relation_type/block_kind/lens; never invent canonical names; (2) tool signatures are queryable via list-tools — consult, don't guess; (3) bootstrap/materialization is init -> accept-all -> install (FGAP-091), not schema reconstruction. Consider tightening append-relation/amend-config to fail loud on unregistered names at write (reversing the FGAP-075/076 deferral where layering permits) so confabulation can't write silently. Sequence with FGAP-085 (descriptions) + FGAP-090 (skill-reach decision).

gaps.title: /project install 'no config.json' error misdirects to /project init — should name /project accept-all (stale post init/accept-all split)
gaps.description: After the consumption-MVP split (init scaffolds dirs only; accept-all writes config.json from the conception; install copies assets), installProject's no-config error (index.ts:314) still reads 'No config.json found in substrate dir — run /project init first.' But init does NOT write config.json — accept-all does. A user running the natural-looking init -> install (skipping accept-all) is told to re-run init, which can't resolve it; the actual remedy (accept-all) is never named. Observed live 2026-05-22: '/project init .context' (Created .context/ + .context/schemas/) then '/project install' -> 'No config.json found in substrate dir — run /project init first.' The message names the wrong command.

evidence.reference: installProject no-config error: 'run /project init first' — but init (post-split) writes no config.json; accept-all does
init = bootstrap pointer + mkdir dirs only (FGAP-067); project-accept-all writes config.json — the split that made the message stale

gaps.impact: A fresh adopter following the apparent init -> install path hits a dead-end whose stated remedy (re-run init) cannot fix it; the resolving command (accept-all) is unnamed. UX trap at the most common first-use path; erodes the 'self-describing surface' aim.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Change the index.ts:314 message to name the resolving command: 'No config.json in the substrate dir — run /project accept-all to adopt the conception (or author config.json), then /project install.' One-line source fix (plan mode per feedback_no_adhoc_dev). [SUBSUMED-ON-LANDING by FGAP-095/DEC-0042: /context start makes install a non-user-verb and collapses the remedy to a single "run /context start"; the misdirect cannot occur once start lands. Bug remains live until then — interim one-line message fix optional.]

gaps.title: Ship NO concrete substrate-dir default — samples/conception.json smuggles a hidden root: '.project'; contradicts DEC-0015 (no default dir) + DEC-0011 (ship-no-defaults) + perpetuates legacy 'project' naming
gaps.description: DEC-0015 mandates NO default substrate dir (/project init requires an explicit dir name; resolveContextDir hard-throws BootstrapNotFoundError on absent pointer — no fallback). But samples/conception.json — the packaged conception (DEC-0037) a fresh adopter consumes via accept-all — ships root: '.project'. That is a HIDDEN, shipped substrate-dir default. accept-all's adoptConception overrides it to the user's init'd dir (project-context.ts), so it is operationally masked and NOT load-bearing in the normal init -> accept-all -> install flow; but the SHIPPED artifact still imposes '.project': any consumer reading conception.root directly, or any code path that bypasses the override, inherits the legacy default. Triply wrong: (1) a default substrate dir at all (DEC-0015 says none); (2) shipping a default value (DEC-0011 ship-no-defaults); (3) the legacy 'project' name in the going-forward .context-era artifact (the FGAP-074 project-residual cluster — which explicitly SCOPED OUT the substrate-dir-name as 'a separate concern', i.e. THIS gap). Worst because it is hidden inside a config field, not an obvious default. Surfaced 2026-05-22 reviewing install substrate-dir resolution; user directive: 'we should not be shipping anything project as default let alone when it is hidden'.

evidence.reference: ships root: '.project' — a hidden shipped substrate-dir default (the conception is the packaged template a fresh adopter accepts)
adoptConception overrides conception.root to the actual contextDir — masks the shipped '.project' but does not remove it from the artifact
resolveContextDir hard-throws (no .project fallback) per DEC-0015; PROJECT_DIR='.project' @deprecated constant — the legacy literal

gaps.impact: The shipped conception imposes/leaks the legacy '.project' default; correctness relies on accept-all's override to mask it, which is fragile — a consumer reading the conception directly, or a future path skipping the override, silently gets '.project'. Perpetuates 'project' naming in the .context-era (DEC-0029) artifact and contradicts the no-default-dir invariant the framework otherwise enforces by hard-throw. This is the organizing instance of the residual-'project' cluster's substrate-dir-name concern (paired with FGAP-074 source/surface naming).
gaps.proposed_resolution: [RESOLVED APPROACH 2026-05-23, DEC-0041] Make config.root an OPTIONAL schema field (drop from required[], v1.3.0->1.4.0) and drop root from the packaged conception; the .pi-context.json pointer is the substrate-dir SoT and projectRoot already falls back to it when root is absent. samplesCatalog stops surfacing root. Shipped default-.project doc claims (config.schema desc, skill-narrative:16, README:24) corrected; SKILL.md regenerated. Frozen .project/schemas/ copy untouched (DEC-0036; loadConfig uses the bundled schema). Rejected: a separate conception.schema.json (heavier, no functional gain — config.root already redundant with the pointer). FGAP-074 location/naming cutover refs left untouched. [CLOSED 2026-05-23 — commit ce87262, TASK-058/VER-030. conception ships no root; config.root optional + v1.4.0; catalog drops root; default-claim docs corrected; adoptConception comments de-staled. DEC-0041 (open) records the contract change, awaiting user enactment.]

gaps.title: Build /context start — the impossibilized single-entry onboarding conductor (state-machine; READY-gated twinned operational verbs; init/accept-all/install dissolved)
gaps.description:
GAP: there is no single safe entry to substrate bootstrap; the 3-step init->accept-all->install DAG is user-sequenced and out-of-order is representable (FGAP-093). DEC-0042 settles the design: /context start is the sole human entry; bootstrap is human-only; all other verbs are twinned + READY-gated; init/accept-all/install dissolve into start. This FGAP is the implementation spec.

ARCHITECTURE.
Bootstrap state is a pure function of the filesystem (DEC-0040 — nothing stored): deriveBootstrapState(cwd) -> NO_POINTER (no .pi-context.json) | NO_CONFIG (pointer present, no config.json) | NOT_INSTALLED (config present, declared installed_schemas[]/installed_blocks[] not all materialized on disk) | READY (all present). 

/context start = state machine with FALL-THROUGH, human-only command, NO tool twin, precondition-free, never errors:
  NO_POINTER -> elicit substrate dir name (suggest .context; NEVER default — DEC-0015) -> writeBootstrapPointer + mkdir dir + dir/schemas -> fallthrough
  NO_CONFIG -> elicit accept-all | step-through (DEC-0038 fork) -> accept-all: adoptConception(cwd); step-through: conversational curation (FGAP-066, LLM-via-tools pi.sendMessage pattern) -> fallthrough
  NOT_INSTALLED -> installProject(cwd) (idempotent, never-clobber) -> fallthrough
  READY -> render the /context status view (thin pass-through; status owns the read home — no duplicate home)
Re-running start from any state resumes; READY re-run just shows status.

GATING. Every /context subcommand EXCEPT start checks deriveBootstrapState(cwd)===READY at the DISPATCH layer before invoking its handler. Unmet -> do not run; emit the single unified remedy 'substrate not set up — run /context start' (+ ctx.ui.setEditorText('/context start') prefill). start is the only precondition-free verb. Gate is STATE-based, not a 'did you run start' flag.

DISSOLUTION. init/accept-all/install lose their standalone command forms AND their tool forms; remove the project-init and project-accept-all Pi tools from the registry. The library fns initProject / adoptConception / installProject REMAIN, called only as start's internal phases.

TWINNING. start is the ONE human-only exception. status/view/validate/validate-relations/roadmap-* + the block CRUD tools stay twinned (command+tool) per DEC-0019/0020, all READY-gated.

UNIFIED REMEDY. BootstrapNotFoundError message + the command-gate redirect both read 'run /context start'; tool throws carry the guidance so the harness-confined LLM (DEC-0014) relays the correct HUMAN action (the LLM cannot bootstrap — intentional per DEC-0042).

STARTUP SLOT. In the existing session_start handler (index.ts:459), gate on event.reason==='startup' && ctx.hasUI && deriveBootstrapState(ctx.cwd)!==READY -> ctx.ui.setWidget(key, ['pi-context installed — run /context start to begin'], {placement:'aboveEditor'}) + optional setEditorText; clear the widget when READY. Per analysis/2026-05-23-pi-tui-onboarding-banner-research.md (pi's own update-banner slot is NOT extension-addressable; setWidget is the closest persistent extension-owned slot; surfaces are text-only).

evidence.reference: PROJECT_SUBCOMMANDS dispatch — where the uniform READY gate + the start verb are added; where init/accept-all/install command forms are removed
session_start handler — where the startup-slot setWidget hint is added (today only checkForUpdates->notify)
adoptConception — becomes a start internal phase (no longer a standalone tool)
resolveContextDir / BootstrapNotFoundError — carry the unified 'run /context start' remedy in the throw
startup-slot SDK mechanism + the not-extension-addressable update-banner limitation

gaps.impact: Closes the out-of-order class entirely (subsumes FGAP-093); gives a fresh adopter one verb reachable from any state; aligns the human command surface with DEC-0042/0038; makes the in-pi LLM's unset-substrate behavior a single correct human-redirect. Without it, onboarding stays a memorized multi-step sequence with misdirecting errors.
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: impossibilization-by-design, bootstrap state machine, READY-gate, dual-surface twinning, human-only entry
gaps.proposed_resolution:
Phased, each phase dual-surface where applicable (DEC-0019/0020) + runtime demo + fresh-context adversarial (DEC-0018) + tests.
P1 deriveBootstrapState(cwd): pure fn returning the 4-state enum from pointer/config/asset-materialization signals; library + unit tests (all four states + idempotent re-derivation).
P2 Dispatch READY-gate: in the subcommand dispatcher, every sub except start gates on deriveBootstrapState===READY else unified redirect + setEditorText; start precondition-free. Tests: each gated sub on a non-READY dir redirects; on READY runs.
P3 /context start conductor: fall-through state machine (NO_POINTER dir-name elicit -> pointer+dirs; NO_CONFIG adopt-mode elicit -> adoptConception | step-through-stub; NOT_INSTALLED -> installProject; READY -> render status). Human-only command, NO tool twin. Elicitation via the pi.sendMessage follow-up-turn pattern (lens-curate precedent). Runtime demo: fresh dir -> start -> answer -> READY end-to-end (accept-all branch).
P4 Dissolve init/accept-all/install: remove their command forms; remove project-init + project-accept-all tools from the registry; keep library fns as start internals. Adversarial: grep that no command/tool path reaches them except via start.
P5 Unified remedy: BootstrapNotFoundError + command gate text = 'run /context start'; tool throws carry it. Test: a tool on an unset dir throws with the start guidance.
P6 Startup slot: session_start setWidget hint gated to not-READY + reason===startup + hasUI; cleared at READY. Demo via the SDK surfaces in the analysis report.
P7 step-through branch = FGAP-066 (honest stub in P3: offered, lands with FGAP-066; never silently substitute accept-all). On landing, close FGAP-093 (subsumed). 
SEQUENCING: assumes the /context surface (FGAP-074 / DEC-0036 step-4 cutover). Buildable on /project first then renamed, OR after cutover — user's call. The whole spec is the FGAP-066 vehicle's superstructure (start), with FGAP-066 supplying the step-through interior. [INSTRUCTIONAL-PROJECTION REQUIREMENT 2026-05-23, user-directed] The READY/status view + start conductor + startup slot must deliver INSTRUCTIONS, not bare counts: every derived-state line pairs the fact with its exact write AFFORDANCE (the tool/command to act). E.g. not-installed -> handled by start (run /context start); ready + 0 edges -> "0 edges — add via the append-relation tool"; ready + empty block kind -> "<kind>: 0 items — add via the append/file-block-item tool". This is the chosen alternative to pre-seeding empty files (pre-seeding advertises hand-editing forbidden by F-006, imposes content against DEC-0011, clutters the dir so it no longer reflects real work, and freezes a stale schema_version on never-written blocks). The derived view delivers the same discoverability with zero materialized files. Cross-cuts: status render (project-sdk + /context status handler), start READY branch (renders status), and the startup slot. Intersects FGAP-085 — the human-facing "add via X" affordance strings and the in-pi LLM tool-description discovery are two faces of one self-describing surface; design them together.

gaps.title: Cross-package src consumers catch BootstrapNotFoundError via instanceof — same FGAP-080 module-instance pattern, outside the orchestrator-CLI scope
gaps.description: FGAP-080 (closed) converted ORCHESTRATOR-CLI catches of pi-context error classes to name-based, because instanceof across the pi-context package boundary is module-instance-fragile under tsx (a class reached via a subpath import vs a transitive relative import resolves to two different objects). Surfaced during Unit B (FGAP-080) by the fresh-context adversarial probe: the SAME pattern lives in cross-package SRC consumers outside FGAP-080's filed scope — pi-jit-agents/src/compile.ts:242,263 and pi-workflows/src/step-shared.ts:181 catch BootstrapNotFoundError via `instanceof`. These are RUNTIME-SAFE today (pi loads pi-jit-agents/pi-workflows as built dist via node = a single module instance; their tests import fresh under tsx --test = single instance) — so they are NOT in the demonstrated-fragile tsx-CJS-interop path the orchestrator CLIs hit. But the FGAP-080 PRINCIPLE (name-based across the pi-context package boundary) applies, and a future tsx-load or refactor of the throw path could break them silently.

evidence.reference: if (err instanceof BootstrapNotFoundError) — cross-package (imports BootstrapNotFoundError from @davidorex/pi-context/project-dir)
if (!(err instanceof BootstrapNotFoundError)) throw err — cross-package
precedent name-based fix (err.name === 'BootstrapNotFoundError')

gaps.impact: Low/latent: no live failure (runtime path is single-instance), but the FGAP-080 pattern is not fully eliminated — leaving 3 principle-violating sites that could regress to silent-miss if their load/throw path changes. Closing FGAP-080 for orchestrator scope while these remain is the residual.
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: name-based error identity across package boundary, module-instance fragility
gaps.proposed_resolution: Convert the 3 cross-package src-consumer catches to name-based (err instanceof Error && err.name === 'BootstrapNotFoundError'), matching the FGAP-080/077 precedent; drop the now-unused BootstrapNotFoundError imports. Trivial + uniform. Optionally consolidate via a canonical isBootstrapNotFoundError(err) predicate exported from pi-context (would also DRY the orchestrator CLIs + write-schema/accept-all). Batch with a future hygiene pass or Unit C. [CLOSED 2026-05-23 — commit 4fb4d1e / TASK-062 / VER-034: compile.ts:242,263 + step-shared.ts:181 instanceof BootstrapNotFoundError -> name-based; unused imports dropped.]

gaps.title: In-pi substrate-read tools should emit an onboarding-actionable message pre-bootstrap instead of a raw BootstrapNotFoundError
gaps.description: Block-level read tools that an in-pi LLM may invoke before a substrate is set up (read-block + named-block reads generally) surface the raw resolveContextDir BootstrapNotFoundError when no .pi-context.json pointer exists. The error message IS already actionable (it names 'run /context init'), but it is a thrown error rather than a structured, onboarding-consistent tool response. A friendlier emit ('no substrate yet — run /context start') paired with the write affordance would align with the DEC-0042/FGAP-095 instructional-projection requirement (pair every fact/absence with its remedy). This is a UX/error-shaping improvement, NOT a correctness gap: read-block/readBlock are throw-on-missing by contract, so throwing pointer-less is correct behavior (confirmed during the tryResolveContextDir class fix, TASK-069); degrade-to-empty would mislead and is explicitly NOT wanted here. Relates to FGAP-095 (onboarding conductor) + FGAP-085 (tool description discovery-readiness).

evidence.reference: read-block tool calls readBlock(ctx.cwd, params.block) directly; pointer-less it throws BootstrapNotFoundError (via blockFilePath->resolveContextDir) rather than returning an onboarding-shaped tool result
readBlockDir now degrades to [] pointer-less (tryResolveContextDir) — a directory-enumeration read; the named-block read tools intentionally still throw per readBlock's throw-on-missing contract

gaps.impact: Minor in-pi onboarding friction: an LLM calling a read tool in a fresh repo gets a thrown error (actionable text, but not a structured/consistent onboarding affordance). No correctness or data-integrity impact.
gaps.proposed_resolution: When a substrate-read tool handler hits a missing-pointer condition, catch BootstrapNotFoundError (name-based, per FGAP-080) at the TOOL boundary (not in readBlock itself — its throw contract stays) and return a structured tool result naming the remedy ('run /context start'), consistent with the FGAP-095 instructional-projection pattern. Scope to the in-pi tool handlers; orchestrator CLIs keep throwing (misuse should fail loudly). Sequence with / fold into FGAP-095 onboarding work.

gaps.title: No FGAP<->FGAP cross-reference affordance — framework-gaps schema lacks related_gaps; per DEC-0013 the fix is a gap_relates_to_gap relation_type + edges, not an FK field
gaps.description: The framework-gaps item schema exposes related_features, related_decisions, and related_issues (inline-FK arrays, legacy .project style) but NO field for linking one FGAP to a sibling FGAP. Filing FGAPs that reference other FGAPs (common — clusters, follow-ups, supersedes) has no structured home; such cross-refs currently live only in prose. Encountered concretely while filing FGAP-097 (a related_gaps key was rejected by AJV additionalProperties:false). Per DEC-0013 (closure-table is THE primitive for ALL inter-item relationships; FK-as-field forbidden) the correct resolution is NOT to add a related_gaps FK array (that would extend the legacy inline-FK pattern the rebuild is removing) but to register a gap_relates_to_gap relation_type in the conception (samples/conception.json) and express links as relations.json edges — mirroring the decision_relates_to_decision precedent added in Unit E (TASK-064). The existing related_features/related_decisions/related_issues inline-FK fields on the frozen .project schemas are themselves DEC-0013-violations slated to become edges in the .context re-derivation; related_gaps should be born as an edge, never added as a field.

evidence.reference: item schema lists related_features/related_decisions/related_issues but no related_gaps; additionalProperties:false rejects a related_gaps key (observed filing FGAP-097)
relation_types[] — add gap_relates_to_gap (mirror decision_relates_to_decision, Unit E): category data_flow, source_kinds/target_kinds [framework-gaps]

gaps.impact: FGAP<->FGAP relationships are unstructured (prose-only), unqueryable, and not walkable by the closure-table lens primitives. Low severity (P3) — cross-refs still expressible in description text — but it is a consistency gap in the relation vocabulary and a DEC-0013 cleanliness item for the .context catalog.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Add a gap_relates_to_gap relation_type to samples/conception.json relation_types[] (category data_flow; source_kinds/target_kinds [framework-gaps]; mirror decision_relates_to_decision). Do NOT add a related_gaps FK field to the framework-gaps schema (DEC-0013). FGAP<->FGAP links become relations.json edges. Backfill prose-only cross-refs (incl. FGAP-097->FGAP-095/085) as edges after the .context cutover, alongside the DEC-0045 backfill (TASK-065).

gaps.title: Workflow agent-step dispatch does not clamp child tool surface to the parent — spec omission defaults to full privilege (harness sandbox is per-process, not transitive)
gaps.description: The workflow agent-step dispatch path imposes no relationship between a spawned child agent's tool surface and the dispatching parent's. buildArgs derives the child's tools solely from the agent spec: spec tools are split into builtins (-> --tools) and extension paths (-> --extension), with no read of the parent's active tool set. When the spec omits `tools`, NO --tools flag is passed and the child runs pi's default surface (bash/read/write/edit builtins + all loaded extensions). A main agent launched under a restrictive --tools allowlist (e.g. scripts/launch-constrained-pi.sh, the DEC-0014 harness-confined model) is therefore NOT a containing boundary: by invoking workflow-execute on an on-disk workflow whose agent step references a broadly-privileged or tools-unspecified spec, it spawns a fully-capable child (bash + file read/write). The unsafe direction is the default — restriction is opt-in per spec; omission yields maximal privilege.

evidence.reference: Tool filtering applied ONLY `if (agentSpec.tools?.length)`. Spec tools split into builtinTools -> `--tools <list>` and extensionPaths -> `--extension`. Code never reads the parent process's active tool set; no intersection/subset check.

buildArgs is the sole argv builder; reads only step + agentSpec. Absent agentSpec.tools, no --tools flag is emitted -> child inherits pi's full default surface (builtins + all extensions). Least-privilege is opt-in; omission = maximal privilege.

spawn("pi", args, { cwd, env: process.env }) — a fresh independent pi process inheriting the full parent environment. The parent's launch-time --tools restriction is a per-process flag and does not propagate to the child.

workflow-execute resolves the workflow by name via findWorkflow(params.workflow, cwd) — on-disk specs only. The constrained surface has no spec-authoring (file-write) tool, so reachability of a privileged child is bounded to specs already on disk; the escalation primitive itself is unconditional.

gaps.impact: The tools-only harness sandbox is per-process, not transitive across dispatch. Confinement of the main agent (DEC-0014) does not confine what it can spawn: child capability is not required to be a subset of parent capability, and no human ratification gates the spawn. This is the privilege-escalation hole underneath the agents-as-tools arc (FEAT-004) and the capability-mutation DEC-0047 proposes to govern. Before agent-dispatch becomes a first-class tool, the dispatch boundary must clamp child tools to a subset of the parent's (or an explicit ratified grant) and default to least-privilege on spec omission. Reachability today is bounded by which on-disk workflow + agent specs exist; the escalation primitive is not.
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: privilege-escalation, sandbox-escape, capability-confinement, transitive-least-privilege, ambient-authority, fail-closed-default
gaps.proposed_resolution: At the dispatch boundary (buildArgs/dispatch in pi-workflows — the canonical cross-cutting locus): (1) thread the dispatching context's effective tool set into DispatchOptions; (2) intersect agentSpec.tools with it and fail-closed on any spec tool outside the parent set; (3) on agentSpec.tools omission, default to a least-privilege surface (empty or an explicit safe-read set) rather than pi's full default; (4) gate any capability-broadening beyond the parent set behind the DEC-0047 human-ratification path. Pairs with FEAT-005 (JIT capability composition + sandbox). Requires settling the parent-surface source of truth (launch --tools vs a declared capability manifest) alongside DEC-0044 (agent-dispatch home).

gaps.title: Canonical credentialed verification protocol is stale post-FGAP-074 — needs a successor before the next arc-completion release
gaps.description: docs/reports/pi-internal-verification-protocol-2026-05-02.md is the canonical step-12 (CLAUDE.md completion-sequence) credentialed pre-publish verification gate for arc-completion releases. It was authored for the v0.24.x substrate and cannot be executed verbatim against the current surface: (1) it invokes removed project-* tool names (project-status/project-validate/project-init) renamed to context-* by FGAP-074; (2) it dispatches via a removed bare `workflow` tool — the current dispatch tool is `workflow-execute`; (3) it gates credentialed sections on OPENROUTER_API_KEY, contradicting CLAUDE.md step-12's directive that the protocol uses pi's auth.json directly (no separate env-var gate); (4) it assumes `gtimeout` is on PATH for per-call timeouts (absent on the current machine — neither gtimeout nor timeout present); (5) it asserts on old substrate fixtures (DEC-0001..0005 exact set, REVIEW-001/spec-reviews findings counts) that the pi-context-rebuild re-derivation has moved past. Per the protocol's own 'or successor' clause, a successor is now required. For the v0.26.0 release a SCOPED credentialed smoke was run in lieu (read/derive/validate + write->read->remove round-trip through the renamed context-* and block tools, with real tool_execution events asserted and byte-identical baseline restore verified); the deferred portion is the full agent contextBlocks injection + credentialed agent-dispatch tier.

evidence.reference: Invokes project-status / project-validate / project-init — removed by FGAP-074 (now context-status/context-validate; init folded toward /context start per DEC-0042).
Dispatches agents via 'call the workflow tool with workflow X' — no bare `workflow` tool exists on the current surface; the dispatch tool is `workflow-execute`.
Gates credentialed sections on OPENROUTER_API_KEY env var; CLAUDE.md step-12 specifies pi's auth.json directly (no env-var gate). Credential source observed at ~/.pi/agent/auth.json.
Every dispatch wrapped in `gtimeout NN pi -p ...`; gtimeout (and GNU timeout) are not installed on the current machine, so the commands fail with exit 127 as written.
Required-state assumptions assert a specific old substrate (DEC-0001..DEC-0005 as the full set; REVIEW-001 findings count) superseded by the pi-context-rebuild re-derivation.

gaps.impact: The mandated step-12 pre-publish credentialed gate cannot be run verbatim for arc-completion releases, so each such release must improvise an ad-hoc scoped smoke (as v0.26.0 did) instead of executing a maintained, comprehensive, repeatable protocol. Without a successor, credentialed verification coverage silently narrows release over release, and the deferred agent-dispatch/contextBlocks injection tier (the deepest end-to-end credentialed path) goes unexercised at release time.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Author a successor protocol (e.g. docs/reports/pi-internal-verification-protocol-<successor-date>.md) that: uses context-* tool names + workflow-execute; sources credentials from pi's auth.json (no OPENROUTER_API_KEY gate); drops the gtimeout dependency (rely on the harness/Bash timeout); asserts against current .project substrate (or a disposable temp substrate it bootstraps via /context start); and includes the full agent contextBlocks injection + credentialed agent-dispatch tier deferred from the v0.26.0 scoped smoke — authoring the required fixtures under .project/agents/*.agent.yaml + .workflows/*.workflow.yaml (NEVER .pi/, per the no-touch rule; agent loader project tier is .project/agents/, workflow discovery is .workflows/), dispatching credentialed via workflow-execute, and asserting the <context_block role="data"> XML boundary + injected item content reach the model. Pin a fast model + restrict --tools per call (F-006 cost discipline). Use the v0.26.0 scoped-smoke transcript (docs/reports/v0.26.0-step12-scoped-credentialed-smoke-2026-05-25.md) as the tier-1 starting point.

gaps.title: list-tools is a single-shot full-schema dump — no progressive disclosure (names/index -> per-tool detail); the ~54-tool payload exceeds the 50KB cap and truncates, blinding the agent's own discovery surface
gaps.description: list-tools (pi-context/src/index.ts:997) returns one JSON blob of { tools:[{name,description,parameters,sourceInfo}], active, total, activeCount } for ALL loaded tools (~54), each carrying its FULL parameters schema, then truncateHead-caps at 50KB (index.ts:1016) appending only the prose note '[Truncated: N bytes exceeds 50KB limit.]' (index.ts:1019; observed ~58KB -> truncated). Because list-tools is the harness-confined agent's PRIMARY discovery mechanism (DEC-0014 / FGAP-044), truncating it is uniquely perverse: the one tool whose job is to reveal the surface silently drops the tail of that surface, and a satisficing LLM treats the partial catalog as complete (the FGAP-089 false-completeness failure, applied to discovery itself). There is no two-level disclosure: no compact INDEX mode (tool name + one-line summary, fits under cap) and no DETAIL mode (full parameters for a named tool / subset). This is the same progressive-disclosure pattern FGAP-045 established for blocks (read-block all-or-nothing -> read-block-item + read-block-page {items,total,hasMore}), un-applied to the tool catalog. Surfaced 2026-05-25 by the user observing the truncation in a constrained-harness session.

evidence.reference: list-tools registration; builds {tools:[{name,description,parameters,sourceInfo}],...} over getAllTools()/getActiveTools(), JSON.stringify + truncateHead at :1016, prose-only '[Truncated: N bytes exceeds 50KB limit.]' at :1019. Single mode; full parameters per tool; no index/detail split.

read-block-page is the established progressive-disclosure precedent ({items,total,hasMore}, offset/limit) for blocks — the shape list-tools should mirror for the tool catalog (index page + per-tool detail).

Observed live in a constrained-harness session: list-tools truncated at 50KB (~58KB payload); the agent satisficed on the partial view and explicitly reached for a read-block-page-style index->detail for the tool catalog ('let me try read-block-page approach... no, that's for substrate blocks') before finding it absent — direct confirmation of the missing progressive disclosure this gap describes.

gaps.impact: The agent's primary (and, per FGAP-090, near-sole) discovery channel is also the payload most likely to exceed 50KB, so discovery is silently incomplete: tools past the cap are invisible to the in-pi LLM, which then hand-waves or confabulates signatures (the FGAP-092 confabulation pattern). Compounds FGAP-085 (even well-authored descriptions are dropped past the cap) and instances FGAP-089 (prose-only truncation signal). As the tool count grows, the truncation worsens.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Make list-tools two-level (progressive disclosure), mirroring read-block-page: (1) default INDEX mode returns a compact entry per tool (name + one-line summary + param-count + sourceInfo + active flag) for ALL tools — small enough to never truncate; (2) a DETAIL mode (param e.g. { tool: '<name>' } or { names: [...] }) returns the full parameters schema for the requested tool(s). Pair with FGAP-089 (emit a structured completeness signal, not a prose note) and FGAP-085 (the index surfaces descriptions, so description quality becomes load-bearing). Keep Pi-tool-only per FGAP-044's deliberate DEC-0019/0020 deviation (runtime introspection lives only in a loaded pi session; the orchestrator is served by grep/skills). Implementation routes through plan mode (source edit to index.ts).

gaps.title: Autonomous code-change loop validates via LLM-self-report, not real deterministic checks — DEC-0018 not inherited; no build/check/test gate, runtime-demo, adversarial-against-artifacts, or attested commit
gaps.description: self-implement.workflow.yaml (the shipped agent code-change loop) verifies via an LLM `verifier` agent emitting a prose status, gated by `echo '${{ steps.verify.output.status }}' | grep -q passed` (lines 42-54). The verdict is an LLM grading an LLM's work against prose criteria — the SAME KIND of process as the thing it checks, with no real deterministic check. Verified: NO bundled workflow runs npm run check / npm test / tsc, and NONE commits. The privileged spec-implementer agent (tools:[read,write,edit,bash,grep,find]) therefore writes code that is never compiler/test-verified, never runtime-demonstrated, and never landed. This relocates the DEC-0018 false-pass (tests-pass necessary-not-sufficient; LLMs perform) into the VERIFY position — the most dangerous place for it — so the agent grades its own work instead of a real check grading it (DEC-0047: the terminal verdict must be a real check the executive cannot fake). The capability the Claude-Code orchestrator applies every step (run the real toolchain and read its exit code; runtime-demo the actual path; adversarially re-check EXTERNAL artifacts like the git diff / event stream) is absent from the autonomous loop. The engine already HAS the real-check primitive — command/gate steps spawn sh (step-command.ts:28, step-gate.ts:24) — the loop simply does not use it for validation.

evidence.reference: verify step = LLM `verifier` agent emitting output.status; check gate = `echo '${{ steps.verify.output.status }}' | grep -q passed`. Terminal acceptance keys on an LLM's prose verdict, not an real-check exit code.

Verified zero matches for 'npm run check' / 'npm test' / 'npm run build' / 'tsc' / 'biome' and zero 'git commit' across all *.workflow.yaml — no bundled workflow runs a real build/test gate or commits.
Privileged code-writer: tools:[read,write,edit,bash,grep,find]. Its edits are accepted on the LLM-verifier verdict, never compiler/test-verified.
command step spawn('sh',['-c',command]) — the real-check primitive that exists but is unused for code-change validation (same for step-gate.ts:24).

gaps.impact: The 'validated' in 'validated code changes' is fake: an autonomous (or constrained-agent-dispatched) self-implement run can land — or claim to land — broken code on an LLM's say-so. This is the load-bearing blocker for the north-star loop (FEAT-006): a constrained orchestrator dispatching a privileged code agent without a real-check gate produces unvalidated changes under a validation-shaped wrapper.
