The fix is low-mechanism (the command/gate real-check primitive already ships) but load-bearing for trust.
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: real-check-validation, validator-independence, runtime-verification-gate, exit-code-gating, real-check-backed-acceptance, false-pass
gaps.proposed_resolution: Port DEC-0018 into the loop as an executable real-check acceptance tail: (1) replace the LLM-verifier terminal gate with a command step running the real toolchain (npm run build && npm run check && npm test) gated on EXIT CODE, full output / no pipe-masking per the project's own rule; (2) a runtime-demonstration step that executes the actual feature path and gates on emitted facts (events/diffs/exit codes), not a description; (3) an adversarial step that checks EXTERNAL artifacts (git diff, a re-run), not re-grades prose; (4) accept the implementer's edits ONLY when the real checks pass; on fail feed the REAL compiler/test errors back for another implement pass (fix root cause, never bypass); (5) commit through husky (itself the external check) with DispatchContext agent-authorship attestation. ENFORCEMENT INVARIANT: a workflow-validate check (+ config policy) forbidding an LLM-verdict as the TERMINAL acceptance of a code-change workflow — the terminal verdict must key on a real external-command exit code; an LLM verifier may triage/route but may not be the terminal gate. Refines DEC-0047 (the terminal verdict is a real check the executive cannot fake) and realizes DEC-0018 in autonomous loops.

gaps.title: No universal, DRY element-level read across JSON surfaces — config/schema/relations/roadmap/block-dir are read-all-or-nothing; element-read exists only for blocks and is bespoke, not a shared primitive
gaps.description: Every JSON-backed read surface the in-pi LLM can reach must let it fetch an INDIVIDUAL element (by id / key / path), never only the whole blob. Today element-level read exists ONLY for blocks (read-block-item by id + read-block-page offset/limit, FGAP-045) plus resolve-item-by-id (cross-block id) and read-samples-catalog (kind-level). Every other JSON surface is JSON.stringify + truncateHead WHOLE-BLOB: read-config (index.ts:964, NO params -> returns the entire config.json; cannot read one registry like relation_types, or one entry by key), read-schema (:1206, whole schema per kind; cannot read one property/field), context-roadmap-load (:1867, whole roadmap; no per-phase/per-milestone), context-edges-for-lens (whole lens edge set), read-block-dir (whole dir). Read-all-or-nothing is the anti-pattern to eliminate everywhere: when a surface exceeds the 50KB cap the LLM gets a truncated, unreliable view and hand-waves/confabulates (FGAP-089/092) instead of surgically fetching the element it needs. This is the UNIVERSAL principle of which FGAP-045 (blocks) and FGAP-101 (the tool catalog) are per-surface instances. DRY DIMENSION (user-emphasized 2026-05-25): the element-reads that exist are hand-rolled per surface (read-block-item is block-specific); adding read-config-entry / read-schema-field / read-roadmap-phase as separate bespoke tools would repeat the same addressing + extraction + truncation logic N times. The fix must be ONE shared element-addressing primitive every JSON surface routes through — not per-surface copies.

evidence.reference: read-config takes NO params and JSON.stringify+truncateHead the entire config.json — no way to read a single registry (e.g. relation_types) or one entry by key. Whole-blob-or-nothing.
read-schema returns the whole schema for a kind; no path/property addressing to read one field definition.
context-roadmap-load returns the whole roadmap; no per-phase / per-milestone element read (cf. FGAP-014 render pagination).
read-block-item + read-block-page are the ONLY element-level reads — and they are block-specific, the bespoke pattern to generalize into a shared primitive rather than copy per surface.

The JSON.stringify + truncateHead + '[Truncated: N bytes exceeds 50KB limit]' whole-blob pattern is repeated across ~8 read handlers (read-block-dir/read-block/read-config/read-samples-catalog/read-schema/list-tools/read-block-item/read-block-page) — duplicated truncation+serialization logic that a shared primitive would unify.

gaps.impact: Any JSON surface that grows past 50KB becomes undetailed-readable: a config with many registries, a large schema, a big roadmap, the tool catalog (FGAP-101) — the LLM cannot surgically fetch the element it needs, so it truncates+hand-waves (FGAP-089) or confabulates (FGAP-092). And the per-surface implementation guarantees drift and duplicated addressing/truncation logic as surfaces multiply — the opposite of DRY.
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: element-level-read, addressable-read, json-path-extraction, DRY-single-primitive, read-all-or-nothing-antipattern, structured-completeness-envelope
gaps.proposed_resolution: Introduce ONE generic element-addressable read primitive: a shared function taking a surface + an addressing key (id | object-key | JSON-path) + paging, wrapping every result in the structured-completeness envelope (reuse read-block-page's {items,total,hasMore} / the FGAP-089 structured truncation signal). Route EVERY JSON read surface through it — config (one registry / one entry by key), schema (one property/field by path), roadmap (one phase/milestone), relations (edges by endpoint/type), samples-catalog (deepen beyond kind), block-dir, AND refactor the existing block element-read (read-block-item/read-block-page) onto it so blocks are not a special case. No bespoke read-X-item per surface — each is a parameterization of the one primitive; dual-surface per DEC-0019/0020 = one library fn + one tool + one CLI reused. Generalizes FGAP-045 (blocks), subsumes FGAP-101 (list-tools index/detail becomes an instance), and carries FGAP-089's structured signal in the shared envelope. Implementation via plan mode (source edit).

gaps.title: tsx-eval-reachable modules must not statically import the SDK root barrel for VALUES — it silently breaks the orchestrator's tsx -e verification path
gaps.description: @earendil-works/pi-coding-agent's package.json exports map has only "." + "./hooks" (no tools/core subpath) and the root barrel has no resolvable exports main under `tsx -e` -> ERR_PACKAGE_PATH_NOT_EXPORTED. A static VALUE import of the root barrel in any module reachable from context-sdk / block-api / context (the tsx-eval-reachable graph) therefore breaks `npx tsx -e "...validateContext/contextState..."` — the orchestrator's primary verification path — while npm build / npm test (tsx --test) / the in-pi extension all still resolve (so the gate stays green and the breakage is silent). Occurred 2026-05-25 when FGAP-103's read-element.ts imported truncateHead from the root barrel (read-element <- context-sdk). Type-only root-barrel imports (e.g. index.ts ExtensionAPI) are erased at compile and are harmless. Recurrence of the class documented in analysis/2026-05-10-tsx-barrel-pi-coding-agent-flake.md.

evidence.reference: exports map = { ".": ..., "./hooks": ... } — no tools/core subpath; root barrel unresolvable under tsx -e

the regression: static `import { truncateHead } from "@earendil-works/pi-coding-agent"` (root barrel) pulled into context-sdk's graph via context-sdk.ts:22 -> broke validateContext via tsx -e. Fixed by vendoring (TASK).
prior documentation of the same tsx-eval + SDK-root-barrel hazard class

gaps.impact: Silent degradation of the orchestrator's tsx -e verification harness (used constantly for validateContext/contextState/state checks); the gate (npm test) does not catch it because tsx --test resolves the barrel. Each new SDK util needed by a tsx-eval-reachable module risks re-breaking it.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Rule: tsx-eval-reachable modules (anything imported by context-sdk/block-api/context) must NOT statically import the SDK root barrel for VALUES. Vendor the needed util locally (as done for truncateHead, TASK), or import from a resolving SDK subpath if the SDK adds one. Type-only imports are fine (erased). Optional hardening: a check/test that greps tsx-eval-reachable modules for root-barrel value-imports.

gaps.title: Derived-state / computed read tools bypass serializeForRead — uncapped full bodies: no token-budget protection and no fail-closed if a result exceeds the cap
gaps.description: FGAP-089/103 routed the DATA reads (read-block/read-config/read-schema/read-samples-catalog/list-tools/join-blocks + block-item/page) through serializeForRead, giving them capping + fail-closed + addressing. But the DERIVED-STATE / computed read tools return their result via a raw `JSON.stringify(result, null, 2)` directly as the tool text, bypassing serializeForRead entirely: context-status, context-current-state, context-validate, context-validate-relations, context-bootstrap-state, resolve-item-by-id, context-walk-descendants, and the roadmap render/validate/list paths (surfaced by the FGAP-089 fresh-context audit, 2026-05-25). Consequence: these results are UNCAPPED. There is NO partial-as-complete risk (they do not truncate — they arrive whole), so this is NOT a fail-closed regression. But (a) a >50KB derived state (e.g. context-status / a deep walk / a large validate issue-list as the substrate grows) arrives whole -> token-budget pressure / context bloat with no signal, and (b) the read surface is INCONSISTENT — some reads cap+fail-closed, these do not. The user's intent was 'all over-reads' uniform; these were missed because they don't currently truncate.

evidence.reference: raw JSON.stringify(result,null,2) returned as tool text (bypassing serializeForRead) at the derived-state read handlers: context-status (~944), context-validate (~967), context-current-state (~1168), context-bootstrap-state (~1193), resolve-item-by-id (~1529), context-validate-relations (~1749), context-walk-descendants (~1803), roadmap render-error/validate/list (~2009/2044/2065). Line numbers approximate (per the FGAP-089 audit grep); the locus is every read handler not using serializeForRead.
serializeForRead is the chokepoint that provides capping + fail-closed (FGAP-089) + the structured envelope; the derived-state reads do not route through it.

gaps.impact: As the substrate grows, a large derived-state result (status/walk/validate-issues) arrives uncapped -> token-budget pressure / context bloat for the in-pi LLM, with no completeness signal. Inconsistent read surface (some reads capped/fail-closed, these not). Completes the 'all over-reads uniform' intent that FGAP-089/103 began.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Route the derived-state / computed read tools through serializeForRead too, so capping + fail-closed + the structured envelope are UNIVERSAL across every read result. Most are single computed objects with no natural addressing -> the edge-case head-leading marker applies on over-cap; for the few that are collections (resolve-items map, walk results, validate issue-lists) pass paging/overCapDirective where a narrowing exists. Consider a summary/count mode for inherently-large derived state (intersects FGAP-014 render-pagination). Goal: NO read handler emits a raw uncapped JSON.stringify; every read goes through the one serializer.

gaps.title: Substrate-dir name '.project' is hardcoded in framework-facing tool descriptions + SKILL.md — teaches a fresh agent that state lives in .project, contra DEC-0015 (no default dir; user-chosen)
gaps.description: A fresh constrained agent (no-pointer scratch dir) surveyed pi-context and repeatedly asserted that project state 'lives under a .project/ directory' and that no-pointer = 'no .project/ dir at all'. There is NO default substrate dir (DEC-0015/0011; the dir is chosen at /context start, suggested .context; FGAP-094 dropped the config root:.project default). The leak is at the DESCRIPTION level: tool descriptions + param hints + result labels hardcode '.project/' as the canonical/example dir, and SKILL.md (generated from them) propagates it. So a fresh agent learns the wrong, repo-specific dir name as canonical. The code path-resolution is fine (pointer-driven via .pi-context.json, any dir name); only the human/agent-facing GUIDANCE strings leak. Note: the init/dir-name surfaces were already fixed (index.ts:400 '/context init .project or /context init .context … Per DEC-0015, no default'; :1411 'Substrate dir name (e.g. .project). Required per DEC-0015 — no default') — that correct pattern just wasn't applied to the READ tool descriptions.

evidence.reference: read-block-dir desc 'all .json files in a .project/<subdir>/' + param 'Subdirectory under .project/' — presents .project as the dir
resolve-item-by-id desc 'across all .project/ blocks'
serializeForRead labels + handoff/install/roadmap descs hardcode .project/ + .project/config.json
the CORRECT pattern already present on the init/dir-name surfaces (cites DEC-0015 no-default) — extend this to the read descriptions

gaps.impact: Every fresh agent is mis-taught the substrate dir on first contact — it confidently reports/uses '.project' (this repo's frozen choice) as the canonical/default dir, contra DEC-0015 (user picks at /context start, suggested .context). Onboarding-relevant (intersects DEC-0042 /context start + FGAP-090 orientation). The agent's bootstrap-state table even says no-pointer = 'no .project/ dir' — doubly wrong (no dir AND no chosen name yet).
gaps.proposed_resolution: Scrub '.project' from framework-facing GUIDANCE (tool descriptions, param hints, result labels) — replace with a neutral referent: 'the substrate dir (resolved via .pi-context.json; you choose it at /context start, suggested .context)' or '<substrate-dir>/<block>.json'. Keep code path-resolution pointer-driven (unchanged). Regenerate SKILL.md. Extend the already-correct DEC-0015 framing at index.ts:400/1411 to all read descriptions. Pairs with FGAP-090 (the orientation block should also frame the dir as user-chosen) + DEC-0015/0042.

gaps.title: FGAP-090 orientation block directs the agent to `/context start`, which is NOT a registered subcommand — mis-guides to a non-existent command (conductor is FGAP-095-pending)
gaps.description: The FGAP-090 eager orientation block (orientation.ts:36, injected via before_agent_start) tells the in-pi agent 'Bootstrap / onboarding: run /context start'. But /context start is NOT a registered subcommand — the registered CONTEXT_SUBCOMMANDS are init/install/accept-all/view/lens-curate/roadmap-*/status/add-work/validate/help (no 'start'; per the 2026-05-25 explore agent). The /context start unified conductor is DEC-0042-enacted but FGAP-095 P2-P6-pending (only P1 deriveBootstrapState landed). So a fresh agent following the orientation directs the user to a command that returns 'unknown subcommand'. The real current ceremony is /context init <dir> -> /context accept-all -> /context install (three separate subcommands). Same guidance-accuracy class as FGAP-106 (.project leak): the eager guidance describes aspirational/wrong surface vs the live one.

evidence.reference: '- Bootstrap / onboarding: run `/context start`.' — references an unregistered command (shipped in FGAP-090/TASK-074)

CONTEXT_SUBCOMMANDS registration has no 'start' key (init/install/accept-all/view/lens-curate/roadmap-list/roadmap-view/roadmap-validate/status/add-work/validate/help); the real ceremony is init -> accept-all -> install (per the explore agent enumeration)

gaps.impact: A fresh constrained agent, steered by the orientation block, tells the user to 'run /context start' — which fails (unknown subcommand). The keystone onboarding guidance points at vapor; mis-onboards every fresh user until /context start is built or the orientation names the real ceremony.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Until FGAP-095 builds the /context start conductor, change the orientation block (and any tool/SKILL text referencing /context start) to name the ACTUAL current bootstrap: /context init <dir> -> /context accept-all -> /context install. When FGAP-095 lands /context start, switch the orientation to it (single entry per DEC-0042). Pairs with FGAP-106 (same eager-guidance-accuracy surface) + FGAP-090 + FGAP-095 + DEC-0042. Verify via a live constrained run that the orientation names a command that actually resolves.

gaps.title: Agent-facing /context init guidance omits or inconsistently names the required <substrate-dir> argument
gaps.description: Several agent-facing strings instruct the agent/human to 'run /context init' WITHOUT the required substrate-dir argument, or with an inconsistent placeholder token. Per DEC-0015 /context init takes a user-chosen dir name (no default; suggested .context); a bare '/context init' under-specifies the onboarding step. The codebase already carries two correct idioms: the placeholder form '/context init <substrate-dir>' (orientation.ts:36, index.ts:1179/1411, and the FGAP-106 edits at lens-view.ts:48/229, roadmap-plan.ts:301) and the self-explaining form (index.ts:400: '/context init requires a substrate dir name (e.g. .context). Per DEC-0015, no default'). The defective sites diverge from both. Same onboarding-guidance-accuracy family as FGAP-107 (/context start) but a distinct class (command-argument completeness, not command name). Partly INTRODUCED by FGAP-106's rule-3 (append <substrate-dir> to /context init only inside the .project-bearing strings being edited), which left sibling bare strings inconsistent. Surfaced by the FGAP-106 fresh-context audit. Enumeration to be made exhaustive via explore (audit listed 6; prior FGAP-106 passes proved first-pass enumerations non-exhaustive).

evidence.reference: 'No .pi-context.json bootstrap pointer found. Run /context init first to bootstrap the substrate.' — bare /context init (notify + result.error)
'No config.json found in substrate dir — run /context init first.' — bare
notify('Run /context init <dir> first') — uses <dir>, inconsistent with the <substrate-dir> token used elsewhere
'Block schema not found ... Run /context init to scaffold schemas.' — bare, in a validation issue message
thrown: 'no .pi-context.json bootstrap pointer ...; run /context init to declare substrate dir per DEC-0015' — bare
the CORRECT established idioms (self-explaining at 400; <substrate-dir> placeholder at 1179/1411) to converge the defective sites onto

gaps.impact: A fresh agent steered by these messages tells the human to 'run /context init' without naming that it requires a chosen dir argument — incomplete onboarding guidance, same first-contact mis-onboarding family as FGAP-106/107. The <dir> vs <substrate-dir> token split also presents an inconsistent vocabulary for the same concept.
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: onboarding command-affordance completeness / consistent placeholder vocabulary
gaps.proposed_resolution: Exhaustively enumerate all agent-facing /context init|accept-all|install mentions (src + behavior-monitors/index.ts + skill-narrative.md + generated SKILL.md), then converge every /context init mention onto a single canonical idiom: the placeholder '/context init <substrate-dir>' for terse error/notify strings, or the self-explaining form (cite DEC-0015 'no default; you choose the name') where space allows; retire the '<dir>' token in favor of '<substrate-dir>'. Update lockstep tests; regenerate SKILL.md. Verify via the same DEC-0018 gate (build/check/test + live constrained run + fresh-context audit). Resolve under TASK-079 alongside FGAP-106/107.

gaps.title: Exported-API JSDoc + code-comments assert false .project/ resolution paths, contradicting pointer-resolved code and the FGAP-108-corrected READMEs
gaps.description: Surfaced by the FGAP-108 fresh-context audit. Several exported-API JSDoc blocks (and module-level / interface doc-comments) document substrate discovery/resolution paths as literal `.project/...` tier-lists — e.g. agent-spec.ts:210 and types.ts:96 ('1. {cwd}/.project/agents/{name}.agent.yaml'), template.ts:6 ('1. {cwd}/.project/templates/'), behavior-monitors/index.ts:190-192 ('block-api derives the on-disk path as <cwd>/.project/<block>.json'). The runtime code is POINTER-RESOLVED (verified: agent-spec.ts/template.ts use tryResolveContextDir; compile.ts uses schemaPath; both from @davidorex/pi-context/context-dir; no hardcoded `.project`), so the JSDoc is factually wrong AND now directly contradicts the FGAP-108-corrected README (pi-jit-agents/README.md:31 says `{cwd}/<substrate-dir>/agents/...`). This is the developer-API-doc surface (IDE hover / generated API docs), distinct from the agent-facing + end-user-README surfaces FGAP-106/108 fixed (those were deliberately scoped to LEAVE JSDoc). Same .project-as-canonical mis-teaching class, different audience. NOT a code bug — code is correct; only the docs lag.

evidence.reference: createAgentLoader JSDoc tier-list '1. {cwd}/.project/agents/{name}.agent.yaml' — code uses tryResolveContextDir (agent-spec.ts:226)
LoadContext JSDoc teaches .project/agents/, .project/<name>.json, .project/schemas/<name>.schema.json — pointer-resolved in reality; contradicts README:31
module-level JSDoc '1. {cwd}/.project/templates/' — code uses tryResolveContextDir (template.ts:47)
MonitorAction JSDoc 'block-api derives the on-disk path as <cwd>/.project/<block>.json' — block-api is pointer-resolved

JSDoc describing block-api as operating on .project/*.json — illustrative/historical; resolution is pointer-driven. Enumerate exhaustively (the audit found a subset; ~30+ .project JSDoc lines across packages).

gaps.impact: A developer reading the exported pi-jit-agents / pi-behavior-monitors / pi-context API (IDE hover or generated docs) is taught that discovery/writes go to a fixed `.project/` dir — false per DEC-0015 (pointer-resolved, user-chosen dir) and contradicting the corrected READMEs. Low runtime impact (docs only) but a real internal contradiction introduced by FGAP-108's README fixes.
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: API documentation accuracy / single-source-of-truth for resolution-path docs
gaps.proposed_resolution: Exhaustively enumerate (partitioned grep, pure enumeration) every JSDoc/code-comment across all packages that asserts a `.project/` resolution or discovery path (tier-lists, 'derives the on-disk path as', 'reads .project/*.json', 'resolves to .project/schemas/...'). Converge to the pointer-resolved truth: reference resolveContextDir / the substrate-dir (e.g. '<substrate-dir>/agents/' or 'the resolved substrate dir') rather than the literal `.project`. Distinguish factually-wrong path-assertions (FIX) from incidental this-repo/historical mentions. Keep code unchanged. Verify: zero JSDoc `.project`-as-resolution-path outside an explicit LEAVE set; no README/JSDoc contradiction remains. Same DEC-0018 gate where applicable (build/check/test; this is comment-only so no runtime demo needed beyond grep + audit).

gaps.title: Per-item valid/current bits + a constrained-agent read-gate (reads only valid+current) — makes blocks-not-changelogs structural and generalizes the substrate to a domain-agnostic data room
gaps.description: Generalizes pi-context from a software-project substrate to a domain-agnostic context engine (the 'data room' pattern: any work that needs curated context; the conception/config is the only domain-specific part). Two net-new primitives: (1) every block item carries two independent bits — `valid` (an audit has confirmed the item is accurate/grounded against its raw source) and `current` (not superseded; the live one) — set NOT by the process that authored/decomposed the item but by a SEPARATE audit process (separation of powers: decomposer 'finds', validator 'decides'); (2) a READ-GATE so the constrained agent's default read surface is exactly `valid AND current` — superseded/journey/un-audited content lives in a raw tier (immutable provenance) + git and is structurally OUT of the constrained agent's reach. This makes 'blocks are current-truth, not changelogs' (feedback_substrate_blocks_not_changelogs) a STRUCTURAL INVARIANT rather than a discipline that can be (and was) broken: the agent cannot mistake a changelog for truth because it never sees one. The decider is a permitted JIT agent (read + amend-block) amending the bit with a grounded contract (cite the raw span it validated against) — mechanism = existing primitives (jit dispatch + updateItemInBlock + DispatchContext attestation); this gap is the FIELD it amends + the GATE that enforces the read boundary. Extends the FGAP-089 read chokepoint (serializeForRead / lens read tools) — same enforcement point, adds a valid+current filter as the constrained-agent read mode. Four-state model from the two bits: valid+current = default agent read (authoritative live truth); valid+superseded = 'background' (accurate-but-old, e.g. an old deck; exposed only on explicit request, never in the default gate); invalid (un-audited or failed) = raw tier, never agent-readable. Concept source: analysis of Nate B. Jones 'data room' (/Users/david/Projects/data-room-pi-context.md) — source-inventory/conflict-log/missing-context/duplicates artifacts map onto typed blocks + closure-table edges; this gap is the staleness/validity primitive that underpins them.

evidence.reference: serializeForRead is the read chokepoint (FGAP-089 fail-closed); the valid+current read-gate extends it as the constrained-agent read mode
updateItemInBlock + DispatchContext attestation already provide the amend-the-bit mechanism; the bits are not yet schema fields
proof-of-mechanism that a SEPARATE audit process flips a status via an edge; valid/current generalizes this from task-verification to per-item bits
concept source: 'read valid + current, not full; the valid bit flipped by a different audit than the decomposer; organization-management as a task; raw -> decompose into blocks'

gaps.impact: Without it, 'blocks-not-changelogs' stays a discipline the LLM must follow (and breaks under correction-layering); the constrained agent can read superseded/journey/un-audited content and mistake it for current truth — the exact structural-hallucination failure the data-room pattern exists to prevent. With it, validity/staleness becomes a substrate invariant, the same engine serves any domain via a conception pack (software dev is conception #1), and the constrained agent's context is by construction the curated valid+current projection.
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: validity/recency lifecycle bits + read-time visibility gating (curated-projection-as-read-surface)
gaps.proposed_resolution: 1. Decide the semantics fork: two independent bits (`valid`, `current`) vs one lifecycle enum — and whether the read-gate is a HARD harness boundary or a default-on lens. 2. Add `valid` + `current` (+ provenance: who/when/against-what-raw-span) as schema-level item fields, settable only via the audit flip (not the authoring write). 3. Extend serializeForRead / the lens read tools with a constrained-agent read mode returning only valid+current by default; valid+superseded ("background") only on explicit request; raw/invalid never. 4. FLIP AUTHORITY = HUMAN-IN-THE-LOOP (NOT FINALIZED — needs more thinking; verging into Karpathy LLM-wiki territory): the JIT validator agent PROFFERS a grounded flip (reads raw + block, cites the raw span/evidence) but the HUMAN'S OK is the authority that actually flips valid/current — the agent does not self-certify. The permitted JIT agent (read + amend-block, depends FGAP-099 clamp) prepares + proposes; the human approves; intersects FGAP-102 (grounded/real-check-not-LLM) + DEC-0047 (judiciary). Whether/when this can delegate to a real-check vs always-human is the open question. Depends on the raw tier + decomposition front-end (FGAP-111). Concept source: /Users/david/Projects/data-room-pi-context.md. Verify: a constrained agent loaded against a substrate with superseded+invalid items reads ONLY valid+current, cannot reach the journey; round-trip decompose(FGAP-111) -> agent-proffer -> human-OK flip -> read demonstrated.

gaps.title: Raw tier + raw->block decomposition front-end — ingest heterogeneous source files, preserve originals, decompose into typed source-blocks (the data-room ingestion step)
gaps.description: The one genuinely-missing capability for the data-room generalization (FGAP-110): pi-context operates on already-typed JSON blocks; it has no front-end that walks a folder tree, reads heterogeneous raw source files (PDF / deck / transcript / spreadsheet / email / md), PRESERVES the originals, and DECOMPOSES them into typed source-blocks (inventory rows that point at the preserved original via path + metadata — the block is the inventory row, not the file content). This is the 'build me the room / source inventory' first step of the data-room workflow ('raw: then decompose into blocks'). The decomposition is the constrained agent's 'finds' work: it produces CANDIDATE blocks that enter the RAW tier marked invalid + not-current (un-audited) — they become valid+current only via the separate human-in-the-loop flip in FGAP-110 (agent finds, human decides). Feeds FGAP-110 (provides the raw items the valid/current gate curates) and underpins the data-room artifacts (source inventory, conflict log, missing-context, duplicates) which are then typed blocks + closure-table edges over the decomposed source-blocks.

evidence.reference: 'find the relevant materials, preserve the originals, build me a data inventory... summarize every source before you synthesize. do not write the deliverable yet' — the ingestion/decomposition step; 'raw: then decompose into blocks'
appendToBlock / writeBlock are the write target for decomposed source-blocks; the front-end that produces them from raw files does not exist

gaps.impact: Without ingestion, the data-room generalization (FGAP-110) has nothing to curate — the substrate can only hold hand-authored blocks, not a decomposed source set. The 'first prompt is build the room, not do the thing' workflow (structurally antagonistic to hallucination) is unreachable for general knowledge work; pi-context stays software-project-only in practice even with the valid/current machinery.
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: source ingestion + provenance-preserving decomposition (raw -> typed inventory blocks)
gaps.proposed_resolution: Define: (1) a raw tier (preserved originals + a manifest) distinct from the typed-block tier; (2) a decomposition contract for a constrained JIT agent — walk tree, read raw, emit candidate source-blocks (path/type/date/authority-guess/supports/limitations) that point at preserved originals and enter marked invalid+not-current; (3) the source-block kind(s) shipped as a conception pack (intersects the data-room conception, FGAP-110). Agent finds, never auto-promotes; promotion is FGAP-110's human-in-the-loop flip. Open: how much decomposition is in-engine vs a dispatched agent's job; heterogeneous-file reading (the harness/agent reads PDFs/decks, not pi-context core). Needs more thinking alongside FGAP-110.

gaps.title: Agent-as-tool dispatch home — narrowed to a NEW dedicated pi-agent-dispatch extension whose ONLY scope is the sub-agent→sibling-agent pi.registerTool surface; jit-agents stays library and is imported directly by orchestrator + workflows + monitors
gaps.description: Refined framing (2026-05-26, after working-out with three-consumer constraint). Original FGAP-112 framed DEC-0044 as a binary conflict with JI-021 (orchestrator uses jit-agents directly), with two coherent paths. Working-out collapsed the disjunction: (1) jit-agents is canonically library-not-extension per CLAUDE.md package table + docs/planning/jit-agents-spec.md four-boundary-surfaces definition — B1 (jit-agents-grows-extension-surface) is dead. (2) jit-agents must be usable by orchestrator AND pi-workflows AND pi-behavior-monitors (DEC-0049 uniform-agent axiom) — all three consume jit-agents as a SHARED LIBRARY via direct npm import (TASK-080 is establishing this for workflows + monitors right now). (3) JI-021 'orchestrator uses jit-agents directly' is honored under any path where the orchestrator's own use is a library import — JI-021 does NOT speak to where the sub-agent→sibling-agent registration site lives. (4) The remaining narrow question is exclusively: where does pi.registerTool('call-agent', …) — the surface that exposes agent-as-tool dispatch to sub-agents executing inside pi -p subprocesses — live. (5) Two candidates remained: Path A = dedicated pi-agent-dispatch extension; Path B2a = pi-workflows hosts the registration. Path B2a fails because monitor subprocesses would have to runtime-load pi-workflows to gain agent-as-tool capability, recreating a directional monitors→workflows runtime-load edge precisely when TASK-080 dismantles the source-import edge of the same shape (empirically verified: monitor index.ts:25-27 currently imports createAgentLoader+compileAgentSpec+AgentSpec from pi-workflows, all marked for deletion by TASK-080). (6) Path A survives all constraints. The narrowed Path A is the determinate framing.

evidence.reference: DEC-0044.decision (full): 'the agents-as-tools dispatch surface registers in a NEW dedicated agent-dispatch extension that wraps the pi-jit-agents library' — verbatim from the enacted decision.

JI-021 user verbatim: orchestrator uses jit-agents directly. JI-010 user verbatim: jit-agents owns 'everything between I have a spec and I have a typed result.' JI-038 user verbatim: settle DEC-0044 by exploring jit-agents.
EV-001 conflict — flagged the divergence with JI-010/021/038 as the contradicted intentions.

TASK-080.notes + TASK-080.acceptance_criteria already encode 'NOTE: this scope assumes the user-intent framing (orchestrator uses jit-agents directly per JI-021/010); DEC-0044 new-extension home (EV-001 conflict) awaits separate re-anchoring and is NOT a target of this task.'

gaps.impact: Narrows DEC-0044 to a smaller and clearer surface than the original 'extension wrapping pi-jit-agents' language suggested. The dispatch extension's scope is EXCLUSIVELY the sub-agent→sibling-agent pi.registerTool surface; it does NOT wrap or intermediate the orchestrator's use of jit-agents (which is a direct library import per JI-021). Unblocks FEAT-004 (registration locus = the new pi-agent-dispatch extension), FEAT-005 (capability-clamp lives in the dispatch extension's tool implementation, calling jit-agents library with grant ⊆ enforcement at the boundary), and FGAP-099 (clamp re-homes to the dispatch extension after its construction). TASK-080's body (whole agent layer consumption as shared library) is unchanged and remains correct under this narrowing.
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: agent-as-tool registration site / pi-jit-agents library scope / extension peer-load symmetry / uniform-agent dispatch surface
gaps.proposed_resolution: ADOPT NARROWED PATH A as canon. Concretely: (1) jit-agents stays library, four boundary surfaces (load/compile/execute/introspect), per its spec — NO extension surface added. (2) Orchestrator + pi-workflows + pi-behavior-monitors consume jit-agents as a shared npm library via direct import (TASK-080 finishes this for workflows + monitors). (3) A NEW extension package pi-agent-dispatch (FEAT-004's home) registers EXACTLY ONE tool: pi.registerTool('call-agent', …) (name TBD), whose implementation calls jit-agents' executeAgent() under the hood, enforcing parent-grant ⊆ child-grant clamp (FEAT-005) at the dispatch boundary. (4) pi-agent-dispatch is loaded peer-to-peer by any subprocess context whose sub-agents may invoke siblings — workflow subprocesses, monitor subprocesses, orchestrator if it ever runs agents that need agent-as-tool. (5) Edit DEC-0044 to replace 'a NEW dedicated agent-dispatch extension that wraps the pi-jit-agents library' with the narrower 'a NEW dedicated pi-agent-dispatch extension whose ONLY scope is the agent-as-tool pi.registerTool surface; pi-jit-agents remains library and is imported directly by orchestrator, workflows, and monitors; the extension does not wrap or intermediate library use, it exclusively provides the agent-as-tool registration site for sub-agent dispatch.' (6) Close this FGAP on DEC-0044 edit. Path B variants (B1 jit-agents-as-extension; B2a workflows-hosts-registration; B2b no-agent-as-tool-surface) are documented in this gap's description as eliminated and NOT live options. | CLOSED 2026-05-26: NARROWED PATH A adopted as canon. Closing commits: 3da9edc (FGAP-112 narrowing — three-consumer working-out, B1/B2a/B2b eliminated, narrowed Path A determinate) + a71c782 (DEC-0044 body narrowed to match: extension scope = ONLY the sub-agent→sibling-agent pi.registerTool site + dispatch-boundary capability clamp; jit-agents stays library imported directly by orchestrator + workflows + monitors). Verification: VER-051 (verification_verifies_item edge VER-051 → FGAP-112). Schema extension required for canonical closure: verification.target_type enum extended with 'framework-gap' (this commit) — prior schema admitted only task|phase|requirement.

gaps.title: decisions block conflates pre-decisions with decisions — block-name lies about content + status enum collapses operationally-distinct sub-states
gaps.description: The .project/decisions.json block, by its name, carries the colloquial meaning 'what has been decided.' Its schema (.project/schemas/decisions.schema.json) declares status enum [open | enacted | superseded] where 'open' entries are explicitly 'decisions that need to be made and gate work.' The block therefore contains pre-decisions AND actual decisions simultaneously; the name lies about half the content. The schema's own description acknowledges the stretch with a stipulative definition: 'Each entry is a decision either open (needs to be made and gates work), enacted (ratified by user authority...), or superseded.' Coherent stipulatively; at odds with the word 'decisions' colloquially. Compounding: the single 'open' state collapses at least three operationally-distinct sub-states — (1) recognized (decision-need identified, no drafted answer), (2) drafted (decision field carries canon-derived answer awaiting user review), (3) reviewed-and-revised (user reviewed + widened/refined, only enactment stamp pending). From substrate-query perspective, all three report status:open — indistinguishable. Fresh contexts cannot tell ready-to-enact from still-needs-framing. Empirical evidence in analysis/2026-05-26-decisions-block-shape-survey.md (commit 4900a34): 49 entries surveyed, 20 emergent shape-clusters identified including writer-identity format variance, enacted-tuple completeness variance, in-field stacked revision prose (Amendment/Widening/narrowed), null-state convention variance, decision-as-question vs decision-as-answer. Cross-mapped via data-room lens (per /Users/david/Projects/data-room-pi-context.md and grounding analysis at commit 083630e): our single decisions block conflates three first-class data-room artifacts — decisions (settled), missing-context-list (pre-decisions), conflict-log (in-substrate contradictions).

evidence.reference: status enum: [open, enacted, superseded]; schema description acknowledges the stipulative stretch — 'Each entry is a decision either open (needs to be made and gates work)...'
49 entries surveyed; 20 emergent shape-clusters; commit 4900a34
Side-quest grounding incl. semantic gap framing, data-room mapping, canon-mapping table; commit 083630e

Current state at time of filing: DEC-0001/0002/0003 are open with drafted answers (status:open) but indistinguishable from a hypothetical entry that was open without any draft; DEC-0040 carries amendment block as in-body stacking; DEC-0044 narrowing prose (commit a71c782); DEC-0003 widening (commit cfbb211).

gaps.impact: Substrate-shape conflation impacting all decision-substrate consumers. Fresh contexts cannot distinguish pre-decisions from decisions without field-level inspection. The block name actively misleads as documentation. The status enum coarseness causes the cognitive-load problem observed in handling DEC-0001/0002/0003 — they look identical at status-query level but are in operationally-distinct lifecycle states. The in-body revision stacking (Amendment/Widening) observed in DEC-0040/0044/0003 violates feedback_substrate_blocks_not_changelogs but has no first-class structural alternative under the current schema. Downstream: relations.json edges to decisions.json reference both ratified canon AND pre-decisions; lens projections cannot cleanly distinguish.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Addressed by FEAT-008 (decisions-substrate revision — 3-block split into recognitions / decisions / substrate-conflicts + uniform field tightening) per architectural decision DEC-0050 (data-room three-artifact frame adoption). See analysis/2026-05-26-decisions-substrate-revision-grounding.md commit 083630e for the full canon-mapping showing every revision element traces to established canon (DEC-0040, FGAP-098/110/111, feedback_substrate_blocks_not_changelogs, DispatchContext attestation, data-room frame). Nothing in the resolution is fresh architectural invention.

gaps.title: task.acceptance_criteria is string[] — no AC-addressability for FK depends_on references; no per-criterion status tracking; completion invariant collapses to 'verification field present' rather than schema-level 'every criterion passed'
gaps.description: Current .project/schemas/tasks.schema.json declares acceptance_criteria as string[]. Three gaps follow: (1) no addressability — individual criteria have no canonical id, so depends_on (or any other field that might want to reference a specific criterion) cannot FK-target one; (2) no per-criterion status tracking — task status [planned|in-progress|completed|blocked|cancelled] is task-wide; partial completion (criteria 1/2/4 passed but 3/5 pending) is invisible; (3) completion invariant collapse — the only completion check is 'verification field present,' which says nothing about whether each criterion was actually verified. Surfaced immediately by the TASK-080 split (2026-05-26, commits 4bcab3a + da3ec67 + 0a0dcd0): TASK-082 depends on TASK-081; cannot express finer-grained 'TASK-082 depends on TASK-081's criterion #4' (the executeAgent-grant-wired criterion specifically); coarsening forces serial wait on TASK-081's whole completion when partial-completion sequencing would be cheaper. Same FK-as-free-text anti-pattern documented in FGAP-040/041/046 (tasks[].phase, issues[].resolved_by, tasks[].depends_on inline FK arrays).

evidence.reference: acceptance_criteria current shape: string[]; no id pattern, no status field, no completion invariant
TASK-081/082/083/084 just-filed planned tasks (2026-05-26) — TASK-082 depends_on TASK-081 at task-grain only; cannot express criterion-grain depends_on without AC-addressability
verification.criteria_results[].criterion is free-text restating task AC text verbatim (existing duplication this gap's resolution also eliminates via FK linkage)

gaps.impact: Affects all tasks under active sequencing where finer-grained criterion-based dependencies would enable parallelism or partial-completion handoff. Affects substrate hygiene: VER entries currently duplicate AC text verbatim instead of FK-referencing. Affects completion invariant strength: 'task is completed' currently means 'verification field is populated' (a presence check) rather than 'every criterion is verified passed' (a content check). Substrate carries weaker completion guarantee than canonical-derivable shape would support.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Addressed by FEAT-009 (AC schema revision: object form {id, text, status} with ^TASK-\d{3,}-AC-\d+$ pattern, status enum [pending|passed|failed|blocked], depends_on union(task-id|ac-id), completion invariant shift to 'every criterion.status === passed') per DEC-0051 (open, drafted answer canon-derived). Scoped under .context greenfield transition (not .project in-place migration) — pairs with FEAT-008 as same-arc structural-shape revision. .project tasks continue with string[] form during the transition; object form lands in .context schemas from day one. No data migration required.

gaps.title: ExtensionContext from @earendil-works/pi-coding-agent does not expose currentModel / currentProvider — DEC-0001's enacted resolution mechanism has no canonical source
gaps.description: DEC-0001 (enacted 2026-05-26 commit bb45880) declares: 'Bare model ids in an agent spec resolve against the current session's configured provider via ExtensionContext.' Plan-mode exploration of pi-jit-agents internals (TASK-081 planning, 2026-05-26) empirically verified that pi-coding-agent's ExtensionContext does NOT expose currentModel or currentProvider fields. Grep across node_modules/@earendil-works/pi-coding-agent/ returns zero hits for 'currentModel' or 'currentProvider' as ExtensionContext fields. Model is currently passed directly as a pre-resolved Model<Api> instance in jit-agents' DispatchContext (types.ts:194), constructed by the caller from auth.json + parseModelSpec. The canonical-source DEC-0001 assumed does not exist where canon thought it did. Three resolution paths: (a) extend pi-coding-agent's ExtensionContext to expose currentModel/currentProvider — upstream dependency change; (b) sourceful resolution from auth.json + env at jit-agents level (no ExtensionContext dependency); (c) re-anchor DEC-0001 to reflect actual resolution source (Model<Api> already pre-resolved in DispatchContext; the 'bare model id' problem disappears because resolution happens BEFORE executeAgent receives the dispatch).

evidence.reference: Grep for currentModel / currentProvider as ExtensionContext fields returns zero hits (verified 2026-05-26 during TASK-081 plan-mode exploration)
DEC-0001 (enacted bb45880) decision body cites ExtensionContext as the canonical resolution source

DispatchContext (lines 193-230) carries pre-resolved Model<Api> directly; no spec-string-resolution path exists in jit-agents today; the resolution-via-ExtensionContext path DEC-0001 enacted has no implementation surface
Line 1173+1390: parseModelSpec + Model<Api> construction currently happens in the caller (monitors), defaulting to 'anthropic' — the bug DEC-0001 was filed to address

gaps.impact: Blocks DEC-0001 implementation (TASK-085). The enacted decision body cannot be implemented as written — no ExtensionContext path to current-session model exists. Either pi-coding-agent extends, or jit-agents sources differently, or DEC-0001 re-anchors. Until resolved, the original bug (monitor classify defaulting to anthropic provider regardless of session) remains in code; agents using bare model ids continue to mis-route under non-anthropic auth configurations.
gaps.proposed_resolution: User decision on resolution path: (a) pi-coding-agent upstream extension to expose currentModel/currentProvider on ExtensionContext (canonical-source-restoration); (b) jit-agents reads auth.json + env directly without ExtensionContext dependency (canonical-source-relocation); (c) DEC-0001 re-anchor to acknowledge that model resolution happens BEFORE jit-agents' executeAgent receives the dispatch (caller's responsibility, not executeAgent's) — the 'bare model id' problem moves to the caller surface, addressed by caller-side fix (e.g. monitors classifyViaAgent reads session-canonical source itself + constructs Model<Api>). Implementation surfaces in TASK-085 (DEC-0001 impl) post-FGAP-115 resolution.

gaps.title: Operation-granular capability vocabulary in pi-agent-dispatch lacks composite-tool authoring + per-KIND dual-surface CLI scripts + forbidden-wholesale enforcement — TASK-089 left FEAT-005 capability composition with name-only granularity, not the bounded-composite shape JI-023 names
gaps.description: TASK-089 (commit babe385) landed pi-agent-dispatch with capability composition (composeToolGrant intersection) + operation-vocab.ts (55 canonical Pi tool names as default operations) + config.tool_operations[] (empty seed). The mechanism grants/clamps at TOOL NAME granularity. JI-023 user verbatim names a finer granularity ('A bash command. Not bash.' + 'There can be a registry of them'), and FEAT-005's description ('operation-granular tool+perm grants from an empty-state registry') anticipates BOUNDED COMPOSITES (e.g. git-log-with-these-paths-and-this-since, read-files-only-in-these-roots) — not just 'yes/no on built-in read'. Three concrete gaps surfaced during the 2026-05-28 design exploration (analysis/2026-05-28-hybrid-3-composite-tools-canon-eval.md commit 3b4b32d + analysis/2026-05-28-hybrid-3-refined-composite-tools-design.md): (1) no composite-tool authoring infrastructure — pi-agent-dispatch admits no concept of 'composite KIND with instance params closure-bound at extension load' although pi's tool API supports it via pluggable operations; (2) no per-KIND CLI script under scripts/orchestrator/ — DEC-0019/0020 dual-surface canon requires triple-as-unit (library + Pi tool + CLI script); a composite KIND in pi-agent-dispatch would be in-pi-only without a Claude-Code-side equivalent, violating canonical symmetry; (3) no forbidden-wholesale enforcement — TOOL_OPERATION_DEFAULTS today admits any token; if 'bash' or 'write' or 'edit' is ever added to the registry, the composite-tool pattern dissolves into a gated alternative next to an unrestricted original (feedback_no_parallel_ungated_paths). All three gaps are interrelated: composite authoring without dual-surface produces asymmetric capability surfaces; composite authoring without forbidden-wholesale enforcement allows the design to degrade silently.

evidence.reference: 55 canonical Pi tool names enumerated as TOOL_OPERATION_DEFAULTS; each entry is a TOOL NAME (e.g. 'read-block', 'workflow-execute'). No KIND discriminator, no instance-params, no composite-shape support. No FORBIDDEN_WHOLESALE_OPERATIONS const.

FEAT-005 description: 'operation-granular tool+perm grants from an empty-state registry'; 'A capability/permission registry exists ... tool grants AND operation-level permissions'; 'capability bundle composed JIT'. The bounded-composite shape is implied but not yet realized.
JI-023 user verbatim: 'A bash command. Not bash.' + 'There can be a registry of them.' The finer granularity than tool-name.

Commit 3b4b32d. Canon-eval of v1 Hybrid 3 design surfaced two design-completeness items (DEC-0019/0020 dual-surface missing; forbidden-wholesale under-specified) + identified hedging on substrate-canon forbidden-list.

Refined v2 design closing both gaps with no canon evolution required: per-KIND triples + 5-layer forbidden enforcement + author-tool-grant Pi tool. 14 new + 5 modified files for first build (4 initial KINDS: read-files, git-log, grep-paths, command-allowlist).

No per-KIND composite scripts exist today. DEC-0019/0020 dual-surface canon requires triple-as-unit landings — gap currently leaves any composite KIND in-pi-only without a Claude-Code-side equivalent.

gaps.impact: Without composite-tool authoring + forbidden-wholesale enforcement + per-KIND CLI scripts, FEAT-005 capability composition stays at coarse tool-name granularity. The in-pi orchestrator can grant subagents 'read' (wholesale) or 'composeToolGrant intersection' (still wholesale-vocabulary) but cannot grant 'read-only-from-analysis-and-docs' or 'git-log-with-these-paths-and-this-since'. The bounded-composite shape JI-023 names + the FEAT-006 north-star (constrained orchestrator + privileged agent + scoped grant) require composites. Also without forbidden-wholesale enforcement, a future addition of 'bash' or 'write' to TOOL_OPERATION_DEFAULTS would silently degrade the entire FEAT-005 design into a gated-alternative-next-to-unrestricted-original (feedback_no_parallel_ungated_paths violation). DEC-0019/0020 dual-surface canon is breached for every composite added without a sibling CLI script.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Addressed by FEAT-010 (Hybrid 3 composite-tool infrastructure) per DEC-0052. Concretely the refined v2 design at analysis/2026-05-28-hybrid-3-refined-composite-tools-design.md: per-KIND triple (library function + config-driven Pi tool registration + CLI script under scripts/orchestrator/) for each of 4 initial KINDS (read-files / git-log / grep-paths / command-allowlist); 5-layer forbidden-wholesale enforcement (TS const FORBIDDEN_WHOLESALE_OPERATIONS + engine test invariant + runtime guard at extension load reading L1∪L5 union + JSDoc citation + substrate config.tool_operations_forbidden[] for project-specific additions under writer.kind=human); new author-tool-grant Pi tool wrapping the config mutation surface with writer.kind=human enforcement per DEC-0047. Zero canon evolution required — all elements are concrete enactments of existing canon (DEC-0019/0020 dual-surface; DEC-0040 substrate single source of truth; DEC-0047 capability authoring human-only; DEC-0048 engine-tests-as-gates; FEAT-005 operation-granular from empty; JI-023 bounded composites; feedback_no_parallel_ungated_paths defense-in-depth).

gaps.title: pi-agent-dispatch launch-chain invisibility — META package + generate-skills.js + package self-registration ALL omit the 5th extension; 5 static tools + entire FEAT-006 loop unreachable from launch-constrained-pi.sh
gaps.description: Audit (analysis/2026-05-28-launch-script-harness-canonical-audit.md commit 945dc93, gaps 1-5 CRITICAL) empirically established that pi-agent-dispatch is INVISIBLE to the launch chain through three coordinated absences: (1) META `packages/pi-project-workflows/package.json` `dependencies[]` (lines 32-34) + `pi.extensions[]` (lines 22-26) list only the 3 prior extensions; pi-agent-dispatch is in neither; no `agent-dispatch-extension.ts` shim parallel to the existing project/workflows/monitors shims. Therefore `pi install -l $META` step 1 of the launch script does not install pi-agent-dispatch. (2) `scripts/generate-skills.js:999` hardcodes `packageDirs = ['pi-context', 'pi-workflows', 'pi-behavior-monitors']`; pi-agent-dispatch is absent. Therefore no SKILL.md is emitted for it under `packages/pi-agent-dispatch/skills/`. (3) pi-agent-dispatch's own `package.json` has no `pi.skills` field, no `skills/` directory, no `skill-narrative.md`, no `skills/` in `files[]` — diverges from the parity pattern of the other 3 extensions. Net effect: empirical grep of `packages/*/skills/*/SKILL.md` returns 53 tool names; ZERO of pi-agent-dispatch's 5 static tools (call-agent, author-agent-spec, run-real-checks, commit-attested, author-tool-grant) appear. The launch script's --tools derivation therefore cannot include them. Launching the current script produces a session where the entire FEAT-006 north-star loop (orchestrator → author spec → dispatch agent → run-real-checks → commit-attested) is unreachable; JI-021 (orchestrator uses jit-agents directly) is violated because call-agent is absent; DEC-0044 (pi-agent-dispatch loaded peer-to-peer) is violated.

evidence.reference: lines 22-26 (pi.extensions[] omits agent-dispatch shim); lines 32-34 (dependencies[] omits @davidorex/pi-agent-dispatch)
line 999 hardcoded packageDirs list omits pi-agent-dispatch
no pi.skills field; files[] omits skills/; whole package omits skill-narrative.md + skills/ dir
Audit commit 945dc93 gaps 1-5 CRITICAL; per-gap evidence + canon-violated columns

gaps.impact: Launching the canonical `scripts/launch-constrained-pi.sh` today produces a session that cannot execute the FEAT-006 north-star loop. DEC-0014 negative clause (no bash/read/write/edit) is honored; positive clause (the canonical tool surface) is violated by omission. JI-021 + DEC-0044 + FEAT-005 + FEAT-010 + FEAT-006 all blocked at the surface where they were meant to manifest.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Addressed by FEAT-011 (pi-agent-dispatch launch-chain integration). Four file edits + 1 new file per audit's resolution scope: (a) packages/pi-project-workflows/package.json adds @davidorex/pi-agent-dispatch dep + agent-dispatch-extension.ts to pi.extensions[]; (b) NEW packages/pi-project-workflows/agent-dispatch-extension.ts one-line re-export shim mirroring the existing 3 shims; (c) packages/pi-agent-dispatch/package.json adds pi.skills field + skills/ to files[]; (d) NEW packages/pi-agent-dispatch/skill-narrative.md hand-authored narrative source (YAML frontmatter + XML-tagged sections per pi skill standard); (e) scripts/generate-skills.js packageDirs list adds 'pi-agent-dispatch'. After: npm run skills regenerates → pi-agent-dispatch SKILL.md exists with 5 static tool tags → launch script grep picks them up → launched session sees all 5. Closes via canonical-symmetry, no canon evolution required. [CLOSED 2026-05-28 via TASK-091 unified (FEAT-006+FEAT-011) merge at task-091-unified-feat-006-feat-011 + VER-057 attestation. See verification body for per-criterion evidence.]

gaps.title: Dynamic composite tools have no skills-generation pathway by construction — composite-loader reads loadContext(process.cwd()) which at build-time = repo root (config:null degrade), while per-project composite instances live in target launch dir; skill-discovery vs runtime-discovery split unreconciled
gaps.description: Audit (analysis/2026-05-28-launch-script-harness-canonical-audit.md commit 945dc93, gap 6 HIGH + F.1) empirically established: scripts/generate-skills.js:803 invokes factory(mockPi) with process.cwd() = repo root at npm run skills time. Repo root has .project/ (not .pi-context.json pointer). loadContext() graceful-degrades to {config: null}. composite-loader registers ZERO composite Pi tools under this condition. Therefore even after FGAP-117 closure (pi-agent-dispatch wired into META + skills-gen + own package self-reg), the DYNAMIC composite tools (config.tool_operations[] entries registered at extension-load) would NEVER appear in any generated SKILL.md. The launch script's --tools grep-from-static-SKILLs derivation cannot capture them. The architectural mismatch: skill discovery is build-time (per-repo), composite registration is runtime (per-target-dir). Closing FGAP-117 establishes the skills-gen path for STATIC tools but does not address dynamic composites' invisibility. Two coherent resolution paths surface: (a) launch script reads target-dir config.tool_operations[] at launch time + extends --tools allowlist with derived names (per-target-dir discovery, separate from skills-gen); (b) generate-skills.js gains a target-dir-aware mode that reads composite vocabulary from a designated source then emits a 'composites' SKILL.md (build-time per-target — adds complexity); (c) accept that composites can only be granted via flag-driven launch script extension (FGAP-120 territory) and skills-gen captures only the static surface.

gaps.impact: Bounded-composite capability vocabulary is DEMONSTRABLY available only in test fixtures (per pi-agent-dispatch tests); production-launch surface has no composite instances. gaps.proposed_resolution: Empty seed stays canon-correct: composites are project-specific bounded capabilities; no project-agnostic defaults can exist (a default `read-files-scoped-to-WHAT` makes no sense without knowing target project shape). For THIS repo's dogfooding of FEAT-006 demos, author a small composite set via the `author-tool-grant` Pi tool (writer.kind=human enforced per DEC-0047) targeting this repo's actual shape: `read-files-analysis-docs` scoped to {allowed_roots: ['analysis/', 'docs/']}, `git-log-recent` with {since: <iso>, limit: 20}, `grep-paths-substrate` scoped to .project/. End-user projects adopting pi-agent-dispatch author their own composites the same way. The `author-tool-grant` Pi tool is the SOLE canonical authoring path; calling `amendConfigEntry` directly via tsx-eval bypasses the writer.kind=human enforcement gate (anti-canon per DEC-0047 capability-widening clause + feedback_no_parallel_ungated_paths). pi-agent-dispatch README documents this authoring cadence as the canonical pattern post-FEAT-011. Sequencing: FEAT-011 (launch-chain integration) is the hard prerequisite — it lands first so a constrained pi session can be launched where author-tool-grant is reachable; then the authoring TASK invokes author-tool-grant 3 times (one per composite) from inside that session.

gaps.title: SKILL.md surfaces don't anchor canonical vocabulary; in-pi agent enumerates block_kinds/relation_types from prior expectation rather than from substrate query
gaps.description: Observed 2026-05-28 in launched constrained-pi session: agent asked to describe pi-context to operator enumerated 8 block_kinds (canon ships 16) including the invented 'roadmap' kind that doesn't exist, and gave verb-only relation_type shortforms ('verifies', 'addresses', 'implements') that violate the canonical '<parent>_<verb>_<child>' form and will be refused by append-relation schema validation at write time. The agent had read-samples-catalog (pre-install) and read-config (post-install) in its tool surface but no instruction in the pi-context SKILL.md telling it: 'before describing or recommending block_kinds / relation_types to a user, call read-samples-catalog (pre-install) or read-config (post-install) — never enumerate from prior expectation.' The agent reasoned from general schema-substrate priors instead of querying the substrate.

ships 16 canonical block_kinds and 28 canonical relation_types; agent's response enumerated 8 + 4 respectively, with 1 invented kind ('roadmap') and 1 invented relation_type ('task implements requirement')

Optionally extend to: 'Use the returned canonical_id values verbatim; do not abbreviate relation_type names to verb-only shortforms.'

gaps.title: Canonical relation_type naming convention '<parent>_<verb>_<child>' is invisible to freshly-launched agents; no operator-facing layer surfaces the pattern or rejects verb-only shortforms with a helpful diagnostic

The '<parent>_<verb>_<child>' naming convention is canon in samples/conception.json relation_types[] but is not declared as a convention anywhere an agent or operator naturally reads pre-action. append-relation schema validation will refuse a verb-only relation_type at write time, but the error path is downstream of operator confusion + agent miseducation upstream.

When operator tries to author an edge with the shortform, append-relation refuses with a generic 'relation_type not registered' message that doesn't point to the canonical form. Increases time-to-correct-edge + erodes operator mental model.

gaps.title: context-init lacks the writer.kind=human enforcement that documentation (HANDOFF + in-pi agent narrative) repeatedly claims it has; documented intent and code-side enforcement are out of sync
gaps.description: Observed twice in launched constrained-pi session 2026-05-28: the in-pi agent stated 'The bootstrap is human-only by design' when introducing pi-context to an operator. Verification: grep over packages/pi-context/src/index.ts at the context-init tool registration shows zero writer.kind=human enforcement — unlike author-agent-spec and author-tool-grant in pi-agent-dispatch which DO refuse non-human writers per DEC-0047. context-init's 'human-only-ness' is operator-convention documented in CLAUDE.md / HANDOFF.md but not enforced at the tool surface. An agent (writer.kind=agent) can in principle call context-init. The documented intent and the code-side enforcement diverge; the in-pi agent fabricates the enforcement claim from the documented intent.

evidence.reference: context-init tool registration has no writer.kind check; ctx is consumed only for non-attestation purposes
author-agent-spec enforces writer.kind === 'human' and throws on agent/monitor/workflow — the canonical enforcement pattern context-init claims to follow but doesn't
Install ceremony section describes /context init as a human ceremony but does not call out that this is convention-only, not code-enforced

gaps.impact: In-pi agent (and likely operator-facing docs elsewhere) repeats the 'human-only by design' claim as if it were a framework property. Reality: agents can write the bootstrap pointer. This is a substrate-vs-runtime drift case identical to the FEAT-004-vs-pi-agent-dispatch-built drift surfaced earlier in the same session — documented narrative ≠ code enforcement. Either gives operators false confidence (if they treat the documented invariant as enforced) or makes the docs lie.
gaps.proposed_resolution:
Resolve the divergence (one or the other; user decides direction). Either: (a) ADD writer.kind === 'human' gate to context-init tool execute (matching the author-agent-spec / author-tool-grant precedent — they are the canonical enforcement template) AND propagate the same gate to context-accept-all and context-install which share the bootstrap ceremony; OR (b) REMOVE 'human-only by design' phrasing from all documentation (CLAUDE.md install-ceremony section, skill-narrative.md, HANDOFF.md, any in-pi-agent-facing narrative) and acknowledge it as operator-convention with no tool-side guard.

CLOSED: closed as side-effect of FGAP-133/134 arc (branch fgap-133-134-auth-gate, commit e6a1799). context-init is in the FGAP-134 AUTH_REQUIRED_TOOLS Bucket-2 surface; the pi.on('tool_call') auth-gate handler intercepts context-init invocations at the pi-dispatch boundary regardless of caller-supplied writer.kind value. The divergence between documented intent (human-only) and code enforcement (any caller) is resolved at the dispatch layer rather than by adding a per-tool writer.kind branch to context-init's execute() body. VER-060 attests (one VER closes all three FGAPs in the arc).

gaps.title: conventions block_kind in samples/conception.json has no prefix field — structurally inconsistent with the other 15 block_kinds and likely causes downstream enumerators to filter-and-drop it
gaps.description: Verified 2026-05-28 via direct read of packages/pi-context/samples/conception.json: of 16 block_kinds, 15 declare a `prefix` field (TASK-, DEC-, FEAT-, FGAP-, REQ-, STORY-, R-, REVIEW-, VER-, RAT-, PHASE-, CTX-, WO-, PLAN-, issue-) and 1 — `conventions` — does NOT. Surfaced empirically by an in-pi agent's enumeration response: agent claimed to query read-samples-catalog per-kind and returned 14 of 16 block_kinds; conventions was one of the two omitted. Most likely cause: agent's enumeration pipeline (or read-samples-catalog response shape) groups/filters kinds by structural property such as 'has prefix' and silently drops kinds lacking that property. Whether the canonical-id-without-prefix is intentional (conventions items addressed by some other naming pattern) or an oversight is not declared anywhere.

evidence.reference: conventions block_kind entry: keys = [canonical_id, display_name, schema_path, array_key, data_path] — NO prefix field; sibling entries (e.g. layer-plans, work-orders) all carry prefix
block_kinds[] schema declares prefix as optional vs required (whichever it is, the conception+schema combination permits the inconsistency)

gaps.impact: Downstream consumers (in-pi agents enumerating, validators iterating, ID-pattern resolvers, prefix-vs-block invariant in buildIdIndex) all have to handle the missing-prefix case as a special case. Empirically observed: an enumeration agent dropped conventions from its 14/16 response. If conventions items are intended to carry typed IDs (CONV-? CVT-?) the absence is a canon-completeness gap. If conventions items are intended to be prefix-less (named by canonical_id alone or by content-derived slug), the absence is unstated convention that needs declaring.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Resolve the conception inconsistency. Either: (a) ADD a canonical prefix to conventions in samples/conception.json (e.g. CONV- or VOC-) and propagate to its schema's id-pattern + any sample blocks/ items; OR (b) DECLARE prefix-less-by-design explicitly — annotate the conventions block_kind entry with `prefix: null` plus a schema-side note that items use canonical_id-as-id (no prefix), and update buildIdIndex's prefix-vs-block invariant + read-samples-catalog response shape to surface this kind as 'prefix-less' rather than silently omit. User-decides which direction; both close the structural inconsistency.

gaps.title: issues block_kind prefix is 'issue-' (lowercase) in samples/conception.json — violates DEC-0027 (uppercase canonical IDs); conception itself disagrees with its own enacted decision
gaps.description: Verified 2026-05-28 via direct read of packages/pi-context/samples/conception.json: the `issues` block_kind declares `prefix: "issue-"` (lowercase). DEC-0027 (enacted as part of the theatre-trio resolution earlier this session) mandates uppercase canonical IDs across the substrate vocabulary, with migrate-forward semantics for legacy lowercase. Every other prefixed block_kind in conception follows uppercase (TASK-, DEC-, FEAT-, FGAP-, REQ-, STORY-, R-, REVIEW-, VER-, RAT-, PHASE-, CTX-, WO-, PLAN-). `issues` is the lone non-conformer. The conception ships and is adopted via /context accept-all as a downstream project's config baseline — any downstream substrate following the conception inherits the DEC-0027 violation at install time.

evidence.reference: issues entry: prefix='issue-' (lowercase) — contrast with all 14 other prefixed kinds which are uppercase
DEC-0027 enacted — canonical IDs uppercase; legacy lowercase migrate forward

gaps.impact: conception ships its own DEC-violation. Downstream consumers running /context accept-all adopt non-canonical vocabulary. Migration tooling and validators that check DEC-0027 conformance will flag adopting projects as non-compliant by virtue of using the canonical-shipped conception. In-pi agents enumerating canonical_ids from conception report 'issue-' verbatim (as observed in P-C1 telemetry response 2026-05-28) — propagating the violation to operator-facing surface.
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: DEC-0027 uppercase canonical IDs; conception-as-canon-baseline; canonical-self-consistency
gaps.proposed_resolution: Migrate the issues prefix in samples/conception.json from 'issue-' to 'ISSUE-' per DEC-0027. Update the issues schema's id-pattern (^ISSUE-\d+$ or equivalent); update any shipped samples/blocks/issues.json items; record a migration entry per the migrate-forward semantics DEC-0027 already prescribes (legacy 'issue-NNNN' resolves to 'ISSUE-NNNN' for read; new items written uppercase). Annotate the conception change as the canon-source-of-truth migration that downstream /context accept-all picks up automatically on the next conception consumption.

gaps.title: Citation rot at install time — bundled pi-context samples/schemas carry self-referential canonical_id citations (FGAP-NNN / DEC-NNNN / FEAT-NNN / TASK-NNN) that reference this repo's development history; downstream /context accept-all consumers inherit unresolvable references in operator-facing description text
gaps.description: Surfaced empirically 2026-05-28: in-pi agent launched against a foreign target dir (clock-menu-app, no substrate) cited 'NEVER LLM self-report per FGAP-102 + DEC-0047 clause 5' in its FEAT-006 walkthrough. Investigation traced the citation to bundled schema field-description text in packages/pi-context/samples/schemas/work-orders.schema.json (and likely siblings): the agent read read-samples-catalog --kind=work-orders, which returned package-intrinsic descriptions containing pi-project-workflows-substrate canonical_ids verbatim. The agent parroted IDs that are MEANINGFUL ONLY in this repo's .project/ substrate and that no downstream consumer will ever resolve locally. The shipped conception artifacts mix framework canon (vocabulary + schemas + invariants — legitimately shipped) with this repo's own development-substrate state (FGAP / DEC / FEAT / TASK canonical_ids — illegitimately leaked into operator-facing descriptions). Concrete instances surfaced: work-orders schema status field cites DEC-0018; real_check_criteria field cites FGAP-102 + DEC-0047 clause 5 + DEC-0018; id field cites DEC-0026/0027; scope field cites FEAT-005 / TASK-089; target_agent field references 'the agents tier' (less concrete but same pattern). Likely-affected: other sample schemas + sample blocks + conception.json description strings (full audit needed).

evidence.reference: field descriptions contain 'per DEC-0018', 'per FGAP-102 + DEC-0047 clause 5', 'per DEC-0026/0027 enacted', 'FEAT-005 / TASK-089' — pi-project-workflows-internal canonical_ids
candidate site for additional citation rot in block_kind description fields, relation_type descriptions, etc. (full audit pending)
all 16 shipped schemas are candidates for the same audit

gaps.impact: Two distinct downstream failure modes. (1) Citation rot at install time: downstream operator running /context accept-all + /context install materializes schemas containing references to FGAP-102 / DEC-0047 / etc., none of which exist in their .project/ or .context/ — references are opaque, unresolvable by resolve-item-by-id, unverifiable by the operator. (2) Apparent-confidence-from-bundled-text: in-pi agent reading bundled descriptions appears to cite project-deep canonical_ids; operator cannot tell that the citations are sourced from package-intrinsic strings (vs from the operator's own substrate) and therefore cannot tell that they reference foreign canon. Compounds with FGAP-124 (SKILL.md doesn't anchor canonical vocabulary): together, agents present unsourced authoritative-sounding citations from bundled text without operator-visible provenance.
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: framework-canon vs project-substrate separation; conception-as-shipped-artifact vs development-history-as-internal-state; portable-canonical-artifacts
gaps.proposed_resolution: Audit all shipped pi-context artifacts (samples/conception.json + samples/schemas/*.json + samples/blocks/*.json) for embedded references to this repo's substrate canonical_ids (regex: FGAP-\d{3}|DEC-\d{4}|FEAT-\d{3}|TASK-\d{3}|VER-\d{3}|R-\d+|REQ-\d+|REVIEW-\d+|RAT-\d+|PHASE-\d+|CTX-\d+|WO-\d+|STORY-\d+|PLAN-\d+). For each hit, rewrite the description string to use abstract framework-level language that self-describes without citing originating canonical_ids. Example: 'NEVER LLM self-report per FGAP-102 + DEC-0047 clause 5' → 'NEVER LLM self-report; verification verdict comes from deterministic real-checks the agent cannot fake'. 'DEC-0026/0027 enacted' on id fields → 'uppercase canonical_id, 3-digit zero-padded suffix'. 'FEAT-005 / TASK-089' on scope field → 'the capability composer uses scope to clamp the agent's grant at dispatch'. The framework canon survives; the development-history reference is stripped. Add a lint / test to packages/pi-context that fails CI if any shipped sample artifact contains pi-project-workflows canonical_id patterns — prevents regression. [CLOSED 2026-05-28 via FGAP-130 resolution arc + VER-058 attestation. Citations stripped from 8 schemas + 2 src files; regression test in samples-catalog.test.ts gates against reintroduction; SKILL.md regen clean.]

gaps.title: Sibling-package citation rot un-audited and ungated — pi-workflows + pi-behavior-monitors + pi-agent-dispatch + pi-jit-agents shipped artifacts (skill-narrative.md / SKILL.md / bundled agents / schemas / docs / src description strings) carry unknown count of pi-project-workflows substrate canonical_id citations that downstream consumers cannot resolve; FGAP-130 closure explicitly scoped to pi-context only
gaps.description: FGAP-130 resolution (merge 9b65e4c 2026-05-28) stripped citation rot from packages/pi-context/* and added a samples-catalog.test.ts regression gate scoped to pi-context's samples/ + src/. By explicit plan scope (in approved plan + in FGAP-130 body), sibling packages were OUT OF SCOPE. They remain unaudited and ungated. Same root cause exists in their shipped surfaces if present: skill-narrative.md files regenerated into SKILL.md (operator-facing); bundled .agent.yaml specs (operator-facing prompt text + descriptions); bundled .workflow.yaml specs (likewise); bundled .monitor.json specs (likewise); src/*.ts files with description strings on pi.registerTool calls and parameter descriptions that surface to operators via SKILL.md generation; README.md files inside each package (if shipped via files[]); JSDoc comments that get pulled into description strings via doc-generation scripts. Empirically: the FGAP-130 adversarial probe CHECK 11 already flagged one known sibling-package leak — packages/pi-project-workflows/skills/pi-project-workflows/SKILL.md line 41 contains a citation sourced from pi-agent-dispatch/skill-narrative.md. Unknown how many more across 4 packages. Downstream consumers running `pi install -l <META>` followed by reading any sibling-package SKILL.md or bundled-spec description will encounter the same unresolvable references.

evidence.reference: FGAP-130 adversarial probe CHECK 11 (2026-05-28) identified one citation here that leaks to packages/pi-project-workflows/skills/pi-project-workflows/SKILL.md line 41 after meta-skills regeneration
shipped artifacts unaudited — bundled .workflow.yaml + .agent.yaml + skill-narrative.md + src/*.ts description strings all candidates
shipped artifacts unaudited — bundled .monitor.json + bundled .agent.yaml classifier specs + Nunjucks prompt templates + skill-narrative.md + src/*.ts all candidates
library (not extension) but ships docs/planning/jit-agents-spec.md + README.md + src/*.ts JSDoc — unaudited

gaps.impact: FGAP-130's resolution is partial: in-pi agent launched against any target dir that loads any extension other than pi-context can still cite unresolvable canonical_ids sourced from sibling-package shipped surfaces. The FGAP-130 regression test gates only pi-context; the same regex pattern violated in a sibling package's shipped artifact escapes detection. Downstream consumers experience the same citation-rot failure mode FGAP-130 catalogued but via a different package. The 'paramount canonical pipeline' completion-claim from FGAP-130 has a known-gap; closing FGAP-131 closes the actual user-facing problem completely.
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: framework-canon vs project-substrate separation; portable-canonical-artifacts; cross-package regression-gate uniformity
gaps.proposed_resolution: CLOSED: implemented via FGAP-131/132 arc on branch fgap-131-132-rigorous-citation-gate (10 commits). Rigorous AST + JSON + markdown/YAML scanner introduced in packages/pi-context/src/citation-rot-scanner.ts replaced the FGAP-130 naive line-by-line scan. All 5 sibling packages stripped of operator-facing canonical_id citations (pi-workflows + pi-behavior-monitors + pi-agent-dispatch + pi-jit-agents + pi-project-workflows META). SKILL.md regen via npm run skills produced clean outputs. Single workspace-wide regression gate in samples-catalog.test.ts asserts 0 hits across all packages — runs in husky pre-commit + CI. Attested by VER-059. Original proposed_resolution preserved below for archaeology: Audit every shipped artifact in packages/pi-workflows + packages/pi-behavior-monitors + packages/pi-agent-dispatch + packages/pi-jit-agents for the canonical_id regex pattern from FGAP-130 (the original text — full audit + Pattern A/B/C/D rewrite + per-package gate).

gaps.title: FGAP-130 citation-rot regression test is naive-text-scan-based with heuristic exclusions deferring rigor — mandate-007 violation in plan text 'is sufficient — pick complexity later if false-positives surface'; AST-based traversal foreclosing both false-positive and false-negative classes never built
gaps.description: FGAP-130 plan step 3 + implementation landed a regression test in packages/pi-context/src/samples-catalog.test.ts that: (a) does naive recursive JSON walk for samples/**/*.json — every string value; (b) does line-by-line raw text scan of src/index.ts + src/orientation.ts; (c) uses heuristic exclusions ('property named id at item level' for JSON, 'comment-only line OR line containing both e.g. and NNN' for source).
