The plan text explicitly deferred rigor with the phrase 'pick complexity later only if false-positives surface' — a mandate-007 anti-pattern (don't favor deferring discovered issues; if a known-fragility-possibility exists, surface and foreclose it now). Specific known fragility classes the naive scan misses or risks: (1) MULTI-LINE STRINGS in TypeScript — template literals or split-across-newlines string concatenation where a canonical_id straddles the line break escapes the line-by-line scan; (2) COMMENT-EXCLUSION FALSE NEGATIVE — line with code + trailing inline comment containing a citation (`const x = 'y'; // see TASK-088`) is NOT excluded but the citation is dev-internal; not currently a leak path but the exclusion logic isn't doing what its phrasing implies; (3) e.g.+NNN HEURISTIC FALSE POSITIVE — a legitimate sentence containing 'e.g.' and the digits 'NNN' in unrelated contexts collides with the exclusion; conversely, a real citation in a sentence that happens to contain both substrings escapes detection; (4) JSON enum-value FALSE POSITIVE — a schema enum like ['FGAP-001', 'FGAP-002'] would trip the test even though enums of canonical_ids may be legitimate substrate-vocabulary declarations (none ship today but could land); (5) STRUCTURAL ID EXCLUSION is path-suffix-based ('samples/blocks/*.json') and key-name-based ('property named id') — does not distinguish item-level id from nested id (e.g. an object {someField: {id: 'TASK-001'}} nested in a description-context); (6) JSDoc-comment dev-internal IDs in TypeScript are not scanned at all (current scope) but the line-by-line exclusion is unaware whether a string literal IS in JSDoc context vs in a tool-description string — if a JSDoc-style block contains a string literal with a citation, it's still in source text and the test may flag or miss inconsistently. The AST-based alternative: parse TS via tsc/typescript compiler API, identify all string-literal nodes inside pi.registerTool({description: ...}) calls + parameter description fields specifically, and scan only those — eliminates JSDoc + comment confusion entirely. Parse JSON via deep visitor with full JSONPath tracking + carve out exact paths (e.g. /properties/id, /enum at registry-block-paths) — eliminates heuristic exclusion. The plan acknowledged the AST option as the rigor-foreclosing path but chose naive-now-rigorous-later — exactly the mandate-007 deferral.

evidence.reference: 'Naive recursive JSON walk + regex per Implementation step 3 is sufficient — pick complexity later only if false-positives surface' — verbatim mandate-007 deferral; the plan was approved and the test built per the naive form
the newly-landed citation-rot regression test (commit 4fd28a6) implements the naive form documented in the plan

gaps.impact: Two failure-mode classes unresolved by the naive gate. False-positive class: future legitimate canonical-id-vocabulary changes (e.g. shipping a registry of canonical_id form examples; expanding ID-format-illustration coverage) will trip the test and force either test-disablement OR ad-hoc exclusion accretion — both negative outcomes. False-negative class: citations that escape via multi-line strings or unusual formatting silently land in shipped artifacts post-FGAP-130 closure without the gate catching them; the gate's apparent green becomes a false-confidence signal. Both classes mean the FGAP-130 closure is not as robust as the VER-058 attestation implies. Combined with FGAP-131 (sibling-package scope gap), the overall citation-rot-foreclosure story is weaker than the merge message claims.
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: AST-based string-literal extraction; rigorous-gate vs heuristic-gate; mandate-007 deferred-fragility-foreclosure
gaps.proposed_resolution: CLOSED: implemented via FGAP-131/132 arc on branch fgap-131-132-rigorous-citation-gate (10 commits). The naive line-by-line CITATION_RE scan from FGAP-130 (commit 4fd28a6) has been deleted and replaced by packages/pi-context/src/citation-rot-scanner.ts — a rigorous AST + JSON + markdown/YAML body scanner. The six fragility classes the FGAP-132 body enumerated are each foreclosed: (1) multi-line strings — full-text scan with newline-counting line attribution catches multi-line straddle; (2) JSDoc / comment-exclusion ambiguity — TypeScript compiler API SyntaxKind discrimination ensures comment trivia is never visited by ts.forEachChild over CallExpression argument subtrees; (3) e.g.+NNN heuristic — heuristic removed entirely; format-illustration NNN-placeholders applied uniformly per Pattern C across all packages; (4) schema enum false-positive — JSON walker carves out enum string values inside *.schema.json explicitly; (5) path-suffix coarseness — JSONPath-aware carve-out narrows to top-level id under samples/blocks + registry/blocks + defaults/blocks + .project rather than whole-file suffix-exclusion; (6) JSDoc-vs-tool-description ambiguity — only string literals reachable through registerTool / Type.X CallExpression subtrees are flagged. 13/13 scanner unit tests pass including synthetic-injection probes per surface type. Attested by VER-059.

gaps.title: Runtime error-message string-literals escape FGAP-131/132 citation-rot scanner — scanner extracts string literals at Type.String/Object description fields and at pi.registerTool description argument but does NOT extract string-literal arguments to throw new Error(...) / new <ErrorClass>(...) constructions in tool execute() bodies; canonical_id citations in error messages reach operators verbatim ungated
gaps.description: Surfaced empirically 2026-05-29 by in-pi agent telemetry: agent attempted author-agent-spec with writer.kind=agent; pi-agent-dispatch's tool execute() body threw an error whose message included verbatim 'DEC-0047: Capability/spec authoring is human-only; sub-agents have no escalation path.' Downstream operator reading that error in a fresh-substrate target dir cannot resolve DEC-0047 locally — same citation-rot failure mode FGAP-130/131/132 closed for description text, surviving via a different AST surface the scanner does not visit. Verified scope: packages/pi-context/src/citation-rot-scanner.ts walks ts.CallExpression nodes filtering on PropertyAccessExpression pi.registerTool and Type.String/Type.Object call patterns; it does NOT visit NewExpression nodes (e.g. new Error('...')) or ThrowStatement argument string-literals. Each pi-agent-dispatch tool throws structured errors with canonical_id citations: AuthorAgentSpecRefusedError (DEC-0047), CommitAttestedRefusedError (DEC-0047), AuthorToolGrantRefusedError (DEC-0047), composeToolGrant failures, plus pi-context's own BootstrapNotFoundError (DEC-0015). Full citation count in error-message strings across all 5 packages unknown until the scanner is extended + re-run.

evidence.reference: AST walker visits Type.String/Object description call-argument string-literals + pi.registerTool description-field string-literals; does not visit NewExpression / ThrowStatement / AwaitExpression argument string-literals
throws new AuthorAgentSpecRefusedError with citation-rich message ('Capability/spec authoring is human-only per DEC-0047...')
throws CommitAttestedRefusedError with DEC-0047 citation
throws AuthorToolGrantRefusedError with DEC-0047 citation

gaps.impact: Citations in runtime error messages reach operators unfiltered. The FGAP-131/132 scanner reports zero hits while the leak persists via a parallel AST surface. The FGAP-130/131/132 closure narrative is therefore partial: operator-visible canonical_id leakage continues through every error-throw path in tool execute() bodies. Worse: error-message citations are MORE visible to operators than description-field citations because errors fire at the moment of failure — operators read them directly to diagnose what went wrong, where the foreign canonical_id is most likely to be parroted and copy-pasted into bug reports / questions.
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: AST-surface-coverage completeness; error-message-string-literal extraction; parallel-ungated-path closure
gaps.proposed_resolution:
Extend packages/pi-context/src/citation-rot-scanner.ts AST walker to ALSO visit: (1) NewExpression nodes where the constructor identifier ends in 'Error' (Error / TypeError / RangeError / project-specific *RefusedError / *NotFoundError / etc.) — extract their first string-literal argument; (2) ThrowStatement argument string-literals (cases where throw uses a string directly or a constructor whose first arg is a template literal). Apply the same canonical_id regex to extracted string-literals. Re-run scanner across all 5 packages; strip every hit per Pattern A — rewrite error messages to convey the same operational meaning without naming substrate canonical_ids (e.g. 'Capability/spec authoring is human-only per DEC-0047' → 'Capability/spec authoring requires writer.kind=human'). Verify by extending citation-rot-scanner.test.ts with a synthetic-injection test for the new surface type. Closes citation-rot at the runtime-error path; combined with existing scanner coverage, no parallel ungated string-literal path remains in shipped operator-visible TypeScript surfaces.

CLOSED: implemented via FGAP-133/134 arc on branch fgap-133-134-auth-gate (commit 56113cb). citation-rot scanner extended in packages/pi-context/src/citation-rot-scanner.ts with isErrorConstructorCall predicate + NewExpression visitor branch; new ScanResult.surface variant ast-error-message; the one extant DEC-0047 citation at packages/pi-agent-dispatch/src/attested-commit.ts:48 rewritten per Pattern A to 'human-only authoring enforced'. Scanner unit-test count 16/0 (was 13/0). Driver run across all 5 packages' src + skills surfaces returns 0 hits post-strip. VER-060 attests.

gaps.title: writer.kind enforcement on author-agent-spec / author-tool-grant / commit-attested is field-validation not identity-verification — caller-supplied writer.kind='human' is accepted at face value; in-pi agent demonstrated spoof 2026-05-29 (capability-widening / git-write tools bypassable by setting writer.kind='human' in tool-call args); the entire DEC-0047 governance assumption (capability widening is human-only; orchestrator cannot escalate its own grant) is undercut
gaps.description:
Empirical demonstration 2026-05-29: in-pi agent launched via scripts/launch-constrained-pi.sh against foreign target dir attempted author-agent-spec with writer.kind='agent' — rejected per DEC-0047 (working). Same agent then spoofed writer.kind='human' in its tool args — gate accepted the field value at face value and proceeded to substrate-write attempt (failed only because the foreign target had no substrate pointer; on a properly-bootstrapped target the spoofed write would have succeeded). Root cause: author-agent-spec / author-tool-grant / commit-attested all gate at execute()-body entry on a caller-supplied writer.kind string field; nothing in pi's tool-call interface verifies the caller's actual identity. The gate is theatrical defense against compliant agents, no defense against motivated or confused ones. Pi-coding-agent does expose a tamper-proof per-tool pre-execute gate via pi.on('tool_call', handler) returning {block: true, reason} + ctx.ui.confirm() (verified 2026-05-29 by Explore investigation of node_modules/@earendil-works/pi-coding-agent/dist/core/extensions/types.d.ts:605-611 + docs/extensions.md:69-73). The gate fires BEFORE the tool's execute() body, so the agent's args (including spoofed writer.kind) cannot escape it. CAVEAT: ctx.ui.confirm() auto-succeeds in non-interactive modes (pi --mode json / RPC / print) — the handler must explicitly refuse non-interactive contexts for sensitive tools to prevent JSON-mode bypass.

Filed with concrete 3-bucket tool-tier conception (settled 2026-05-29 with user) baked into proposed_resolution so the implementation arc has canonical decisions in substrate not just conversation memory. BUCKET 1 (default-grant, no auth): all read tools + substrate-item writes (append/update/remove-block-item + nested + append-relation) + call-agent + run-real-checks + run-work-order-loop + complete-task + gather-execution-context + render-item-by-id + enforce-budget + list-tools + workflow-* reads + monitors-* reads. BUCKET 2 (auth-required via tool_call handler + ctx.ui.confirm): author-agent-spec + author-tool-grant + write-schema + amend-config + write-block + commit-attested + rename-canonical-id + workflow-execute + workflow-resume + workflow-init + monitors-control + monitors-rules (write mode) + context-init + context-accept-all. BUCKET 3 (off-allowlist; already-forbidden per FEAT-010 5-layer): bash + write + edit + shell + execute.

evidence.reference: gates on params.writer.kind === 'human' read directly from caller-supplied tool-call args; no identity verification
same pattern
writer.kind=agent enforcement is field-validation; same spoof surface
ToolCallEventResult { block?: boolean; reason?: string } — the pre-execute gate API
pi.on('tool_call', async (event, ctx) => ...) — pattern docs include ctx.ui.confirm usage

gaps.impact: P0 security finding. Every Bucket-2 tool (13 tools listed in description) is currently bypassable by an in-pi agent setting writer.kind='human' in its own tool-call args. The orchestrator we launch via scripts/launch-constrained-pi.sh can therefore (a) author new privileged sub-agent specs with arbitrary capability declarations, (b) widen its OWN capability grants via author-tool-grant, (c) commit arbitrary files to git under fabricated agent_id attestation. The FEAT-006 north-star governance story (orchestrator-cannot-widen-its-own-grant; human-OK gate at iteration boundary) is undercut at the foundation. FGAP-130/131/132 work to close citation rot is necessary but orthogonal — they close the citation problem, not the authority problem.
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: identity-verification vs field-validation; pre-execute pi-dispatch gate vs in-execute body gate; tool_call event interception; defense-in-depth at the trust boundary
gaps.proposed_resolution:
Register a single pi.on('tool_call', handler) in pi-agent-dispatch's extension factory. Handler filters event.toolName against a const Bucket-2 list (the 13 tools enumerated in description). For each match: (a) if !ctx.ui.isInteractive return {block: true, reason: 'tool <name> requires interactive user-confirm; current context is non-interactive'} — closes JSON-mode bypass; (b) if interactive, call await ctx.ui.confirm(<tool-name + args-summary>); on false return {block: true, reason: 'user declined'}. One handler, one file (packages/pi-agent-dispatch/src/auth-gate.ts), wired into the extension factory at packages/pi-agent-dispatch/src/index.ts. Unit tests assert (1) Bucket-2 tools require confirm; (2) Bucket-1 tools do not invoke the handler's confirm path; (3) non-interactive context returns block:true with refusal reason; (4) user-decline returns block:true; (5) user-approve allows tool to proceed. Side-effect: closes FGAP-126 simultaneously (context-init becomes truly human-only at pi-dispatch layer, not just documented-as-human-only). Once landed, the writer.kind field can be derived from the verified interaction (set programmatically inside the handler based on ctx.user) rather than trusted from caller args — second iteration cleanup; first iteration is the block-gate alone.

CLOSED: implemented via FGAP-133/134 arc on branch fgap-133-134-auth-gate (commits 7be299b + e6a1799). The writer.kind spoof is now closed at the pi-dispatch layer rather than at each tool's execute() body: packages/pi-agent-dispatch/src/auth-gate.ts registers a pi.on('tool_call') handler from the extension factory; AUTH_REQUIRED_TOOLS pins the 14 Bucket-2 sensitive tools verbatim (membership-pinning test guards against drift); non-interactive contexts (ctx.hasUI=false) are refused unconditionally with a structured reason; interactive contexts invoke ctx.ui.confirm with a sanitized arg summary. 8 new unit-test cases cover the 5 branch outcomes + the message-rendering surface. Writer.kind field becomes derived-from-verified-interaction rather than caller-trusted; a future cleanup iteration may delete the field-validation branches in author-agent-spec / author-tool-grant / commit-attested entirely. VER-060 attests. NOTE: the launch-script behavioral demo (operator types 'no' to a synthetic spoof attempt) + the fresh-context adversarial probe are deferred to orchestrator follow-on per plan Phase 3.

gaps.title: pi's built-in `read` tool truncates at 2000 lines / 50KB hardcoded + appends a trivially-skimmable text marker at end of content; structured `details.truncation` IS present but unused at the LLM-facing surface; in-pi agents blithely proceed as if content is complete after partial read — no extension-side gate detects truncation + replaces output with a hard-refusal directive analogous to pi-context's existing overCapDirective canon
gaps.description: Surfaced 2026-05-29 after commit af682c4 granted pi's built-in `read,ls,grep,find` to the constrained orchestrator (Bucket-1 default-grant per user directive). Recon of `node_modules/@earendil-works/pi-coding-agent/dist/core/tools/read.js` (lines 143-272) + `truncate.d.ts` confirms: pi's read tool applies `truncateHead()` with hardcoded DEFAULT_MAX_LINES=2000 OR DEFAULT_MAX_BYTES=50*1024, keeps file HEAD, drops tail. Truncation signal: a single text marker appended at END of content (`[Truncated: showing N of M lines (2000 line limit)]` OR `[Truncated: N lines shown (50KB limit)]`). This is structurally weak — end-of-content position is trivially skimmable by an LLM scanning content for the substantive bit it wanted to find. Structured `details.truncation` object IS present in the tool result (`{ truncated: boolean, truncatedBy, totalLines, totalBytes, outputLines, outputBytes }`) but never exposed to the LLM — only the marker text is. Result: in-pi agents reading large files routinely proceed as if they have full content + reach wrong conclusions / miss relevant content past the truncation boundary. The harness's own typed-read canon does the right thing here (pi-context's `serializeForRead` + `overCapDirective` emits hard-refusal text `⚠️ READ REFUSED — ... Nothing was returned (a partial read would mislead). Narrow your read: call <tool> with <params>` and returns NO body) — pi's built-in read lags this canon. We cannot modify canonical pi but pi exposes `pi.on('tool_result', handler)` (verified at `node_modules/@earendil-works/pi-coding-agent/dist/core/extensions/types.d.ts:629-669,726-730`) which fires AFTER tool execution + BEFORE the LLM sees the result + returns `ToolResultEventResult { content?, details?, isError? }` allowing the handler to replace the content entirely. The extension-side gate would detect truncation via the structured details field + replace content with a directive mirroring pi-context's overCapDirective phrasing canon.

evidence.reference: truncation invocation at line 227 (`truncateHead(selectedContent)`); end-of-content marker construction at lines 120-129; constants in `truncate.js` (DEFAULT_MAX_LINES=2000, DEFAULT_MAX_BYTES=50*1024); no env-var override available
TruncationResult shape exposes the full truncation metadata that pi.on('tool_result') handlers can inspect

ToolResultEvent (incl ReadToolResultEvent with `details: ReadToolDetails | undefined` carrying `{ truncation?: TruncationResult }`); ToolResultEventResult shape `{ content?, details?, isError? }`; pi.on('tool_result', handler) registration
serializeForRead + READ_ELEMENT_FOOTER_PREFIX + ReadEnvelope + the canonical overCapDirective phrasing pattern we already use across read-block-page / read-schema / read-samples-catalog
structural precedent — single-handler tool-call gate in pi-agent-dispatch's extension factory; same pattern shape for tool-result gate

gaps.impact: In-pi agents granted the read tool (per af682c4) routinely reach incorrect conclusions when reading files >2000 lines or >50KB because they treat truncated content as complete. Compounds with FGAP-124 (`SKILL.md doesn't anchor canonical vocabulary; agent enumerates from priors`) — agent reads a file head + extrapolates the rest from priors rather than acknowledging the missing content. Bigger blast radius post-af682c4 because the agent now reads ARBITRARY files, not only substrate-typed reads which have proper overCapDirective protection. The capability we just granted has a known-weak guardrail upstream + our harness has the canon to repair it without upstream changes.
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: tool_result event interception; hard-refusal directive vs end-of-content marker; serializeForRead overCapDirective phrasing canon; fail-closed vs fail-soft truncation semantics
gaps.proposed_resolution: Register a single `pi.on('tool_result', handler)` in pi-agent-dispatch's extension factory (alongside the existing FGAP-134 auth-gate which uses tool_call — orthogonal events; no coexistence concern). New file `packages/pi-agent-dispatch/src/read-truncation-gate.ts` exports `registerReadTruncationGate(pi: ExtensionAPI): void`. Handler: filter on `event.toolName === 'read'`; if `!event.details?.truncation?.truncated` return void (pass through). Else replace `event.content` with a single text content item carrying a structured directive following pi-context's canonical phrasing: "⚠️ READ TRUNCATED — file <path> is <totalLines> lines / <totalBytes> bytes; only the first <outputLines> lines / <outputBytes> bytes were returned. The content is INCOMPLETE. Do NOT proceed as if you have the full file. To continue from where the read stopped: call `read` again with `offset=<outputLines>` (and `limit=...` if desired). To find specific content without reading the rest of the file: use `grep` with a pattern + path. The truncated head is NOT returned — re-issue the read with `offset` or use `grep`." Return shape `{ content: [{ type: 'text', text: <directive> }], isError: false }`. Hard refusal — no head content returned — mirrors pi-context overCapDirective pattern + forces the agent to either narrow via grep or paginate via offset. Wire into pi-agent-dispatch's extension factory at `packages/pi-agent-dispatch/src/index.ts` (one-line call after `registerAuthGate(pi)`). Unit tests at `packages/pi-agent-dispatch/src/read-truncation-gate.test.ts` covering: (a) truncated read replaced with directive; (b) non-truncated read passes through (handler returns void); (c) non-read tools pass through; (d) directive contains canonical phrases ('TRUNCATED', 'INCOMPLETE', 'Do NOT proceed', 'offset=', 'grep'); (e) directive includes exact line/byte counts from event.details.truncation. Coexists with FGAP-134's tool_call auth-gate (orthogonal events). No change to canonical pi. [CLOSED 2026-05-29 via FGAP-135 arc — branch fgap-135-read-truncation-gate, 3 commits 38ad48e..0703bc5, VER-064 attests. read-truncation-gate registers pi.on('tool_result') in pi-agent-dispatch factory; replaces content with hard-refusal directive when details.truncation.truncated; mirrors pi-context overCapDirective canonical phrasing.]

gaps.title: write-schema 'replace' that bumps version has no tool-surface migration path — code-level MigrationFn registration via packages/pi-context/src/schema-migrations.ts is the only mechanism; in-pi LLM customizing schemas mid-project for a downstream substrate cannot evolve a schema's `version` field without out-of-band code edits + recompile + redeploy, defeating the in-pi customization promise the 11-config-registry surface otherwise affords
gaps.description: Surfaced 2026-05-29 by an in-pi LLM working in the clock-menu-app substrate (per `/Users/david/Projects/Pi-Context-May 29 2026 LLM Perspective.md`, the section enumerating 11 customization surfaces). Verified by direct read of `packages/pi-context/src/schema-write.ts:139-144` (writeSchemaChecked docstring) + the `write-schema` tool description at `packages/pi-context/src/index.ts` (caveat verbatim: 'a replace that changes the schema's version does NOT migrate existing block items — read-time validateBlockWithMigration throws a version mismatch until a code-level MigrationFn is registered (no tool surface for that)'). The MigrationFn registry mechanism at `packages/pi-context/src/schema-migrations.ts` exposes `MigrationFn = (data: unknown) => unknown` + `MigrationRegistry { register, resolve }` populated by import-time `register()` calls — pure in-memory; no on-disk persistence; no tool surface. Asymmetric capability surface: `create` (no prior version) works cleanly via tools; `replace` works for non-version-bumping edits (field-shape tweaks that keep the same `version` value); but the moment a schema needs a version bump (the canonical signal for a breaking change requiring data transformation), the in-pi agent is stuck — the only path forward is out-of-band: edit MigrationFn TypeScript in pi-context source + npm run build + redeploy. This defeats the in-pi customization promise that the LLM Perspective doc enumerates across 11 registries. Particularly meaningful for downstream projects (like clock-menu-app) whose domain entities don't match the shipped 16 block kinds — schema iteration is the natural workflow as the operator refines the substrate vocabulary; version bumps will happen.

evidence.reference: writeSchemaChecked docstring: 'forward when a `replace` changes the schema's `version`. A breaking ... throws a version mismatch until a code-level MigrationFn is registered via [the registry]'
write-schema tool description: 'CAVEAT: a replace that changes the schema's version does NOT migrate existing block items ... no tool surface for that'
MigrationFn = (data: unknown) => unknown; MigrationRegistry { register, resolve }; createRegistry() — pure in-memory; no persistence; populated by import-time `register()` calls in source files

in-pi LLM observation 2026-05-29: 'schema `replace` that changes the version number requires a code-level MigrationFn — no tool surface for migration. Plan schemas carefully or version-lock early.' + 'The limitation is not the harness — it's that schema replace with version bumps has no tool-surface migration path. But create from scratch (no prior version) has no such constraint.'

gaps.impact: Downstream projects adopting pi-context cannot iterate their custom schemas through breaking-change version bumps using only the in-pi tool surface. The natural workflow — author schema v1.0.0 → realize a field needs a different shape → bump to v2.0.0 → transform existing items — requires either (a) extending pi-context's TypeScript source out-of-band and rebuilding (which the downstream project may not own), or (b) avoiding version bumps entirely and accreting non-breaking edits (which lies about backwards-incompatibility and breaks the version field's semantic contract), or (c) pretending versions don't matter (which defeats validateBlockWithMigration's purpose). The 11-registry customization surface enumerated in the LLM Perspective doc is incomplete without this; the gap is most acute exactly when the substrate is most malleable (early project customization with empty or sparse block data).
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: MigrationFn registration; substrate-persisted migration spec; tool-surface schema-evolution; version-bump with operator-asserted data shape (no-op migration); declarative transform DSL for cross-version data shape
gaps.proposed_resolution: Primary path (narrow + implementable now): add a `write-schema-migration` Pi tool registered in pi-context that accepts `schemaName + fromVersion + toVersion + kind: "identity" | "declarative-transform"` parameters. For `kind: "identity"`: persist a migration declaration to `<contextDir>/migrations.json` (new substrate-managed file with its own schema) recording an identity (no-op) MigrationFn — the operator explicitly asserts no data transformation needed, only the version field updates. The migration registry initialization path at packages/pi-context/src/schema-migrations.ts is extended to read `<contextDir>/migrations.json` at registry init + register the persisted entries alongside any compile-time-registered MigrationFns. This unblocks the common case (in-progress schema iteration with empty or compatible existing data) entirely. For `kind: "declarative-transform"`: accept a transform spec (JSONPath-based field-rename / default-insert / field-delete operations as a structured DSL) — handler builds the corresponding MigrationFn at registry-init time. Same persistence file. Both kinds are writer.kind=human enforced (same DEC-0047 governance precedent as author-agent-spec / author-tool-grant — schema migrations are capability-widening for substrate semantics). Also passes through FGAP-134 auth-gate (add to AUTH_REQUIRED_TOOLS) since migration registration is a sensitive operation. Re-verifies write-schema tool description caveat text post-resolution (substrate-tool-surface now COVERS the migration registration; caveat updated). Future iteration (separate FGAP if surfaces): richer transform DSL (computed defaults, type coercion, conditional logic) — initial scope is identity + simple structural transforms.

gaps.title: scripts/launch-constrained-pi.sh does not explicitly support --continue (pi's canonical resume-last-session flag); operators invoking the script with --continue get the pre-flight install + composite-discovery + symmetry-verification steps re-run unconditionally on each resume, which is at minimum wasted overhead and at worst interferes with pi's session-resume semantics
gaps.description: Empirically: scripts/launch-constrained-pi.sh parses --grant flags out at lines 38-45 + passes the remaining ARGS through to pi at line 88 (`exec pi --tools "$TOOLS" "${ARGS[@]+"${ARGS[@]}"}"`). So --continue mechanically reaches pi as a regular arg today. BUT the script's pre-flight sequence runs unconditionally regardless of whether the invocation is a fresh-launch or a resume: (a) pre-flight pointer check (lines 33-36), (b) pi install -l <META> (line 47 — writes to target's .pi/), (c) per-target composite discovery via read-config-operations.ts helper (lines 62-78), (d) 4-extension symmetry verification (lines 80-84), (e) SKILL.md grep for --tools allowlist derivation (lines 54-55 + 81 read-grant append). On a resume invocation, steps (b)-(d) are at minimum wasted overhead (the install was done on first launch; the composites + symmetry don't change between fresh-launch and resume); at worst, the install step may interfere with pi's session-resume semantics (untested — pi install may reset / refresh state pi --continue depends on). The script needs explicit --continue handling that either skips or short-circuits the redundant pre-flight steps on resume. User directive 2026-05-29: 'a user can use --continue with our script and just as in canonical pi --continue it continues the last active session.'

evidence.reference: pre-flight sequence runs unconditionally; --continue not detected or special-cased in the ARGS parser at lines 38-45; passes through to pi at line 88 alongside the still-derived --tools allowlist
pi install -l "$META" runs every launch invocation; on --continue this is redundant + potentially state-disruptive
canonical pi --continue behavior: resumes the last active session for the cwd; exact session-state expectations + interaction with --tools allowlist + extension install state not yet recon'd

gaps.impact: Operators wanting to resume their last constrained-pi session experience either (a) the cumulative latency of the pre-flight install + composite-discovery + symmetry-verification on every resume (operationally heavy), or (b) silent failure / unexpected session reset if any pre-flight step interferes with --continue semantics. Either way the operator-promised parity with canonical pi --continue is not met. The script's positive clause (DEC-0014 harness-confined orchestrator) was designed for fresh-launch flows; resume was not a design consideration.
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: session-resume; --continue flag passthrough; idempotent vs side-effecting pre-flight; harness-confined session lifecycle
gaps.proposed_resolution: Recon canonical pi --continue behavior FIRST (single Explore agent over node_modules/@earendil-works/pi-coding-agent/) to verify: (a) what state --continue reads from + writes to; (b) whether pi install -l <META> is idempotent against an already-installed target; (c) whether the --tools allowlist needs to match the resumed-session's allowlist or is re-derivable; (d) whether composite-discovery runs again on resume or relies on cached state. Then update scripts/launch-constrained-pi.sh: detect --continue presence in ARGS (parse alongside --grant); when present + the target's .pi/ already shows a prior install, skip the pi install step + skip composite-discovery + skip symmetry-verification (the prior launch did these; resume inherits the same surface). Pre-flight pointer check still fires (cheap; informational). --tools allowlist still derived + passed (pi may require it on resume regardless). exec pi --continue + other args. Verification: runtime demo invokes the script with --continue against a tmpdir + fake-pi capturing whether install ran (it should NOT) + whether --tools was passed (it should). Documentation refresh in script JSDoc header explaining the --continue special-casing. [CLOSED 2026-05-29 via FGAP-137 arc + VER-062 attestation at commit dcdcae7. Header-only edit + runtime-demo extension; install + --tools allowlist + composite discovery + symmetry verification all unchanged per user direction. --continue / -c now first-class documented passthrough flags; mechanical correctness was already in place.]

gaps.title: Post-FGAP-134 canonical reframing: in-body writer.kind=='human' checks are redundant with the pi-dispatch auth-gate that is now the canonical identity check; 'human-only' framing in docstrings + error messages + test narratives is leftover from the pre-FGAP-134 spoofable model + misleads agents into refusing to issue calls the canonical model expects them to issue (under user authorization); identity-stamping is conflated with auth-verification — writer.user in caller-supplied args becomes the substrate attestation stamp but the auth-gate verifies SOMEONE confirmed at the terminal without verifying the supplied identity matches; surfaced 2026-05-29 by an in-pi agent's telemetry claiming 'a human must issue the write-schema-migration call' (false — agent issues call, user authorizes via auth-gate prompt)
gaps.description: Three coupled gaps revealed by in-pi agent telemetry 2026-05-29 + verified by reading the canonical pi-dispatch auth-gate (FGAP-134) + the affected tool bodies. (1) IN-BODY WRITER.KIND CHECKS ARE REDUNDANT: author-agent-spec, author-tool-grant, commit-attested, write-schema-migration each carry an in-execute-body 'if writer.kind !== human: throw' check. Pre-FGAP-134 these were the actual gate (spoofable, hence FGAP-134). Post-FGAP-134 the auth-gate at pi-dispatch is the canonical identity check (operator confirms via ctx.ui.confirm before tool body runs). The in-body check now exists only as defense-in-depth + backward-compat with existing tests, but its docstrings + error messages still describe it as the primary 'human-only enforcement.' The other 11 Bucket-2 tools (write-schema, amend-config, write-block, rename-canonical-id, context-init, context-accept-all, workflow-execute, workflow-resume, workflow-init, monitors-control, monitors-rules) are gated ONLY by auth-gate without an in-body check — inconsistent model across Bucket-2. (2) FRAMING IS MISLEADING: 'human-only authoring enforced' in error messages + 'capability authoring is human-only; sub-agents have no escalation path' in author-spec refusals + similar in docstrings + test narratives implies the agent is AGENT-BLOCKED from even attempting the call. Canonical reality is AGENT-ISSUES-CALL + USER-AUTHORIZES-VIA-AUTH-GATE-PROMPT. The misleading framing observably caused the in-pi agent on 2026-05-29 to incorrectly claim 'I can propose a migration but a human must issue the write-schema-migration call' — the agent SHOULD have issued the call and let auth-gate prompt the user. The framing actively misdirects orchestrator behavior. (3) IDENTITY-STAMPING VS AUTH-VERIFICATION CONFLATION: writer.user in caller-supplied tool args becomes the substrate attestation stamp via DispatchContext.writer.identity persisted into block items + the migrations.json items. The auth-gate verifies SOMEONE pressed Y at the terminal but does NOT verify the supplied writer.user identity matches the confirming-user identity (which pi knows internally from session state / env / git config). An agent could stamp 'alice@example.com' on a substrate item that 'davidryan@gmail.com' actually confirmed; substrate attestation lies; auth-gate is unaware. The writer-supplied-identity field is trust-on-claim despite the auth-gate being the actual identity verifier.

evidence.reference: AUTH_REQUIRED_TOOLS const includes all 4 in-body-checked tools (author-agent-spec, author-tool-grant, commit-attested, write-schema-migration) AND the 11 no-in-body-check Bucket-2 tools. Auth-gate handler is the canonical identity verifier post-FGAP-134.
in-body writer.kind=='human' enforcement + 'capability authoring is human-only' error message — redundant with auth-gate + misleading framing
same in-body check + framing as author-agent-spec

in-body check on writer.kind=='agent' branch requiring agent_id; the misframing here is asymmetric since commit-attested writes WITH writer.kind=agent for the attested-commit-by-agent semantic — yet the FGAP-133 strip rewrote the error to 'human-only authoring enforced' which is now wrong on multiple axes

in-body writer.kind=='human' enforcement + 'Capability/migration authoring is human-only; sub-agents have no escalation path' error message — the message that prompted the 2026-05-29 in-pi agent to refuse to issue the call

test narrative was refreshed at commit 18d510c (FGAP-136 CHECK 13 follow-up) but still frames the boundary as 'human-authored migration' rather than 'human-authorized agent-issued migration' — partial fix

DispatchContext.writer.identity persists into block items + migrations.json items as authorship attestation; field is sourced from caller-supplied args; no cross-check against the actual user-at-the-terminal identity from ctx
ExtensionContext exposes ctx.hasUI but not (in the surface we've audited) a verified-user-identity field that the auth-gate could thread to the tool body to overwrite caller-supplied writer.user

gaps.impact: P1 because three coupled fragilities compound: (a) agents read 'human-only' framing + REFUSE to issue calls they should issue (empirically observed 2026-05-29), reducing operator efficacy in the very flow FEAT-006 north-star + the 11-registry customization promise depend on; (b) the inconsistent in-body model across Bucket-2 tools means future maintainers reading the codebase get a non-uniform picture of how authorization actually works; (c) identity-stamping trust-on-claim means audit trails (DispatchContext attestation persisted into substrate items) can be misleading without the operator or any reviewer being able to detect the divergence. The whole point of post-FGAP-134 governance was identity-verification-at-trust-boundary; this gap restores the verification at the gate but leaves the identity-stamp on the lie.
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: human-authorized vs human-authored; trust-boundary vs trust-on-claim; auth-gate canonical identity check; identity-stamping verified at terminal vs supplied in args
gaps.proposed_resolution: Three-part coordinated reframing under a single arc: (1) STRIP IN-BODY writer.kind=='human' CHECKS from author-agent-spec, author-tool-grant, commit-attested, write-schema-migration. The auth-gate is the canonical identity check; in-body duplication confuses the model + invites the misframing that produced this FGAP. Tool bodies trust that the auth-gate fired (it always does for Bucket-2 tools — guaranteed by AUTH_REQUIRED_TOOLS membership). Tests for these tools that asserted in-body refusal of writer.kind='agent' get removed or refactored to assert auth-gate behavior instead. (2) RENAME FRAMING ACROSS ALL DOCSTRINGS + ERROR MESSAGES + TEST NARRATIVES from 'human-only' / 'human-only authoring' / 'sub-agents have no escalation path' to 'human-authorized' / 'requires user authorization via auth-gate' / 'agent issues; user authorizes'. Specific sites: each of the 4 affected tool source files' description + error strings; auth-gate.ts header + error-reason strings; schema-write-tool.test.ts narrative (already partially refreshed at 18d510c — complete the reframe). Cite the canonical model in the auth-gate.ts top-of-file docstring + reference it from each Bucket-2 tool's description. (3) IDENTITY-STAMP VERIFICATION: extend the auth-gate handler to mutate event.input.writer.user (or .writer.identity) to the actual confirming-user identity drawn from pi's session/env/git-config before passing through to the tool body. The handler runs BEFORE the tool body per ToolCallEventResult contract — mutating event.input is the documented mechanism (per node_modules/@earendil-works/pi-coding-agent/dist/core/extensions/types.d.ts:605-611 'To modify arguments, mutate event.input in place instead'). Source for the verified-identity: investigate which ctx field carries the launched-pi-process operator identity (likely from $USER env or pi config). On confirm: overwrite event.input.writer.user to that verified value. On decline: still return {block: true}. Tool bodies + substrate attestation now reflect actually-confirming-user rather than agent-supplied claim. Tests verify the overwrite happens + the persisted DispatchContext.writer.identity matches the verified value not the caller-supplied value. [CLOSED 2026-05-29 via FGAP-138 unified arc — branch fgap-138-canonical-reframing-identity-verify, 6 commits d872fce..7af28e6, VER-063 attests. Auth-gate at pi-dispatch is now the canonical identity verifier + mutates event.input.writer to verified terminal-operator identity on confirm=true. In-body writer.kind checks stripped from 3 tools (commit-attested agent_id check retained as different concern). Misleading framing renamed to canonical model across all sites. New verified-identity module reads git config user.email with USER env fallback, module-level cached.]

gaps.title: pi-workflows lacks bundled quality-pattern templates (adversarial-verify / perspective-diverse / judge-panel / loop-until-dry / multi-modal-sweep / completeness-critic) — pi-workflows can express most via existing step types but lacks templates + pattern guidance + voting/scoring primitives; load-bearing evidence is today's FGAP-135 CHECK 11 PARTIAL where a single-Explore adversarial probe self-categorized findings as 'low-risk' that the orchestrator accepted without challenge
gaps.description: Per R-0014 recon axis 3: Claude Code dynamic workflows docs explicitly enumerate 6 quality patterns (adversarial-verify / perspective-diverse / judge-panel / loop-until-dry / multi-modal-sweep / completeness-critic) with concrete script-snippet templates. pi-workflows can express most via existing step types (parallel + agent + transform + gate) but lacks: (a) bundled .workflow.yaml templates under examples/patterns/; (b) Nunjucks macro library for boilerplate (vote-aggregation; majority-survives filter); (c) SDK documentation referencing patterns + when to use each. Load-bearing session evidence: FGAP-135 CHECK 11 PARTIAL surfaced operational ambiguity in 3 TruncationResult edge-case variants; single-Explore adversarial probe self-categorized as 'low-risk'; orchestrator hand-waved past + user caught the violation. With a bundled adversarial-verify pattern (N independent skeptics + structured findings-schema vote-aggregation), the hand-wave would have been mechanically refuted by N-1 of N skeptics rather than depending on orchestrator judgment.

evidence.reference: Part 3 axis 3 — Quality Patterns Enumerated in Docs — ADOPT verdict with concrete bundled-template addition spec
FGAP-135 closing-citation describes the CHECK 11 PARTIAL load-bearing evidence
directory exists but no patterns/ subdir + no bundled quality-pattern workflows today

gaps.impact: Orchestrators improvise quality-verification patterns instead of running canonical templates. Each improvisation re-creates the adversarial-verify or judge-panel logic with risk of judgment-call hand-waves (today's CHECK 11 evidence). N-skeptic vote aggregation, perspective-diverse-lens review, loop-until-dry discovery — each implemented inconsistently across orchestrator runs without reusable + auditable substrate. The framework's quality-pattern story is partial: primitives exist but the canonical compositions don't ship.
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: quality-pattern templates; bundled-workflow library; vote-aggregation primitive; majority-survives filter; pattern-composition macros
gaps.proposed_resolution: Add packages/pi-workflows/examples/patterns/ subdirectory with 6 starter .workflow.yaml templates (adversarial-verify / perspective-diverse / judge-panel / loop-until-dry / multi-modal-sweep / completeness-critic) per R-0014 axis 3 specs. Add Nunjucks macro library (templates/shared/quality-patterns.macros.md) for boilerplate (vote-aggregation across N findings sets; majority-survives filter; per-lens-perspective dispatch). Update pi-workflows skill-narrative + SDK docs to reference each pattern + when canonical. Workflow validation gates ensure patterns load cleanly. No source-code-language changes; YAML + macros + docs.

gaps.title: pi-workflows LoopSpec lacks expression-based termination predicates (until / untilBudgetRemaining); maxAttempts hardcoding cannot express loop-until-dry / loop-until-condition canonical patterns without manual gate+break shims inside the loop body
gaps.description: Per R-0014 recon axis 4: Claude Code workflows let the script body express loop-until-dry via arbitrary expression gates (while loop with break on condition). pi-workflows loop step is hardcoded to maxAttempts (number) or attempts (expression evaluating to number) — both terminate by COUNT, not CONDITION. Loop-until-dry (terminate when no new issues found across iterations) requires a manual gate+break shim inside the loop body that's brittle + verbose + doesn't express the operator's intent at the spec level. Loop-until-budget-remaining (terminate when token budget drops below threshold) cannot be expressed at all — pi-workflows has no aggregate budget surface (see FGAP-141).

evidence.reference: Part 3 axis 4 — Loop-Until-Dry / Loop-Until-Budget / Loop-Until-Count — ADOPT verdict
LoopSpec definition + execution — currently only maxAttempts / attempts; no until / untilBudgetRemaining

gaps.impact: Operators expressing iterative-refinement workflows (review → improve → check → repeat-until-clean) must hardcode iteration counts that either over-iterate (waste tokens after convergence) or under-iterate (terminate before convergence). The canonical loop-until-dry pattern from Claude Code workflows docs cannot be expressed natively; requires manual nested gate+break shims that defeat the spec's declarative intent.
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: expression-based loop termination; until-condition predicates; budget-aware termination
gaps.proposed_resolution: Extend LoopSpec with optional fields: 'until: <expression>' (terminate when expression evaluates truthy AFTER each iteration; supersedes maxAttempts when both are set OR runs alongside as 'either-first' termination per executor decision); 'untilBudgetRemaining: <number>' (terminate when global budget.remaining() < threshold; depends on FGAP-141 budget surface). Updates: packages/pi-workflows/src/workflow-spec.ts (LoopSpec interface + AJV schema); packages/pi-workflows/src/step-loop.ts (executor short-circuits when until evaluates truthy); packages/pi-workflows/src/expression.ts (expression context exposes loop.iteration + loop.priorAttempts for predicate access).

gaps.title: pi-workflows has no aggregate token-budget surface — each step tracks per-step usage but no WorkflowSpec-level budget.maxTokens / budget.spent() / budget.remaining() across all steps; cannot express budget-aware termination or fail-on-overspend semantics
gaps.description: Per R-0014 recon axis 7: Claude Code workflows expose budget.total / budget.spent() / budget.remaining() globals shared across the main script + all spawned agents; decrements as agents run; HARD ceiling — agent() throws once total reached. pi-workflows has per-step usage tracking (input/output tokens, cache, cost via dispatch.ts) but no aggregate across steps + no enforcement at the WorkflowSpec level. Operators cannot cap runaway loops by budget; cannot route by remaining-budget (e.g. fast-path when low; thorough-path when ample); cannot fail-with-structured-error on overspend.

evidence.reference: Part 3 axis 7 — Token Budget Tracking — ADOPT verdict
per-step usage capture; no aggregate sink
WorkflowSpec interface — no budget field

gaps.impact: Runaway loops + accidental overspend on long-running workflows are not contained at the framework layer. Operators must manually track aggregate spend via post-hoc inspection of per-step usage. Budget-aware termination (loop-until-budget-remaining; see FGAP-140) cannot be implemented without this surface. Composability with Claude Code workflows' budget model (when pi-workflows are invoked from Claude-Code-side) is poor — the two budget models don't speak the same language.
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: workflow-level token budget; budget.maxTokens; budget.spent; budget.remaining; budget-aware termination; aggregate usage sink
gaps.proposed_resolution: Extend WorkflowSpec with optional budget object: { maxTokens?: number, maxCost?: number, alertThreshold?: number (0-1 fraction; default 0.8) }. Extend ExecutionState with budgetSpent: { tokens: number, cost: number, byStep: Record<string, {tokens, cost}> }. Executor accumulates per-step usage into budgetSpent after each step completes. Pre-step check: if budget exhausted → throw BudgetExhaustedError with structured payload. Expose to expression scope as budget.spent / budget.remaining for use in transform / gate / until predicates. Updates: types.ts (WorkflowSpec + ExecutionState), workflow-executor.ts (accumulation + check), expression.ts (budget. scope).

gaps.title: pi-workflows agent step validates output.schema POST-execution (wasted tokens on schema-violating output); no pre-execution forcing via StructuredOutput-tool dispatch — agents can ignore declared output contract until validation fails at completion
gaps.description: Per R-0014 recon axis 5: Claude Code workflows' agent(prompt, { schema }) opt FORCES the agent to use StructuredOutput tool before execution begins — schema-violating output is impossible by construction. pi-workflows agent step declares output.schema (validated via AJV against the agent's textOutput POST-completion) — if the agent's output violates schema, the workflow surfaces a validation error AFTER paying full token cost. Operators paying for schema-violating outputs in 'agent confused, returned markdown when JSON was required' scenarios is preventable. Pi-workflows DOES have an output.format: json directive that hints intent but does not force tool-use at dispatch.

evidence.reference: Part 3 axis 5 — Structured-Output Forcing (JSON Schema Pre-Execution) — ADOPT verdict
agent dispatch + post-completion output.schema validation
buildArgs + spawn flow — no --schema-enforce or equivalent flag passed to pi CLI

gaps.impact: Token waste on schema-violating outputs that the workflow then rejects. The cost ratio is unfavorable: full-prompt + full-output cost paid to produce text that the workflow discards. Operators relying on output.schema validation as a contract are paying for the contract violation rather than preventing it.
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: pre-execution schema forcing; StructuredOutput-tool dispatch; output-contract enforcement at dispatch boundary
gaps.proposed_resolution: Extend dispatch.ts buildArgs: when step.output.schema is set, pass schema to pi CLI via new --schema-enforce flag (assumes pi CLI supports it; verify upstream surface) OR inject schema into the system prompt with explicit StructuredOutput-tool-use directive. Pre-execution forcing complements the existing post-execution validation (don't remove the post-check — defense-in-depth). Updates: step-agent.ts (thread schema through to dispatch options); dispatch.ts (build the --schema-enforce arg or system-prompt injection). If pi CLI surface doesn't support pre-execution forcing today, file a follow-on FGAP for the upstream change OR implement system-prompt-injection as the pi-workflows-side fallback.

gaps.title: pi-workflows dispatch does not surface worktree isolation as a step opt — parallel agent steps that mutate shared files have no framework-provided isolation mechanism; today's subprocess + cwd-scoped dispatch leaves parallel-mutation conflicts to operator discipline
gaps.description: Per R-0014 recon axis 8: Claude Code workflows expose isolation: 'worktree' on agent() opt — each agent runs in a freshly-cloned git worktree with auto-cleanup if no changes; concurrent agents mutating the same files cannot conflict. pi-workflows agent step dispatches as subprocess in the same cwd; concurrent parallel agents have no isolation. Today's pattern: when operators need worktree-isolated parallel work, they hand-implement worktree-spawn in shell command steps + manage cleanup themselves. Expensive operationally + error-prone (cleanup-on-failure paths).

evidence.reference: Part 3 axis 8 — Worktree Isolation per-Agent — ADOPT verdict (partial support already in dispatch; surface not exposed)
subprocess spawn in cwd; no worktree spawn / cleanup path

gaps.impact: Parallel agent steps that mutate shared files race + corrupt each other's edits. Operators avoid the parallel pattern entirely or hand-build worktree spawn in command step shims that bypass framework lifecycle management (cleanup-on-error not handled). Worktree-spawning cost (~200-500ms + disk per agent per Claude docs) is justified only when parallel mutation is the threat — but operators have no framework hook to opt into it per-step.
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: worktree isolation; per-agent filesystem sandbox; parallel-mutation safety
gaps.proposed_resolution: Add 'isolation' field to agent StepSpec: 'isolation?: 'worktree' | { type: 'worktree', cleanup?: boolean, branch?: string }'. dispatch.ts checks step.isolation: if 'worktree', spawn agent in `git worktree add <tmpdir> HEAD` + execute subprocess with cwd=tmpdir + on completion: if no changes, remove worktree; else preserve + report path + branch (per Claude docs cleanup semantics). Cost surfaced in step output (worktree-creation latency added to step duration). Documentation: only enable when concurrent mutation risk exists; do NOT default-on (cost overhead).

gaps.title: pi-workflows workflow: step type is declared in spec but REJECTED at parse time (phase-6, not yet implemented) — operators cannot compose workflows from workflows; audit-codebase → forEach file → audit-file workflow pattern is inexpressible at the spec level today; manual subprocess chaining is the only workaround
gaps.description: Per R-0014 recon axis 10: Claude Code workflows expose workflow(name, args) hook as the canonical sub-workflow composition primitive (1-level only — child cannot recurse). pi-workflows declares a workflow step type in spec definitions but the validator REJECTS it at workflow-spec.ts:~291 (phase-6 not-yet-implemented marker). High-value composition patterns are blocked: (a) audit-codebase top-level workflow that fans out to per-file sub-workflow invocations; (b) canonical-pipeline meta-workflow that calls plan-mode + recon + implementation + verification sub-workflows in sequence; (c) batch-FGAP-closure that calls per-FGAP closure-workflow N times with different IDs. All inexpressible today; require manual subprocess chaining outside the framework.

evidence.reference: Part 3 axis 10 — Sub-Workflow Invocation — ADOPT verdict (rejection at parse currently)
validator rejection of workflow step type at parse time (phase-6 marker)

gaps.impact: Composition is blocked. Operators wanting to reuse a workflow as a step in another workflow are forced to hand-chain pi -p subprocess calls outside the framework — losing state-persistence, resume, validation, expression evaluation, all the affordances pi-workflows provides. Today's session pattern (multiple FGAP closures, each through identical canonical-pipeline) cannot be batched as a single parent-workflow invoking per-FGAP-closure sub-workflow. Pattern adoption from Claude Code (workflow() hook is 1-level) is direct + the prior phase-6 design work is wasted as long as the rejection stays.
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: sub-workflow composition; workflow-as-step; 1-level composition; recursive workflow invocation (forbidden); state-hierarchy under parent runId
gaps.proposed_resolution: Implement workflow step type: (1) workflow-spec.ts: remove the parse-time rejection at line ~291; add WorkflowStepSpec interface with required 'workflow: <name>' field + optional 'input: <expression>' + optional 'forEach: <expression>' for iteration; (2) validation: validateWorkflow checks target workflow exists + target's inputSchema matches step's input expression shape + DETECTS recursion (target.name === current workflow OR target transitively invokes current → reject at validation per Claude Code's 1-level limit); (3) workflow-executor.ts: implement execution — recursive call to executeWorkflow with runDir hierarchical (parent-runId/subflows/<workflow-name>-N/); forEach iterates with item context; sub-workflow result lands at steps.<step-name>.output; (4) expression scope: parent workflow can reference sub-workflow output via standard ${{ steps.X.output }}; (5) nesting depth: enforce 2-level limit (parent + 1 sub-workflow level; sub-workflow cannot itself invoke workflow step) per Claude Code's 1-level constraint. Tests: composition smoke + forEach + recursion-detection. No changes to other step types.

gaps.title: pi-workflows DAG planner inserts strict layer-barriers between dependency tiers; cannot express streaming-pipeline-no-barrier semantics where item A is in stage 2 while item B is still in stage 1; asymmetric work (50 slow + 50 fast checks) loses parallelism waiting for slowest item in each layer
gaps.description: Per R-0014 recon axis 1: Claude Code workflows' pipeline(items, ...stages) lets each item flow through stages asynchronously — item A can be in stage 3 while item B is still in stage 1; asymmetric per-stage latency does not block fast items behind slow ones. pi-workflows DAG planner (dag.ts buildPlanFromDeps) builds topological-sort layers + REQUIRES all steps in layer N to complete before layer N+1 starts. For forEach-style work (per-file analysis), this means 50 slow + 50 fast checks all wait on the slowest item in each layer; wall-clock = sum of (slowest item in each layer) instead of max of (slowest item-chain end-to-end). Significant wall-clock loss on heterogeneous work.

evidence.reference: Part 3 axis 1 — Pipeline-No-Barrier vs DAG-with-Barriers — INSPIRE verdict
buildPlanFromDeps → topological layers + buildConservativePlan implicit sequencing

gaps.impact: Wall-clock loss on heterogeneous workflows (per-file audits, per-FGAP closures, per-package scans). The pattern Claude Code calls 'streaming pipeline' is canonical for fan-out-with-per-item-progression but inexpressible in pi-workflows spec today.
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: streaming pipeline; per-item asynchronous stage progression; soft-barrier between stages; asymmetric concurrency
gaps.proposed_resolution: Add a 'stream' step type OR a 'streaming: true' flag on the existing parallel step type. Spec example: steps.analyze-all: { stream: { stages: [read-file, check-syntax, report], forEach: <expression> } }. Executor implementation: collect per-item per-stage promises; resolution polls for ready-items in stage N+1 rather than waiting for layer N completion. Risk: spec semantics now include two parallelism models (DAG-layered + streaming) — operator must choose. Documentation must clarify when each is canonical. Significant refactor in dag.ts + workflow-executor.ts; lower-priority than ADOPT items but not deferred.

gaps.title: pi-workflows parallel step creates all sub-steps eagerly at parse; Claude Code parallel(thunks) defers execution until each thunk is invoked allowing conditional branching where some thunks never run; pi cannot express conditional-fan-out without per-sub-step 'when' fields
gaps.description: Per R-0014 recon axis 2: Claude Code workflows' parallel(thunks: Array<() => Promise<T>>) takes lazy thunks; some thunks may never execute based on prior-stage results. pi-workflows parallel step declares all sub-steps upfront in the YAML spec; all are created eagerly (even unreachable ones via gate breaks). Operators wanting conditional fan-out (e.g., 'run fast-path or thorough-path based on input.mode') must hand-build with gate routing rather than declaring naturally.

evidence.reference: Part 3 axis 2 — parallel() with Thunks vs parallel Step Type — INSPIRE verdict
parallel sub-step eager declaration; no per-sub-step conditional

gaps.impact: Operators expressing conditional fan-out patterns add complexity via gate steps + multiple parallel blocks. Simple lazy-thunk semantics from Claude Code workflows are inexpressible at the spec level. Spec becomes verbose for what should be a single 'parallel + when' construct.
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: thunk-parallel; lazy sub-step evaluation; conditional fan-out; per-sub-step 'when' field
gaps.proposed_resolution: Add 'when: <expression>' field to parallel sub-step definitions. Executor evaluates each sub-step's 'when' before dispatch; skips false-when sub-steps without creating their subprocess. Alternative: new 'thunk-parallel' step type that takes an array of step definitions each with optional 'when'. Simpler addition: extend existing parallel sub-step grammar with optional 'when' — matches the 'when' surface already used at top-level step + on transform/gate/loop steps.

gaps.title: pi-workflows agent dispatch has no content-identity cache (SHA256 of prompt+opts → cached result); each agent dispatch re-runs even on identical input across invocations + sessions; persistent cache could massively speedup repeated runs (nightly audits with same code)
gaps.description: Per R-0014 recon axis 6: Claude Code workflows cache (prompt, opts) → result; longest unchanged prefix returns cached results instantly; survives resume within session. pi-workflows has per-step state persistence (re-runs un-completed steps on resume) but no content-identity cache — identical agent dispatches across invocations or sessions re-run + re-spend tokens. For workflows run repeatedly with same input (nightly audits, idempotent verifications, replay of stable test scenarios), the wasted token cost is significant + the wall-clock latency is unnecessary.

evidence.reference: Part 3 axis 6 — Resume Cache by (Prompt, Opts) Identity — INSPIRE verdict
no cache check before subprocess spawn

gaps.impact: Token waste on repeated runs of identical workflows. Cache invalidation puzzles (changed agent behavior → stale cached result) are real risk but mitigatable by explicit cache-clear flag + content-hash including agent.yaml hash + tool-surface hash. Operators running nightly verifications pay for re-computation that yields identical results.
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: content-identity caching; SHA256(prompt+opts+agent-spec-hash); persistent agent cache; cache-by-input not cache-by-step-id
gaps.proposed_resolution: Optional opt-in cache mode on agent step: 'cache: true | { ttl?: duration, key?: <expression> }'. Compute cache key = SHA256(prompt + JSON.stringify(opts) + agent-spec-content-hash + tool-surface-hash). Store cache in ~/.pi/agent-cache/<hash>.json with metadata (timestamp, agent-spec-version, expires). On dispatch, check cache before spawn; return cached result if hit + not expired. Configuration: 'pi cache clear' / 'pi cache list' / 'pi cache stats' CLI surface. Spec example: steps.audit-file: { agent: file-auditor, cache: true, input: { file: ${{ item }} } }. Risk + mitigation: explicit opt-in only (default off); cache key includes agent-spec hash so spec changes auto-invalidate; ttl bounds staleness; cache-clear command + per-invocation --no-cache flag for forced re-run.

gaps.title: pi-workflows agent step has no agentType dispatch hint — operators cannot request a specialized subagent type (Explore / code-reviewer / etc. analog) from the agent step; pi-workflows agents reference .agent.yaml specs by name only with no subagent-type routing surface
gaps.description: Per R-0014 recon axis 9: Claude Code workflows' agent(prompt, { agentType: 'Explore' | 'code-reviewer' }) lets the script invoke specialized subagent types from the same registry the Agent tool uses. pi-workflows agent step references agents by name (agent: code-explorer) which maps to a .agent.yaml file; no subagent-type field. Two different mechanisms with similar intent — type-routing vs name-routing. Pi's name-routing is more explicit but less composable (no way to say 'I want an Explore-type agent dispatched against any agent spec').

evidence.reference: Part 3 axis 9 — agentType Opt — INSPIRE verdict
agent step references agent by name only; no agentType field on StepSpec

gaps.impact: Operators wanting to compose 'Explore-type-agent runs against this agent spec' cannot express it at the spec level. Mapping to Claude Code workflows surface for cross-system invocation is lossy. Pi's agent specs themselves could declare their type but no current convention captures it.
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: subagent-type routing; agent-spec + agent-type composition; dispatch-hint surface
gaps.proposed_resolution: Add 'agentType: <string>' opt to agent step OR add 'agent_type: <string>' top-level field to .agent.yaml specs. dispatch.ts passes agentType to pi CLI via new flag (assumes pi CLI supports subagent-type dispatch; verify upstream surface). If pi CLI does not support: implement pi-workflows-side dispatch routing where agentType selects from a registry of dispatch-strategy implementations. Simpler shape: agent_type as advisory metadata on the agent spec consumed by dispatch + by introspection tools (workflow-validate could check agent_type compatibility with step.agentType).

gaps.title: pi-workflows discoverability surface is weak — workflows live in ~/.pi/workflows/ or .pi/workflows/ + execute via workflow-execute tool or TUI picker; Claude Code workflows auto-register as /<name> slash commands + appear in / autocomplete; pi has no equivalent runtime-discovery / autocomplete / slash-command-shorthand surface
gaps.description: Per R-0014 recon axis 11: Claude Code workflows registered at .claude/workflows/<name>.js appear as /<name> slash commands in / autocomplete; discoverable via /workflows list. pi-workflows lives in directories but execution requires either workflow-execute tool invocation (with explicit name argument) or TUI picker navigation. No slash-command-shorthand registration; no auto-complete-in-conversation surface; no /workflows-list-style command on the pi side. Operators running frequently-used workflows incur friction every time.

evidence.reference: Part 3 axis 11 — Bundled / Discoverable Workflows — INSPIRE verdict
workflow-execute / workflow-list / workflow-status tools but no slash-command auto-registration

gaps.impact: Operator friction on frequently-used workflows. Workflow discovery requires explicit knowledge of names + invocation conventions. Mapping to Claude-Code-side parity for cross-system operator UX is poor — slash-command-shorthand is the canonical pi-extension command surface but not extended to workflows.
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: slash-command registration; workflow autocomplete; pi-side / autocomplete equivalent; bundled-workflow surface parity
gaps.proposed_resolution: Extend pi-agent-dispatch or pi-workflows extension to register each discovered workflow as a pi slash command. Auto-discovery from bundled (packages/*/workflows/) + project (.pi/workflows/) + user (~/.pi/workflows/) directories at extension load. Slash command shape: /<workflow-name> invokes workflow-execute with that name + parses args per the workflow's inputSchema. TUI: add workflow picker to main menu (alongside other actions). Documentation refresh for discoverability + invocation. Verify pi CLI supports dynamic slash-command registration (may require upstream surface; if not, file follow-on FGAP).

gaps.title: pi-workflows expression evaluator has no determinism enforcement — expressions evaluated at dispatch time can include non-deterministic patterns (current-time / random / uuid) that break resume semantics silently; Claude Code workflows reject Date.now() / Math.random() / argless new Date() at validation time to maintain cache + resume integrity
gaps.description: Per R-0014 recon axis 12: Claude Code workflows throw at script-validation time on Date.now() / Math.random() / argless new Date() — would break the (prompt, opts) cache + resume semantics (same script + same args must produce identical agent results). pi-workflows expression evaluator (${{ }} syntax) lets operators reference any value at dispatch time; if expression evaluates to current time / random number / uuid / etc., resume re-evaluates → different value → resume cache (when added per FGAP-147) would never hit. Today no enforcement; no warning; silent non-determinism breaks resume idempotence.

evidence.reference: Part 3 axis 12 — Resume-Incompatible API Enforcement — INSPIRE verdict
expression evaluator with no determinism enforcement

gaps.impact: Resume semantics silently break when operators include non-deterministic expressions. Cache-by-input (FGAP-147) would never hit. State persistence + resume produce different agent dispatches than original run. Operators discover the gap only when resume yields different results — late + diagnostic-poor.
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: determinism enforcement; resume-cache integrity; non-deterministic-expression detection; pre-flight expression validation
gaps.proposed_resolution: Add pre-flight expression validator to validateWorkflow: scan all ${{ }} expressions for disallowed patterns (Date.now, new Date() with no args, Math.random, crypto.randomUUID, uuid(), or any user-registered non-deterministic filter). Issue warning at validation-time (status: warning) OR error under --strict-determinism flag. Documentation: enumerate which built-in filters are deterministic vs non-deterministic + which expression patterns to avoid. Optional: provide deterministic alternatives (e.g., 'workflow.startTime' filter that captures startup-time once + returns same value across resumes).

gaps.title: run-work-order-loop hard-dep on work-orders block kind is undocumented at tool surface + install ceremony
gaps.description: The run-work-order-loop Pi tool (packages/pi-agent-dispatch/src/run-work-order-loop-tool.ts + work-order-loop.ts) calls readBlock(cwd, 'work-orders') as its first operation; the block kind is hard-required for the tool to function. The dependency is by-design per TASK-088 (which defined work-orders as the canonical source-of-truth for orchestrator work-iteration with attestation + iteration semantics + human-OK gate per DEC-0047). However, the dependency is not surfaced in the tool description, and the /context install ceremony does not warn substrate authors that pruning the work-orders block kind also loses access to run-work-order-loop. Discovered via clock-menu-app usage-feedback FB-014 (2026-05-29T13:37Z): the user intentionally pruned work-orders from their substrate during initial setup and only discovered the lost capability when attempting to invoke the tool. This is the inverse of FGAP-137 (which surfaced the same kind of contract-clarity gap for scripts/launch-constrained-pi.sh --continue / -c documentation) — both surfaces have load-bearing behavior that wasn't visible to operators making install / launch choices.

evidence.reference: Hard-coded readBlock(cwd, 'work-orders') call; throws WorkOrderNotFoundError naming the work-orders block when absent. No fallback path; no inline-input alternative.

Tool description names 'the work-orders block' as the source but does not state that the block kind MUST be installed in the consuming substrate. Operator reading the description without prior context can install pi-agent-dispatch + grant the tool to a subagent + discover the dependency only at first invocation.

FB-014 verbatim filing by clock-menu-app author 2026-05-29T13:37Z: 'After removing the work-orders block kind from our substrate during pruning, run-work-order-loop cannot be used.' Workaround: none — tool is inaccessible. Note included: 'this dependency is not obvious when choosing which blocks to keep.'

Substrate-surfaces investigation report 2026-05-30 verdict: 'intentional coupling, not framework bug' for the readBlock dependency; documentation gap is the separable fix; coupling-itself stays per TASK-088 design.

gaps.impact: Substrate authors making install-time decisions about which block kinds to keep cannot anticipate that pruning work-orders also loses run-work-order-loop. The cliff surfaces only at tool-invocation time. For minimal substrates (project-local micro-substrates, learning examples, demo configurations) the loss may be acceptable but should be a known cost, not a surprise. For users attempting to pair pi-agent-dispatch with a non-orchestrator substrate shape, the tool's value-proposition collapses without warning.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Two-part documentation fix: (1) extend run-work-order-loop-tool.ts tool description to state explicitly 'requires the work-orders block kind installed in the consuming substrate per TASK-088 canonical schema; pruning the block kind during /context install renders this tool inaccessible'; (2) extend the /context install ceremony output (or the install-related skill narratives) to surface a 'tools that depend on this block kind' mapping per kind, so substrate authors making install choices see the downstream tool-availability consequences. Companion FGAP-152 captures the separate question of whether a tool variant accepting work-order JSON inline (without requiring the block) is itself worth adding.

gaps.title: run-work-order-loop has no alternative entry point for substrates that legitimately prune the work-orders block kind
gaps.description: FGAP-151 (companion) documents the immediate fix path — surface the work-orders-block-kind dependency in the tool description + install ceremony so substrate authors do not encounter it by surprise. This FGAP captures the separate scope-decision: whether to add an alternative entry point (e.g. a --inline work-order JSON parameter to run-work-order-loop, or a sibling tool run-inline-work-order accepting one work-order record directly) for substrates that legitimately prune work-orders. Discovered by clock-menu-app usage-feedback FB-014 (2026-05-29T13:37Z): the user's substrate setup is minimal-by-intent (10 standard block kinds + 1 custom usage-feedback kind, work-orders pruned); under FGAP-151 documentation alone, the user knows what they are losing but still has no path to invoke the orchestrator iteration semantics (attestation + human-OK gate per DEC-0047) without re-installing the block kind. The user-hit-it dimension persists past the documentation fix.

evidence.reference: work-order-loop core implementation is fully parameterized over a single WorkOrderRecord — the iteration mechanics + attestation pipeline + human-OK gate do not structurally require the record to come from a block read. The block-read is a single layer at the entry point.

Tool surface accepts a work_order_id parameter; substituting (or augmenting with) a work_order parameter accepting an inline WorkOrderRecord JSON would expose the same iteration mechanics without the block-kind prerequisite.

FB-014 verbatim: substrate pruned work-orders intentionally; tool became inaccessible; no workaround. Expected-behavior section names exactly this alternative: 'Or work orders could be stored in a different block or passed inline.'

Substrate-surfaces investigation report verdict separating documentation-fix (FGAP-151 scope) from alternative-entry-point gap (this FGAP scope). Per feedback_intentional_doesnt_close_gap.md: by-design verdict on coupling does not close the user-hit-it gap on alternative entry.

gaps.impact: Substrates that minimize installed block kinds (project-local micro-substrates; demo configurations; learning examples; substrates with non-orchestrator shape) have no path to invoke the run-work-order-loop iteration + attestation + human-OK gate semantics without re-installing work-orders. The tool's design pattern (canonical iteration semantics) is valuable independent of the block-storage choice, but the only entry point ties it to block storage. Result: a usable iteration primitive is gated behind a substrate-shape choice that may be unrelated to the iteration use case.
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: alternative entry point; tool variant; inline-input parameter
gaps.proposed_resolution: Scope-decision required from user (operator decides whether to build): (a) add --inline / --work-order JSON parameter to run-work-order-loop accepting a WorkOrderRecord directly, bypassing the readBlock call when present; (b) add sibling Pi tool run-inline-work-order accepting the same record type without a loop wrapper; (c) leave as-is and rely on FGAP-151 documentation to clarify install-time choice. Implementation cost (a) is low — the work-order-loop core function is already parameterized over WorkOrderRecord; entry point would dispatch to either readBlock or the inline param. Trade-off: option (a) increases the tool surface (more parameters; precedence rule between work_order_id and work_order); option (c) keeps the surface minimal at the cost of usability cliff for pruned substrates.

gaps.title: resolvePromptField heuristic misclassifies inline prompts containing '/' as template file paths
gaps.description: resolvePromptField at packages/pi-jit-agents/src/agent-spec.ts line 21 classifies any single-line string containing a forward slash as a template file path: `value.endsWith('.md') || value.endsWith('.txt') || (value.includes('/') && !value.includes('\n'))`. Inline prompts that mention paths or URLs in natural language (e.g. 'Read docs/03-solar-engine.md') are routed into the template branch and stored in systemPromptTemplate / taskPromptTemplate instead of systemPrompt / taskPrompt. compileAgent later attempts to read the prompt text as a file path and fails with ENOENT. There is no explicit `inline:` discriminator parallel to the explicit `template:` discriminator in the YAML shape; classification is heuristic-only and the heuristic over-fires on natural-language strings.

evidence.reference: FB-001 'resolvePromptField heuristic misclassifies inline prompts as template file paths'; actual_behavior: prompt text 'Read docs/03-solar-engine.md' is stored as taskPromptTemplate instead of taskPrompt, compileAgent then renderTemplateFile fails with ENOENT

Investigation report FB-001 section confirms code at agent-spec.ts:21 verbatim; verdict 'Code + Schema (dual-layer fix)' — narrow resolvePromptField to treat value as template only if ends with .md or .txt, remove the includes('/') branch; schema introduces explicit inline vs template discriminator
resolvePromptField function definition with the over-firing heuristic on line 21
