gaps.impact: Agent authors writing natural-language prompts that mention paths or URLs file specs that pass author-agent-spec validation, persist to the substrate, and fail at dispatch-time with ENOENT. The misclassification is silent at author-time and load-bearing at dispatch-time. Compounds with FGAP for FB-002 (YAML serializer collapses newlines, removing the only existing escape from the heuristic) and FGAP for FB-003 (round-trip validation does not catch the misclassification).
gaps.proposed_resolution: Code at packages/pi-jit-agents/src/agent-spec.ts line 21: replace the disjunction `value.endsWith('.md') || value.endsWith('.txt') || (value.includes('/') && !value.includes('\n'))` with `value.endsWith('.md') || value.endsWith('.txt')` (file-extension check only). Schema layer: introduce an explicit prompt-discriminator shape (either nested `{inline: string} | {template: string}` form, or sibling marker `systemPromptType: 'inline' | 'template'`) and document it in the AgentSpec schema.

gaps.title: author-agent-spec YAML serializer folds multi-line prompts to a single line, destroying newlines
gaps.description: author-agent-spec at packages/pi-agent-dispatch/src/author-agent-spec-tool.ts line 72 (or line 88 per the investigation report's read) calls `yamlStringify(specObj)` with no options. The yaml package's default scalar style for multi-line strings is folded block scalar (`>-`), which collapses embedded `\n` characters to spaces. A prompt authored as `'line1\nline2'` becomes a single folded line in the written YAML. The author has no control over scalar style at the call site; no options are threaded through the tool surface to force literal block scalar (`|`) preservation of newlines.

evidence.reference: FB-002 'author-agent-spec YAML serializer uses folded block scalar, destroying newlines'; actual_behavior: prompt 'Read doc.\nPath is X.' becomes single folded line 'Read doc. Path is X.'; after folding the '/' triggers the FB-001 template-path heuristic

Investigation report FB-002 section confirms yamlStringify call site verbatim; verdict 'Code (tool parameter)' — pass `{lineWidth: -1, blockQuote: 'literal'}` options to yamlStringify to force literal block scalar preserving newlines
const yamlContent = yamlStringify(specObj); — no options passed, yaml package default folded-scalar style applies

gaps.impact: Multi-line prompts authored via author-agent-spec lose their newline structure at the serialization boundary. Multi-paragraph instructions, numbered workflows, and explicit blank-line separations are collapsed to single folded lines. Compounds with FGAP for FB-001: the author cannot use embedded newlines as an escape from the resolvePromptField '/' heuristic because the serializer strips the newlines before the YAML reaches disk. Authors have no diagnostic indication that their newline structure was lost.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Code at packages/pi-agent-dispatch/src/author-agent-spec-tool.ts line 72: pass options to yamlStringify to control scalar style — `yamlStringify(specObj, { lineWidth: -1, blockQuote: 'literal' })` per the investigation-report verdict. Exact option-name verification requires runtime check of the installed yaml package version; if option names differ, the fix shape stays the same — force literal-block-scalar serialization for multi-line strings, disable line wrapping.

gaps.title: author-agent-spec round-trip validation does not catch resolvePromptField misclassification — false positive at author-time
gaps.description: author-agent-spec validates its written spec by round-tripping through parseAgentYaml at packages/pi-agent-dispatch/src/author-agent-spec-tool.ts line 76 (the investigation report cites line 93 in its read; line numbers vary by file version). When a prompt is misclassified by resolvePromptField (per FGAP-153/FB-001), parseAgentYaml succeeds — the value is stored in systemPromptTemplate / taskPromptTemplate (a string field), which is structurally valid AgentSpec content. Validation reports success. The failure surfaces only later at compileAgent's renderTemplateFile call as ENOENT. The validation gate is shape-only — no semantic check that template-path values resolve to existing files or to `block:` sentinels.

evidence.reference: FB-003 'author-agent-spec validation is a false positive for misclassified prompts'; actual_behavior: spec with 'Read docs/03-solar-engine.md' as inline task prompt passes author-agent-spec validation; call-agent fails with ENOENT trying to open the prompt as a file

Investigation report FB-003 section confirms validation call site verbatim; verdict 'Code + Validation (dual-layer)' — code fix per FB-001 eliminates the misclassification; validation layer adds semantic check that template paths exist or are `block:` sentinels
parseAgentYaml entry point invoking resolvePromptField on spec.prompt.system and spec.prompt.task — no semantic validation after field resolution

gaps.impact: Authors receive author-agent-spec success messages for specs that will hard-fail at first dispatch. The corrupted state persists in the substrate until call-agent surfaces ENOENT. The validation gate's promise (round-trip succeeds = spec is dispatchable) is broken by the heuristic-classification chain in FGAP-153/154. Closing FGAP-153 (the heuristic) eliminates the misclassification entirely and makes this gap moot for the inline-prompt case; this gap stands separately as the structural absence of semantic validation in author-agent-spec for any template-path field.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Code at packages/pi-jit-agents/src/agent-spec.ts (resolvePromptField narrowing per FGAP-153 closure) eliminates the heuristic-driven misclassification. Defense-in-depth at packages/pi-agent-dispatch/src/author-agent-spec-tool.ts: after parseAgentYaml round-trip, verify that any template-path field (systemPromptTemplate, taskPromptTemplate) either resolves to an existing file or matches a `block:` sentinel. Throw at author-time when neither condition holds. The semantic-check layer also catches user-authored template paths that point to genuinely-missing files.

gaps.title: AgentSpec TypeScript type uses top-level prompt fields while YAML parser reads nested prompt.system/prompt.task — silent data loss across the shape divergence
gaps.description: The TypeScript AgentSpec interface at packages/pi-jit-agents/src/types.ts lines 21-37 declares top-level fields: `systemPrompt?: string`, `systemPromptTemplate?: string`, `taskPrompt?: string`, `taskPromptTemplate?: string`. The YAML parser at packages/pi-jit-agents/src/agent-spec.ts lines 178-179 reads `spec.prompt?.system` and `spec.prompt?.task` (nested under a `prompt` object). The two shapes do not agree. An author serializing a spec object that matches the TypeScript type to YAML produces `systemPrompt: ...` / `taskPrompt: ...` at the top level — and parseAgentYaml ignores both keys because it looks under `prompt.*`. The author receives no warning; the parsed spec has empty prompt fields; compileAgent reports 'no prompt content produced'.

evidence.reference: FB-004 'Two competing AgentSpec shapes: TypeScript type vs YAML input'; actual_behavior: writing {systemPrompt, taskPrompt} produces YAML with top-level keys; parseAgentYaml reads spec.prompt?.system and spec.prompt?.task — both undefined; no prompt content is produced

Investigation report FB-004 section confirms both surfaces verbatim; verdict 'Schema (canonical format definition)' — pick one shape (Option A: nested prompt; Option B: top-level fields) and enforce across TypeScript type + YAML schema + YAML parser + author-agent-spec serializer
AgentSpec interface declaring top-level systemPrompt / systemPromptTemplate / taskPrompt / taskPromptTemplate
parseAgentYaml reads spec.prompt?.system and spec.prompt?.task — nested under a prompt object, not top-level

gaps.impact: Author-side tooling that constructs AgentSpec objects from the TypeScript type and serializes them to YAML produces specs that the parser cannot read. The divergence is structural — not a transient code drift — and persists across every author surface that consumes the TypeScript type as the authoring contract. Without canonical-shape enforcement, the divergence will recur whenever new prompt-shape fields are added to either side.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Schema decision — pick ONE canonical shape: Option A (nested `prompt: { system?: string, task?: string }`) or Option B (top-level `systemPrompt: string, taskPrompt: string`) per the investigation-report verdict. After decision: update TypeScript type at packages/pi-jit-agents/src/types.ts to match the chosen shape; update YAML parser at packages/pi-jit-agents/src/agent-spec.ts to read the chosen shape; author or extend a JSON schema (AgentSpec.schema.json) declaring the canonical shape so the divergence cannot recur silently. Bundled author-agent-spec serializer must write the same shape it parses.

gaps.title: AgentSpec.model is type-optional but dispatch-required — call-agent throws 'no model specified' with no fallback
gaps.description: AgentSpec at packages/pi-jit-agents/src/types.ts line 25 declares `model?: string` (optional). callAgentTool.execute at packages/pi-agent-dispatch/src/call-agent-tool.ts line 83 rejects dispatch when modelSpec is absent: `if (!modelSpec) { throw new Error('call-agent: agent X has no model specified.'); }`. A spec passing TypeScript validation with no model field fails at runtime. There is no fallback path — call-agent does not defer to an orchestration-agent default, an ExtensionContext binding, or any other source. The optional-type framing suggests model may be supplied elsewhere; the runtime contradicts that framing.

evidence.reference: FB-005 'model field required at dispatch but optional in AgentSpec type'; actual_behavior: dispatch fails 'call-agent: agent X has no model specified.'

Investigation report FB-005 section confirms type at types.ts:25 and runtime reject at call-agent-tool.ts:83 verbatim; verdict 'Code' — either make model required in the AgentSpec type (Option A, breaking change cascading to all existing .agent.yaml specs without model) or add fallback resolution in call-agent (Option B, deferring to orchestration-agent default); explicitly NET-NEW relative to DEC-0001/0002/0003 and FGAP-115
model?: string; — declares model as optional in the canonical TypeScript type
if (!modelSpec) throw new Error 'call-agent: agent X has no model specified.' — runtime reject with no fallback

gaps.impact: Agent authors writing minimal specs (per the TypeScript type contract) ship specs that fail at dispatch with a runtime error. The type's optional-marker promises a flexibility the runtime does not honor. Distinct from FGAP-115 (which addresses bare-model-id resolution against session provider) — FGAP-115 assumes model is present; this gap is the absence-of-field case.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Code decision required between two options per the investigation-report verdict. Option A: change AgentSpec.model from `model?: string` to `model: string` (required in type) at packages/pi-jit-agents/src/types.ts:25 — breaking change cascading to every existing spec without a model field. Option B: add fallback resolution at packages/pi-agent-dispatch/src/call-agent-tool.ts:83 — defer to the orchestration-agent's default model when modelSpec is absent — keeps the type optional but honors the semantics. Pick one and document the contract in the AgentSpec schema (FGAP-156 dependency for canonical-shape parity).

gaps.title: No tool to discover dispatch-registry models — author must guess valid provider/modelId combinations
gaps.description: pi-agent-dispatch exposes no tool to list models available in the dispatch modelRegistry. ctx.modelRegistry.find(provider, modelId) at packages/pi-agent-dispatch/src/call-agent-tool.ts line 86 looks up models in an in-memory collection populated by pi-agent-core (the orchestrator host) from unknown sources. User-level models.json exists but is not consulted by the dispatch path. Authors discover valid model strings only by trial-and-error — submit a dispatch, observe 'not found in modelRegistry', try a different format.

evidence.reference: FB-007 'No tool to discover available dispatch models'; actual_behavior: two models attempted and rejected (deepseek-v4-pro / kimi-k2.6:cloud); correct format deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro worked; no tool exists to show valid provider/modelId combinations; notes: 'Unknown where the dispatch model registry is populated from'

Investigation report FB-007 section confirms absence verbatim ('No tool exists in pi-agent-dispatch to list available models in the dispatch registry'); verdict 'Tool surface' — create new Pi tool list-dispatch-models / discover-agent-models querying ctx.modelRegistry, plus scripts/orchestrator/list-dispatch-models.ts dual-surface CLI per DEC-0019/0020

gaps.impact: Authors of agent specs have no canonical way to know which models the dispatch path accepts. The mismatch between user-level models.json (visible) and dispatch modelRegistry (opaque) compounds the confusion — a model declared in models.json may still be rejected at dispatch. Iteration cost is high: each guess costs one round-trip dispatch attempt plus error inspection.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Tool surface — new Pi tool `list-dispatch-models` (or `discover-agent-models`) in packages/pi-agent-dispatch/src/ that queries ctx.modelRegistry and returns (provider, modelId, combined `provider/modelId`) tuples. Per DEC-0019/0020 dual-surface canon, also add scripts/orchestrator/list-dispatch-models.ts CLI script. Tool description documents that output reflects models available at dispatch-time under the current auth config — not a static list. A separate decision (DEC-level) is required to clarify whether models.json should be a registry source.

gaps.title: call-agent parent_grant and requested_grant are Type.Optional but functionally required when agent declares tools — silent default-empty triggers grant violation
gaps.description: callAgentTool.parameters at packages/pi-agent-dispatch/src/call-agent-tool.ts lines 48-56 declares both parent_grant and requested_grant as Type.Optional(Type.Array(Type.String())). computeGrantViolation at packages/pi-jit-agents/src/jit-runtime.ts lines 90-95 treats absent grants as empty sets: `parentGrant ?? []`. When an agent's spec declares `tools: [read]` and call-agent is invoked without passing parent_grant, the intersection is empty and GrantViolationError fires with message 'Agent X grant violation: tools [read] not in parent grant (DEC-0047 clamp at executeAgent boundary)'. The type-optional declaration does not match the dispatch-required semantics; no orchestration-context fallback (e.g. caller's own tool set via ExtensionContext) is consulted.

evidence.reference: FB-008 'call-agent requires explicit parent_grant and requested_grant'; actual_behavior: agent with tools: [read] dispatched without parent_grant → 'Agent X grant violation: tools [read] not in parent grant (DEC-0047 clamp at executeAgent boundary)'

Investigation report FB-008 section confirms both surfaces verbatim (call-agent-tool.ts:48-56 Type.Optional declaration; jit-runtime.ts:90-95 default-empty handling); verdict 'Tool surface or DEC' — describe parent_grant as required-when-tools-declared in the schema, or compute parent_grant from orchestration context (ExtensionContext) when absent
parent_grant: Type.Optional(...) requested_grant: Type.Optional(...) — both declared optional in the tool parameter schema
computeGrantViolation: const parent = parentGrant ?? []; — silent default-empty when caller does not pass parent_grant

gaps.impact: First-time call-agent invocations for tool-enabled agent specs hard-fail at the grant clamp. The error message names the DEC-0047 clamp but not the workflow contract (always pass parent_grant). Authors cannot discover the required-when-tools semantic from the tool schema. The semantic is correct per the capability-clamp design (default-empty per DEC-0047) but the surface is mis-aligned: type-optional read as 'safe to omit' contradicts dispatch-required read as 'omission means zero grant'.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Tool surface at packages/pi-agent-dispatch/src/call-agent-tool.ts lines 48-56: parent_grant description states 'Required when the agent spec declares tools. Defaults to empty array; sub-agent tools must be an explicit subset of this grant per DEC-0047 capability-clamp.' OR: extend call-agent execute to compute parent_grant from ExtensionContext when absent, deriving the caller's full tool set as the default parent grant. DEC-level decision required between schema-documentation and runtime-derivation paths.

gaps.title: call-agent returns only status summary — agent output buried in details metadata, not surfaced to orchestrator content
gaps.description: callAgentTool.execute at packages/pi-agent-dispatch/src/call-agent-tool.ts lines 105-117 returns `{ details: result, content: [{ type: 'text', text: 'Dispatched agent X (grant=[...]); result.output type=string' }] }`. The agent's actual output text is held in `details.output` (a pi-ai AgentToolResult convention) but is not transcribed into the content array. Pi's tool-result rendering surface shows `content` to the orchestrator; `details` is treated as metadata and is not auto-rendered. The orchestrator receives the type of result.output but not its value — to read the agent's response the orchestrator must inspect session logs at .pi/agent/sessions/<project>/<timestamp>.jsonl.

evidence.reference: FB-009 'call-agent returns only a status summary — agent output not surfaced to orchestrator'; actual_behavior: tool returns text/plain status; the agent output 'I will start by reading...' exists in details.output in the session log but is not in the tool result content

Investigation report FB-009 section confirms return shape at call-agent-tool.ts:109-117 verbatim; verdict 'Tool surface (call-agent-tool.ts line 114)' — include agent output in content as additional text block; concrete fix-code shown using `typeof result.output === 'string' ? result.output : JSON.stringify(result.output, null, 2)`
return { details: result, content: [{ type: 'text', text: 'Dispatched agent X; result.output type=string' }] }

gaps.impact: Orchestrators dispatching agents and expecting to consume their output cannot do so via the tool result. Workflows that chain agent invocations (output of agent A becomes input of agent B) must implement out-of-band reading (session-log inspection, substrate side-effects). Compounds with FGAP for FB-016 (no dispatch-history query tool) — the only path to the output requires manual log inspection.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Code at packages/pi-agent-dispatch/src/call-agent-tool.ts lines 105-117: append agent output to the content array. Concrete shape per investigation verdict: `const outputText = typeof result.output === 'string' ? result.output : JSON.stringify(result.output, null, 2); content: [{type: 'text', text: 'Dispatched agent X (grant=...):\n\n' + outputText}]`. The change is purely additive at the dispatch boundary; `details` continues to carry the structured result for downstream consumers that want the typed shape.

gaps.title: contextBlocks whole-block injection is all-or-nothing raw JSON — no field projection, filtering, or pagination
gaps.description: wrapBlockContent at packages/pi-jit-agents/src/compile.ts line 162 stringifies the entire block content via `JSON.stringify(content, null, 2)` and wraps it in a context_block XML tag. The bare-string contextBlocks entry form (`contextBlocks: ['requirements']`) injects every field of every item in the block. There is no projection (select specific fields), no filtering (where-clause across items), no pagination (limit/offset), and no per-item macro-rendered option. Template authors can prune in Nunjucks at template-render time, but the full block JSON is read and stringified into the template context regardless of agent needs.

evidence.reference: FB-010 'contextBlocks whole-block injection is all-or-nothing raw JSON'; actual_behavior: contextBlocks: ['requirements'] injects all fields of all items as JSON; even if agent only needs IDs, it receives descriptions, acceptance criteria, status, priority etc for every item

Investigation report FB-010 section confirms wrapBlockContent at compile.ts:162 verbatim; verdict 'Schema + tool surface' — extend bare-string entry to support optional fields[] projection and depth (triggers auto-render); compileAgent auto-applies render_recursive when depth > 0
wrapBlockContent stringifies the entire block via JSON.stringify(content, null, 2) — no projection or filtering applied

gaps.impact: Agent dispatches injecting large blocks (40-60 items with rich field schemas) pay substantial token cost on every invocation. The cost is paid even when the agent only needs a thin view (e.g. IDs to avoid duplicates). Template-level Nunjucks pruning trims rendered output but does not avoid reading the full block into the template context.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Schema + tool surface per investigation verdict. Schema: extend the contextBlocks entry to accept an object form with optional fields — `{ name: string, fields?: string[], depth?: number }` — with bare-string remaining as sugar for `{ name }`. Tool surface at packages/pi-jit-agents/src/compile.ts line 162: wrapBlockContent reads the optional fields array and projects the block content to those fields before stringify. compileAgent auto-applies render_recursive when depth > 0. Documentation in tool description + schema comment clarifies that contextBlocks accepts bare-string (whole block, no projection) or object form (projected fields, optional auto-render).

gaps.title: contextBlocks single-item object form depth field is stored but render_recursive is not auto-applied
gaps.description: Object-form contextBlocks entries `{ name, item, depth }` at packages/pi-jit-agents/src/compile.ts line 460 wrap the referenced item via wrapItemContent (line 180+) — raw JSON inside a context_block XML tag. The depth field is preserved on the injected array element (compile.ts line 469: `depth: ref.depth ?? 0`) as metadata but does NOT auto-trigger render_recursive. Template authors must explicitly call `render_recursive(resolve(id), _<name>_depth)` to get the per-item-macro rendered view that the depth field promises. The depth metadata exists but is plumbing-only; rendering still requires explicit author action.

evidence.reference: FB-011 'contextBlocks single-item injection is raw JSON — render_recursive must be called explicitly'; actual_behavior: _requirements_item contains raw JSON wrapped in context_block XML; _requirements_depth contains the specified depth; template must call render_recursive to get macro-rendered output

Investigation report FB-011 section confirms both surfaces verbatim (compile.ts:460 wrapItemContent invocation; compile.ts:469 depth metadata storage; compile.ts:180-187 wrapItemContent raw-JSON wrapping); verdict 'Tool surface' — modify compile.ts to detect depth > 0 on object-form entries and wrap returned item with render_recursive invocation
wrapItemContent wraps as raw JSON; depth: ref.depth ?? 0 stored as metadata only — no render_recursive auto-application

gaps.impact: Authors expecting depth > 0 to produce a pre-rendered, macro-formatted view receive raw JSON instead. The per-item rendering infrastructure (FEAT-001 array_key column, CANONICAL_MACRO_NAMES registry) is built but the most natural ergonomic — 'I asked for depth 2, I expect a 2-deep recursive render' — requires manual template glue.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Tool surface at packages/pi-jit-agents/src/compile.ts line 460 (wrapItemContent invocation site) and line 469 (array element shape): when ref.depth > 0, wrap the returned item with a render_recursive call invoking the kind's per-item macro at the declared depth, instead of storing raw JSON with depth as unused metadata. Bare-string contextBlocks form remains unchanged (no item, no depth semantic). Sibling to FGAP-161 fix for FB-010 — the auto-render behavior would also be available to bare-string projection per FGAP-161's `depth` extension.

gaps.title: No tool to author per-item macros for custom block kinds — custom-field rendering requires manual filesystem access outside substrate
gaps.description: Per-item macros live at .pi/templates/items/<kind>.md, discovered by the renderer registry at packages/pi-jit-agents/src/renderer-registry.ts line 134 via the three-tier search (project / user / builtin). Builtin macros ship for the 14 canonical block kinds (requirements, decisions, tasks, etc.). Custom block kinds with custom fields — for example a requirements schema extended with `hour_system` and `traces_to_spec_doc` — have no per-item macro authoring tool. Authors must create the macro file by direct filesystem write outside the substrate's tool surface. The renderer registry exposes a programmatic register() API but no Pi tool wraps it. There is no symmetric counterpart to author-agent-spec / write-schema for macros.

evidence.reference: FB-012 'No tool to author per-item macros for custom block kinds'; actual_behavior: render-item-by-id(REQ-001) produces output using builtin render_requirement macro which ignores custom fields hour_system and traces_to_spec_doc; no way to create project-level override via tools

Investigation report FB-012 section confirms renderer-registry.ts:134 three-tier discovery verbatim; verdict 'Tool surface (new tool)' — author-macro symmetric to author-agent-spec / write-schema; reads block schema, generates default macro covering all fields, writes to .pi/templates/items/<kind>.md; FGAP-119 (dogfood composites) blocks on this capability
const projectDir = path.join(cwd, '.pi', 'templates'); — three-tier search root, but no tool to write to it

gaps.impact: Custom block kinds (any schema extension beyond the 14 builtin kinds) lose canonical rendering. The substrate appears to support custom kinds at the schema layer but downgrades them to raw-JSON rendering at the per-item macro layer. Dogfood arc FGAP-119 (composite-tool authoring) is blocked because custom-field rendering is required for the composites' substrates to render usefully.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Tool surface — new Pi tool `author-macro` in packages/pi-jit-agents/src/ (or in pi-agent-dispatch following the author-* convention). Tool reads the target block kind's schema, generates a default macro body iterating all schema fields, writes to .pi/templates/items/<kind>.md. Human can then edit to customize rendering. Per the investigation verdict, FGAP-119 (dogfood composites) should be re-scoped to depend on this tool's completion since custom macros are required to render custom-field substrates correctly.

gaps.title: gather-execution-context requires hand-authored context-contract entry — no schema-derived default
gaps.description: gatherExecutionContext at packages/pi-context/src/execution-context.ts line 172 returns `{ error: 'no context-contract for kind: <kind>' }` when no matching CTX-NNN entry exists in the context-contracts block for the unit's kind. The function design (lines 143+) reads the unit by unitId, reads the matching context-contract entry, then walks each declared relation_type bidirectionally. Authors invoking gather-execution-context for any unit kind must first manually create a CTX-NNN entry declaring relation types and depths. No default context-contract is synthesized from the block's schema (which already declares the relation types the kind participates in).

evidence.reference: FB-013 'gather-execution-context requires a context-contract entry'; actual_behavior: gather-execution-context with unitId REQ-001 and kind requirement returns {error: 'no context-contract for kind: requirement'}

Investigation report FB-013 section confirms execution-context.ts:172 error path verbatim; verdict 'Schema + tool surface' — new tool author-context-contract symmetric to author-agent-spec, generating default contract (all relation_types the kind participates in, depth 1); optional auto-derive flag in gatherExecutionContext
return { error: 'no context-contract for kind: ${args.kind}' }; — hard-errors on missing contract

gaps.impact: First-time use of gather-execution-context for any unit kind fails until the author manually authors a CTX-NNN entry. The schema already declares the relation_types the kind participates in; the framework does not synthesize the corresponding contract automatically. Authors must learn the context-contract schema and write entries before the gather tool produces useful output.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Schema + tool surface per investigation verdict. Tool surface: new Pi tool `author-context-contract` reading the target block kind's schema, generating a default CTX-NNN entry declaring all relation_types the kind participates in at default depth 1. Schema: extend gatherExecutionContext at packages/pi-context/src/execution-context.ts to accept an optional auto-derive flag that synthesizes a default contract on the fly when no entry exists. Authors can override defaults via author-context-contract when the synthesized contract is wrong for their use case.

gaps.title: Zero-token agent dispatches report as success — silent empty output with no framework-level error
gaps.description: executeAgent at packages/pi-jit-agents/src/jit-runtime.ts calls completeFn (pi-ai complete) once and extracts the response. usageFromMessage at lines 404-417 extracts usage.input/usage.output but does not check whether either is zero. If the pi-ai complete call returns an empty AssistantMessage (zero tokens, empty content array) — for example when the model is in the registry but unavailable at the provider, or the provider quota is exhausted, or the API silently rejects the request — the framework returns the empty result as a successful dispatch. call-agent at packages/pi-agent-dispatch/src/call-agent-tool.ts reports 'result.output type=string' with empty content. The orchestrator cannot distinguish 'agent intentionally produced no output' from 'API failed silently'.

evidence.reference: FB-015 'Zero-token dispatches with wrong model produce no error — silent empty output'; actual_behavior: dispatch with model 'claude-sonnet-4-20250514' (no provider prefix) → anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-20250514 → model not found → zero tokens, empty output, reported as success

Investigation report FB-015 section confirms usageFromMessage at jit-runtime.ts:404-417 verbatim; verdict 'Runtime boundary (jit-runtime.ts)' — after extracting usage, check `usage.totalTokens === 0` and throw AgentDispatchError with stopReason and model details; partial confirmation noted because the modelRegistry.find path at call-agent-tool.ts:87 errors before dispatch when model is missing — the zero-token path applies when model IS in registry but the API returns empty
usageFromMessage extracts usage.input/output/cost; no zero-token check on the returned message

gaps.impact: Orchestrators relying on agent output for downstream decisions cannot detect when the agent produced nothing. Silent API failures (model unavailable, quota exhausted, provider rejection) are indistinguishable from successful empty dispatches. Compounds with FGAP for FB-009 (output not surfaced in content) and FGAP for FB-016 (no dispatch-history query tool): even the workaround of inspecting details or session logs requires the orchestrator to first know that something is wrong.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Runtime boundary at packages/pi-jit-agents/src/jit-runtime.ts after the response is extracted: check `usageFromMessage(response).input + .output === 0` (or `usage.totalTokens === 0`); throw AgentDispatchError with the model identifier and the response.stopReason instead of returning success. Concrete shape from investigation verdict: `throw new AgentDispatchError(compiled.spec.name, 'zero-token response from model ' + modelToString(dispatch.model) + ' — possible API failure or model unavailability', { stopReason: response.stopReason })`.

gaps.title: No tool to query agent dispatch history — session logs are the only audit trail
gaps.description: Agent dispatch details (model, provider, token usage, raw API response, agent output, stopReason) are persisted via safeWriteTrace at packages/pi-jit-agents/src/jit-runtime.ts lines 204-212 to .pi/agent/sessions/<project>/<timestamp>.jsonl files. No Pi tool reads or queries these traces. The trace pipeline is write-only audit storage. Orchestrators wanting to inspect recent dispatches must shell out to grep the JSONL files, parse them manually, and filter by agent name or timestamp.

evidence.reference: FB-016 'Session logs are the only place to see agent dispatch details — no tool to query them'; actual_behavior: after call-agent returns status summary, the only way to see agent's output (e.g. 'I will start by reading...') is to grep the session log for toolName call-agent entries and manually parse the JSONL

Investigation report FB-016 section confirms safeWriteTrace at jit-runtime.ts:204-212 verbatim and the absence of a query surface; verdict 'Tool surface (pi-agent-dispatch)' — new tool query-agent-dispatch(agent_name?, limit?, offset?) returning {dispatches: [{timestamp, agent, model, output, usage, stopReason}]} by scanning .pi/agent/sessions/, parsing JSONL, filtering, sorting by timestamp desc, paginating

gaps.impact: Orchestrators have no in-band way to inspect what recently dispatched agents produced, which models they used, what their token costs were, or whether they completed normally. Debugging agent behavior requires manual filesystem operations outside the substrate / tool surface. Compounds with FGAP-160 (FB-009 output not surfaced) and FGAP-165 (FB-015 zero-token silent success) — without a query tool there is no recovery path from those gaps.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Tool surface — new Pi tool `query-agent-dispatch` in packages/pi-agent-dispatch/src/ with signature `(agent_name?: string, limit?: number = 10, offset?: number = 0) -> { dispatches: [{timestamp, agent, model, output, usage, stopReason}] }`. Implementation: scan .pi/agent/sessions/<project>/ directories, parse JSONL entries, filter by agent_name when provided, sort by timestamp descending, return paginated results. Per DEC-0019/0020 dual-surface canon, also add scripts/orchestrator/query-agent-dispatch.ts CLI script.

gaps.title: Substrate schema validation errors reference array indices, not item ids — debugging requires manual cross-reference
gaps.description: ValidationError at packages/pi-context/src/schema-validator.ts lines 82-93 formats AJV errors as `${e.instancePath || ''}: ${e.message}`. The instancePath is a JSON Pointer (e.g. `/feedback/10`) referencing the array index of the violating item, not its semantic id. For blocks with many items, locating the offending entry requires counting array elements. The schema validator does not enrich the error path with the item's `id` field when present (which all canonical block schemas declare).

evidence.reference: FB-017 'Substrate schema validation rejects items missing optional fields'; actual_behavior: append-block-item rejected FB-011 with '/feedback/10: must have required property status' — requires counting items to identify the offending entry; expected_behavior: errors should include item's id field when present like '/feedback/FB-011: must have required property status'

Investigation report FB-017 section confirms ValidationError constructor at schema-validator.ts:82-93 verbatim; verdict 'Tool surface / error formatting' — enhance ValidationError to include item id when the violated item has an id field; AJV reports JSON Pointer paths per JSON Schema spec, no enrichment layer currently maps index back to semantic id
ValidationError constructor; details map formats `${e.instancePath}: ${e.message}` without item-id enrichment

gaps.impact: Authors writing blocks with schema-required fields receive correct but unergonomic error messages. The error points to the right structural location (the array index) but not to the semantic identifier (the item id) that authors actually search for. Debugging cost scales with block size — locating the offending entry in a 50-item block requires manual counting.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Code at packages/pi-context/src/schema-validator.ts lines 82-93: enhance ValidationError constructor to inspect the data structure at the failing instancePath, extract the item's `id` field when present, and substitute it in the error message. Concrete shape: when instancePath matches `/<arrayKey>/<index>(/.*)?`, look up the item at the index in the validated data, read its `id` field, replace `/<arrayKey>/<index>` with `/<arrayKey>/<id>` in the rendered message. Existing JSON Pointer form retained on the ErrorObject for programmatic consumers.

gaps.title: output.format: text combined with tools[] is structurally allowed but produces prose-only output — no compile-time conflict check
gaps.description: An agent spec declaring both `output.format: text` and `tools: [...]` passes schema validation but produces incoherent dispatch behavior. The `tools` array in an agent spec is purely declarative — executeAgent at packages/pi-jit-agents/src/jit-runtime.ts does NOT wire the declared tools into the model context unless `outputSchema` is set (which triggers the phantom-tool forced-toolChoice path at lines 566-570). With `output.format: text`, no outputSchema is set, no phantom tool is registered, no forced toolChoice is applied; the model is free to emit prose. The model sees the system/task prompts (which may mention tools by name) but no actual tools wired in. The model defaults to describing what it would do rather than invoking tool calls. The framework does not validate the conflict; no warning is issued.

evidence.reference: FB-018 'output.format: text combined with tools biases model toward prose description instead of tool invocation'; actual_behavior: spec-requirements-miner with output.format: text, tools: [read, append-block-item] produced 'I will start by reading...' with zero tool calls; the model described its plan instead of executing it

Investigation report FB-018 section confirms compile.ts compileAgent + jit-runtime.ts:566-570 verbatim; verdict 'Compile-time validation (compile.ts compileAgent function)' — add check: if spec.outputSchema && spec.outputFormat === 'text' throw AgentCompileError; if spec.tools.length > 0 && spec.outputFormat === 'text' console.warn; alternatively schema-level mutual exclusion

Investigation report Single-Turn-Dispatch section CONFIRMS executeAgent contains NO LOOP — single try block with ONE completeFn call; tool invocation is the responsibility of the ORCHESTRATOR, not executeAgent; the declared tools array in the spec is purely declarative; this is the architectural context that makes FB-018 a configuration trap, not a runtime bug

gaps.impact: Agent authors declare a spec believing it will iteratively invoke its declared tools; the spec passes schema validation, dispatches without error, and produces prose that describes a plan instead of taking action. The framework provides no diagnostic. The author cannot tell from the spec, the schema, the dispatch result, or the documentation that text + tools is incoherent. This intersects with the architectural single-turn-dispatch property of executeAgent (also confirmed in the source report) — tools declared in the spec are not auto-wired to the model; the orchestrator owns the loop. Authors writing tool-using agents must understand both axioms.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Code at packages/pi-jit-agents/src/compile.ts compileAgent function — add compile-time validation per investigation verdict: `if (spec.outputSchema && spec.outputFormat === 'text') throw new AgentCompileError(spec.name, 'conflicting output.format text and outputSchema; use output.format json or remove outputSchema')`; `if (spec.tools && spec.tools.length > 0 && spec.outputFormat === 'text') console.warn('[pi-jit-agents] agent <name>: output.format text with tools[] may bias model away from tool use; consider output.format json')`. Schema-level alternative: make `outputSchema` and `outputFormat: text` mutually exclusive in the AgentSpec schema, or require `outputFormat: json` when tools are declared. The schema-level fix is stronger but requires FGAP-156 (canonical-shape parity) to land first.

gaps.title: Agent-spec tools[] is structurally unreachable from LLM — declared tools stripped at grant-clamp boundary (line 488) and never delivered (line 568 wires only phantom-tool when outputSchema set); implementation gap relative to canonical body (FEAT-005/006/DEC-0047/JI-029)
gaps.description: executeAgent at packages/pi-jit-agents/src/jit-runtime.ts strips the agent's declared tools[] at the grant-clamp boundary and never delivers them to the LLM. Verified via grep `compiled\.tools|context\.tools|options\.tools` returning exactly two hits: line 488 `computeGrantViolation(compiled.tools, dispatch.parentGrant)` (grant clamp; throws if child not subset of parent; tools then discarded), and line 568 `context.tools = [phantomTool]` ONLY inside `if (compiled.outputSchema)` (delivers only the framework's phantom-tool for forced toolChoice). Single completeFn call at line 572. The agent's declared tools[] is structurally unreachable from the LLM. An agent declaring tools: [read, append-block-item] cannot read files, cannot append to substrate, cannot do anything beyond emit text (or schema-bound JSON when outputSchema is set). The framework's canonical body — FEAT-005 ('subagent receives its OWN tools'), FEAT-006 ('privileged-agent code-change loop'), DEC-0047 (capability-grant model: grants clamped at dispatch then exercised by the agent), TASK-091 ('agent does the work'), JI-029 ('jit agent with perms for read/amend blocks') — declares jit-agents as tool-using agents acting on granted capabilities. The current implementation does not honor that canonical body. jit-agents-spec.md describes the dispatch as phantom-tool-only; the spec doc is the outlier substrate text relative to the canonical body and is being amended to match.

evidence.reference: Single-Turn Dispatch (No Tool-Result Loop) section; verdict 'CONFIRMED (with architectural context)'; verbatim: 'executeAgent contains NO LOOP over tool calls and results'; 'grep confirms no while, for, or iteration over tool calls'; 'Tool invocation is the responsibility of the ORCHESTRATOR (the caller of executeAgent), not executeAgent itself'; 'the declared tools array in the spec is purely declarative — it declares what tools the agent intends to use, but executeAgent does NOT wire them to the model'
Single try block with ONE completeFn invocation; no iteration; ONE response extracted and returned
Phantom-tool registration when outputSchema is set; the phantom tool is the framework's structured-output constraint, not the spec's declared tools

gaps.impact: Every existing agent spec declaring tools[] is broken-by-architecture. clock-menu-app's 4 agent specs (spec-issues-miner, spec-decisions-drafter, spec-requirements-miner, test-reader) all declare tools[] expecting to invoke them; none can. The capability-grant infrastructure landed via FEAT-004/005 + DEC-0047 + TASK-089 (composeToolGrant, clamp semantics, author-tool-grant) is functionally no-op at agent dispatch — capabilities are composed and clamped at line 488 then discarded with the tools at line 568. The 'agent that does things with tools' framework intent (FEAT-006 north-star, JI-029 verbatim) is unmet by the implementation. Authors writing agent specs under the natural reading of tools[] receive prose-only output and discover the cliff at first dispatch with no framework-level signal.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Implementation work: extend executeAgent at packages/pi-jit-agents/src/jit-runtime.ts to wire compiled.tools (intersected with dispatch.parentGrant) into the LLM's tool registry, iterate over tool-call responses, dispatch tool invocations through ExtensionContext, append results to message history, re-call the model until end_turn or max-turns. Substrate work: amend jit-agents-spec.md to describe multi-tool dispatch as canonical and classifier-pattern (forced phantom-tool toolChoice for structured output) as one mode-among-modes. Verification: re-run clock-menu-app's 4 agent specs as regression-positives — they succeed after this lands. Interlocks with FGAP-115 (ExtensionContext.currentModel/currentProvider absent; multi-tool dispatch needs the tool-execution surface that ExtensionContext provides).

gaps.title: No JSON Schema file for AgentSpec — round-trip parseAgentYaml validation does not enforce semantic correctness or enable schema-driven tooling
gaps.description: author-agent-spec validates by round-tripping written YAML through parseAgentYaml at packages/pi-agent-dispatch/src/author-agent-spec-tool.ts. No explicit JSON Schema file for AgentSpec exists in the pi-jit-agents package (no agent-spec.schema.json bundled alongside the verdict.schema.json for monitor classification). The TypeScript AgentSpec interface at packages/pi-jit-agents/src/types.ts is the only canonical-shape declaration. Without a schema artifact: (a) semantic validation (does the template path exist; is the output schema reference valid; are the contextBlocks block-kind names registered) has no declarative source; (b) external tooling (UI scaffolds, form generation, schema browsers) has nothing to consume; (c) the type and the parser can drift (FGAP-156) with no schema-level reconciliation.

evidence.reference: Discovered-gap #2: 'Absence of agent-spec.schema.json: Author-agent-spec round-trips through parseAgentYaml for validation, but no explicit JSON Schema file for AgentSpec is registered in the codebase. The type exists (types.ts) but the schema is implicit or missing. This blocks semantic validation (does template path exist?) and schema-driven tooling (UI, form generation, schema browsers).'
AgentSpec TypeScript interface is the only canonical-shape declaration; no parallel JSON Schema file in the package

gaps.impact: AgentSpec authoring, validation, and tooling all rely on the TypeScript type as implicit truth. The substrate's other surfaces (block kinds, framework schemas) ship JSON Schemas that validate written data; AgentSpec does not. The asymmetry blocks schema-aware tooling, prevents declarative semantic checks, and lets type-vs-parser divergence (FGAP-156) persist without a schema-level reconciliation point.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Author a JSON Schema file `packages/pi-jit-agents/schemas/agent-spec.schema.json` declaring the canonical AgentSpec shape (post-FGAP-156 decision on top-level vs nested prompt fields). Bundle alongside verdict.schema.json. Wire AJV validation through parseAgentYaml (or as a pre-validation step in author-agent-spec). The schema becomes the canonical source for: tooling consumers; field-level semantic validation (FGAP-155 closure depends on this for templatePath existence checks); FGAP-156 canonical-shape parity enforcement.

gaps.title: AgentSpec inline-prompt and template-prompt fields are parallel optional with no mutual-exclusion enforcement
gaps.description: AgentSpec at packages/pi-jit-agents/src/types.ts declares `systemPrompt?: string`, `systemPromptTemplate?: string`, `taskPrompt?: string`, `taskPromptTemplate?: string` as parallel optional fields. Both inline and template variants can be populated simultaneously without type-system or schema rejection. The parser at packages/pi-jit-agents/src/agent-spec.ts resolves with the heuristic in resolvePromptField — which can fire incorrectly (FGAP-153 / FB-001) and which silently chooses one branch when both could apply. No oneOf or discriminated-union construct enforces that exactly one of the two variants is present per prompt slot.

evidence.reference: Discovered-gap #3: 'Shape inconsistency in AgentSpec across consumers: The TypeScript type declares both systemPrompt/taskPrompt (inline) and systemPromptTemplate/taskPromptTemplate (file paths) as parallel optional fields. This optional-pairing design allows both to be present simultaneously, creating ambiguity. The parser chooses one or the other heuristically (FB-001); the type does not enforce mutual exclusion. A oneOf or discriminated-union schema would prevent this.'
AgentSpec declares systemPrompt? + systemPromptTemplate? + taskPrompt? + taskPromptTemplate? as parallel optional fields with no mutual-exclusion construct

gaps.impact: Authors can construct AgentSpec values where both systemPrompt and systemPromptTemplate are populated; the parser silently chooses one; the other is dropped without warning. Combined with FGAP-153 (heuristic over-firing) the dropped variant can be the one the author intended. The shape permits invalid combinations that the canonical contract should reject.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Schema-level — when FGAP-170 (AgentSpec JSON Schema) lands, declare the prompt fields as a oneOf / discriminated-union construct that enforces exactly one of inline-or-template per prompt slot. Concrete shapes per FGAP-156 decision: if nested shape is chosen, `prompt: { system: { oneOf: [{inline: string}, {template: string}] }, task: { oneOf: [...] } }`; if top-level fields are chosen, JSON Schema `oneOf` over the systemPrompt/systemPromptTemplate pair and the taskPrompt/taskPromptTemplate pair. Code-level: extend parseAgentYaml at packages/pi-jit-agents/src/agent-spec.ts to throw when both variants are populated for the same prompt slot, prior to the resolvePromptField heuristic.

gaps.title: Agent-spec template-path heuristic bypasses canonical resolveContextDir(cwd) resolver established in FGAP-026 closure
gaps.description: Agent-spec template-path resolution at packages/pi-jit-agents/src/agent-spec.ts treats template paths as filesystem-relative-to-spec-dir at parse time. The canonical substrate-dir resolver `resolveContextDir(cwd)` established in FGAP-026 closure (DEC-0015 enacted) is the canonical path resolution surface for substrate paths. Agent-spec template paths bypass this canon — the parse-time path interpretation is heuristic, not routed through the same resolver that project-context paths use. The bypass creates two inconsistent path-handling conventions inside the same framework.

evidence.reference: Discovered-gap #1: 'FGAP-026 (closed): Context-dir resolver is canonical; all path construction should use resolveContextDir(cwd). The hardcoded template-path heuristic bypasses this canon (templates are assumed to be relative to spec dir, resolved at parse-time). Agent-spec path resolution should flow through the canonical resolver for consistency with project-context canon.'
resolvePromptField + template-path interpretation lives at parse-time inside agent-spec.ts; no call to resolveContextDir

gaps.impact: Path-handling conventions inside pi-jit-agents and pi-context diverge. Substrate consumers benefit from FGAP-026's canonical resolver (cascading bootstrap pointer, configurable substrate-dir name); agent-spec consumers do not. Cross-package code that needs to resolve both types of paths must understand two separate conventions. Substrate relocation (.context vs .pi-context vs custom name) does not automatically apply to agent-spec template paths.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Code at packages/pi-jit-agents/src/agent-spec.ts: route agent-spec template-path resolution through the canonical resolveContextDir(cwd) resolver (or an equivalent agent-spec-dir resolver that delegates to the same canon). The fix presumes FGAP-153 closure (narrowing the resolvePromptField heuristic) — the resolver change is on the template branch only, not on the inline branch. Schema-level: FGAP-170 (AgentSpec JSON Schema) documents path-resolution semantics so callers know which paths flow through which resolver.

gaps.title: author-agent-spec in-body writer.kind=='human' check is redundant with FGAP-134/138 auth-gate at pi-dispatch layer
gaps.description: author-agent-spec at packages/pi-agent-dispatch/src/author-agent-spec-tool.ts performs an in-body `if writer.kind !== 'human' throw` check. Per FGAP-134 (closed 2026-05-29) the canonical per-tool user-auth gate is at the pi-dispatch layer (auth-gate.ts intercepting 14+ Bucket-2 tools); per FGAP-138 (closed 2026-05-29) the auth-gate mutates event.input.writer to the verified terminal-operator identity on confirm. The in-body check is now structural redundancy — the auth-gate already enforces human-authorized invocation and verifies operator identity. The redundant check predates FGAP-134/138 and lingers with docstring framing that still describes it as the primary enforcement.

evidence.reference: Discovered-gap (secondary) #4: 'author-agent-spec writer.kind enforcement: Per FGAP-134 (closed 2026-05-29), author-agent-spec performs an in-body if writer.kind !== human throw check that is now redundant with the canonical auth-gate at pi-dispatch layer. The gate is moved but the redundant in-body check remains, and its docstring still describes it as the primary enforcement (misleading per FGAP-136 closure). Cleanup: remove in-body check or clarify it as defense-in-depth only.'
author-agent-spec-tool source contains the in-body writer.kind enforcement; FGAP-134/138 closure moved the canonical enforcement to packages/pi-agent-dispatch/src/auth-gate.ts

gaps.impact: Structural redundancy and misleading docstrings. Two enforcement layers exist for the same human-authorization concern; the canonical one (auth-gate) is what actually fires the user-confirm prompt. The in-body check is now a no-op for any invocation that already passed the gate, and is misleading reading for agents debugging authorization behavior — they see the in-body check and may infer it is primary.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Code at packages/pi-agent-dispatch/src/author-agent-spec-tool.ts: strip the in-body writer.kind=='human' check (the auth-gate at packages/pi-agent-dispatch/src/auth-gate.ts is the canonical enforcement per FGAP-134/138 closure). Tool body trusts the (now-verified) writer field stamped by the auth-gate. Equivalent cleanup precedent landed at FGAP-138 closure (commit 3c9e8e4) for author-agent-spec / author-tool-grant / write-schema-migration; this gap is the inverse cleanup-not-yet-applied audit covering whether the cleanup at FGAP-138 actually stripped all sites or if any in-body check remains.

gaps.title: author-agent-spec has no semantic-validation surface for template-path existence, schema-referenced files, or contextBlocks block-kind existence
gaps.description: author-agent-spec at packages/pi-agent-dispatch/src/author-agent-spec-tool.ts validates by round-tripping written YAML through parseAgentYaml. The round-trip catches structural malformedness (missing required fields, wrong types) but does NOT verify semantic correctness of cross-reference fields: (a) systemPromptTemplate / taskPromptTemplate file existence (or `block:` sentinel validity); (b) output.schema reference resolution (existence of the bundled schema file or the cross-package schema reference); (c) contextBlocks block-kind name existence in the project's installed block-kind catalog. Configuration errors in any of these surface at dispatch-time (ENOENT for missing templates, AJV schema-not-found for invalid schema refs, runtime error for unknown block kinds) instead of at author-time.

evidence.reference: Discovered-gap (secondary) #5: 'Validation surface for agent specs at author-agent-spec: The tool validates by round-tripping but does NOT verify semantic correctness (template-path existence, schema-referenced files, output schema files, contextBlocks block-kind existence). A richer validation surface (similar to the real-checks pattern in FGAP-102) would catch configuration errors at author-time rather than dispatch-time.'
author-agent-spec entry point; round-trip validation via parseAgentYaml only; no cross-reference semantic checks

gaps.impact: Authors receive author-time success for specs that will hard-fail on first dispatch. The validation gate's promise (round-trip success = dispatchable spec) is structurally weaker than authors assume. Each cross-reference field is a separate failure-mode; without a semantic-validation surface, authors learn each one only by hitting the dispatch-time error.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Tool surface at packages/pi-agent-dispatch/src/author-agent-spec-tool.ts: after parseAgentYaml round-trip succeeds, run a semantic-validation pass per cross-reference field: (a) for each template path that is not a `block:` sentinel, verify the file exists on disk via fs.existsSync; (b) for output.schema, resolve the reference (bundled schema path or block: sentinel) and verify the target exists; (c) for each contextBlocks entry, verify the named block kind exists in the project's installed block-kind catalog (queryable via pi-context's availableBlocks(cwd) or schemaVocabulary(cwd)). On any failure, throw at author-time with a message naming the failing field + the resolution attempt. Pattern analog: the FGAP-102 real-checks pattern that surfaces deterministic failures pre-dispatch.

gaps.title: No tool to discover available agent templates or emit a canonical agent-spec scaffold — author-agent-spec requires constructing a complete AgentSpec from scratch
gaps.description: author-agent-spec at packages/pi-agent-dispatch/src/author-agent-spec-tool.ts expects the author to pass a complete AgentSpec object. There is no companion tool to: (a) list pre-existing agent templates that the author can clone-and-customize; (b) emit a canonical agent-spec scaffold pre-populated with required fields and sensible defaults that the author can edit. The substrate-block authoring surface has analogous discoverability — append-block-item accepts arbitrary block items but the substrate's pi-context surface exposes availableBlocks(cwd), blockStructure(cwd), schemaInfo(cwd, name), and the canonical samples catalog at packages/pi-context/samples/. Agent-spec authoring has no equivalent discovery surface.

evidence.reference: Discovered-gap (secondary) #6: 'No tool to discover available agent templates or emit canonical agent-spec templates: Author-agent-spec requires the author to construct a complete AgentSpec object. There is no tool to list available agent templates, clone a template, or emit a scaffold. Compared to the substrate-block authoring surface (which has append-block-item + implicit schema defaults), agent-spec authoring has no equivalent scaffold surface.'
author-agent-spec expects complete AgentSpec object as input; no scaffold or template-discovery companion tool exists in pi-agent-dispatch

gaps.impact: First-time authors of an agent spec have no canonical starting shape. They must read source for the AgentSpec type, infer required vs optional fields, and construct the JSON / YAML representation from scratch. Cross-package consumers building agent-spec ergonomics (UI scaffolds, workflow generators) have no programmatic discovery surface for available templates.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Tool surface — two new Pi tools in packages/pi-agent-dispatch/src/: (a) `list-agent-templates` returning canonical agent templates bundled in pi-jit-agents (or registered in the project's .pi/templates/agents/ tree); (b) `scaffold-agent-spec` taking a minimal input (name, optional template-name) and emitting a canonical AgentSpec object pre-populated with required fields and sensible defaults — author can then call author-agent-spec with the scaffolded object. Dual-surface CLI scripts per DEC-0019/0020 canon. Prerequisite: FGAP-170 (AgentSpec JSON Schema) provides the field-default declarations.

gaps.title: AgentSpec optional-field type-vs-runtime asymmetry is structural — FB-005 (model) is one instance; thinking / extensions / skills require audit
gaps.description: AgentSpec at packages/pi-jit-agents/src/types.ts declares multiple fields as optional with a `?` marker: model?, thinking?, tools?, extensions?, skills?. Runtime behavior is not uniform across these optionals: model is dispatch-required (call-agent-tool.ts:83 throws 'no model specified' when absent — FGAP-157 / FB-005); thinking is silently dropped when toolChoice is forced (DEC-0002 enacted; TASK-086 implementation pending); tools' empty-array is a valid runtime state with no error (not a defect); extensions and skills have unclear optional-vs-required semantics. The pattern is structural — type-optional markers do not document runtime contract — and recurs across the spec. FB-005 is the one instance an external user surfaced; the broader audit has not been performed.

evidence.reference: Discovered-gap #4: 'Type vs. runtime asymmetry on optional fields is structural. AgentSpec has multiple optional fields (model?, thinking?, tools?, extensions?, skills?) that are effectively required at dispatch or have silent fallbacks: model required at dispatch (FB-005); thinking silently dropped when toolChoice is forced (DEC-0002, fix in TASK-086); tools empty array valid (not a gap); extensions / skills unclear if optional or required; needs audit. Relation to FB-005: This is a broader class of which FB-005 is one instance. The pattern suggests a need for clearer contract between AgentSpec type definition and runtime enforcement.'

Single-Turn-Dispatch Verdict context: 'The declared tools array in the spec is purely declarative — it declares what tools the agent intends to use, but executeAgent does NOT wire them to the model'; tools field has its own asymmetry (declarative-vs-wiring) layered on top of optional-vs-required
AgentSpec interface declaring all named fields with optional `?` markers; no per-field documentation of runtime contract

gaps.impact: Authors cannot rely on the AgentSpec type as documentation for runtime contract. Each optional field has its own semantic — some default-required, some silently-dropped, some silently-no-op, some unknown. Building tooling on top of the type (UI form generation, schema-driven validators, spec linters) yields incorrect results because the type's optional markers misrepresent reality. FB-005 (model) is the one externally-reported instance; closing FB-005 / FGAP-157 alone does not address the structural pattern.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Audit + documentation. Audit step: enumerate every AgentSpec optional field at packages/pi-jit-agents/src/types.ts; for each field document the actual runtime contract (required-at-dispatch / silently-dropped-on-condition / valid-when-empty / unknown-pending-investigation). Documentation step: per-field JSDoc on the AgentSpec interface declares runtime semantics, not just shape. Per FGAP-170 (AgentSpec JSON Schema) closure, schema field annotations propagate the same runtime-contract documentation to schema consumers. Code-level fixes per-field follow the pattern of FGAP-157 (FB-005 model resolution): pick Option A (make required in type) or Option B (add runtime fallback) per field.

gaps.title: jit-agents-spec.md is the outlier substrate text — describes phantom-tool-only dispatch contradicting canonical body (FEAT-005/006/DEC-0047/TASK-091/JI-029) which declares tool-using agents; spec doc is being amended to match canonical body
gaps.description: jit-agents-spec.md at docs/planning/jit-agents-spec.md describes the dispatch as phantom-tool-only — verbatim: 'executeAgent is unified in-process dispatch. Phantom tool enforcement via buildPhantomTool + forced toolChoice'; 'The same dispatch runtime sends it to the LLM with the same phantom-tool structured-output enforcement.' The implementation at packages/pi-jit-agents/src/jit-runtime.ts matches the spec doc exactly (FGAP-169: compiled.tools discarded at line 488; only phantom-tool delivered at line 568 when outputSchema set). The canonical body for jit-agents is FEAT-005 verbatim ('The subagent receives its OWN tools, JIT-composed per invocation, scoped to EXACTLY the operations the task needs'), FEAT-006 ('constrained-orchestrator → spec-block → privileged-agent → real-check-validated → attested-commit code-change loop'), DEC-0047 (capability-grant model: spec-declared, default-empty, operation-granular, clamped at dispatch — clamp requires that granted capabilities then EXIST as agent-accessible tools), TASK-091 ('agent does the work, real-check verifies, attested-commit lands'), JI-029 ('our tool for deciding valid + current is simply the work of a jit agent with perms for specific things — read, amend blocks'). The canonical body describes tool-using agents acting on granted capabilities. jit-agents-spec.md is the outlier substrate text relative to that canonical body. The spec doc is being amended to match.

evidence.reference: Body A canon: 'executeAgent is unified in-process dispatch. Phantom tool enforcement via buildPhantomTool + forced toolChoice. Thinking not passed (Anthropic API constraint documented).' Spec defines dispatch as phantom-tool-only; no multi-tool path declared.

Body A implementation: compiled.tools at line 488 (computeGrantViolation grant-clamp check, then discarded); context.tools = [phantomTool] at line 568 (only when outputSchema set). Single completeFn call at line 572. No multi-tool wiring.

Body B canon: FEAT-005 verbatim 'The subagent receives its OWN tools, JIT-composed per invocation, scoped to EXACTLY the operations the task needs'; FEAT-006 north-star 'constrained-orchestrator → spec-block → privileged-agent → real-check-validated → attested-commit code-change loop' — privileged agent is one that acts with privileges, not one that classifies.

Body B canon: DEC-0047 enacted 'capability model: spec-declared, default EMPTY, operation-granular, clamped ⊆ parent at dispatch; capability-widening writes are writer.kind=human; terminal verdict = real deterministic checks the executive cannot fake' — the clamp at dispatch logically requires that granted capabilities then exist as agent-accessible tools; line 488 implementation clamps then discards.

Body B canon: TASK-091 (completed 2026-05-28, run-work-order-loop landing) frames the loop as 'agent does the work, real-check verifies, attested-commit lands'; the loop's value-proposition depends on the agent being able to do the work.

Body B user-intent verbatim: 'our tool for deciding valid + current is simply the work of a jit agent with perms for specific things — read, amend blocks.' User-stated intent explicitly assigns read+write actions to jit agents.

Companion gap (sharpened in commit a02581f): documents the implementation-level fact that compiled.tools is structurally unreachable; this FGAP escalates from implementation-fact to substrate-coherence-issue.

gaps.impact: jit-agents-spec.md served as substrate authority for the executeAgent implementation, which built the classifier/transformer-pattern dispatch matching the spec. The rest of the substrate — capability-grant infrastructure, north-star framing, user-stated intentions, completed TASKs that depend on agent tool-use — continues to advertise tool-using-agent semantics. Authors reading the substrate find a framework that promises tool-using privileged agents and use the surface as if those promises hold; agents fail silently at dispatch. The capability-grant infrastructure built per FEAT-004/005 + DEC-0047 + TASK-089 (composeToolGrant, clamp semantics, author-tool-grant Pi tool, operation-granular grants) provides no value at agent dispatch — the grants are computed, clamped, then discarded with the tools. Every clock-menu-app agent spec declaring tools[] is broken-by-architecture as a consequence (per FGAP-169).
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: substrate coherence; canon-internal contradiction; substrate-implementation divergence
gaps.proposed_resolution: Substrate alignment: amend jit-agents-spec.md to describe multi-tool dispatch as canonical and the phantom-tool forced-toolChoice pattern as one mode-among-modes (classifier-mode for structured-output verdicts via outputSchema; agentic-mode for multi-turn tool-using work via declared tools[]). The amendment writes the canonical body's commitments (FEAT-005/006/DEC-0047/TASK-091/JI-029) into the spec doc that the implementation reads as its design source. Implementation work follows per FGAP-169 proposed_resolution (multi-tool dispatch loop in executeAgent).

gaps.title: Capability-grant infrastructure (FEAT-004/005 + DEC-0047 + TASK-089/092) operates at orchestrator-tier but is no-op at agent-dispatch tier — grants are computed and clamped at jit-runtime.ts line 488 then discarded with the declared tools; closure tracked by multi-tool dispatch loop implementation per FGAP-169
gaps.description: The capability-grant infrastructure landed across multiple completed substrate items provides no value at agent dispatch. FEAT-004 (composeToolGrant) computes parent-grant intersection with requested-grant. FEAT-005 (capability composition) commits to 'subagent receives its OWN tools, JIT-composed per invocation, scoped to EXACTLY the operations the task needs.' DEC-0047 enacts the capability-grant model: spec-declared, default-empty, operation-granular, clamped at dispatch. TASK-089 delivered composeToolGrant + 55-operation vocabulary. TASK-092 delivered Hybrid-3-v2 composite-tool infrastructure (per-KIND triples + 5-layer forbidden-wholesale + author-tool-grant). The 6 static + dynamic composite Pi tools (call-agent, author-agent-spec, run-real-checks, commit-attested, author-tool-grant, run-work-order-loop + per-config.tool_operations[] composites) exist and operate at the orchestrator-tier — orchestrator-LLM in pi REPL receives granted composite tools and exercises them. At the AGENT DISPATCH tier (call-agent dispatching a subagent), executeAgent at packages/pi-jit-agents/src/jit-runtime.ts line 488 calls computeGrantViolation(compiled.tools, dispatch.parentGrant) for the grant clamp; on pass, line 568 then registers ONLY the framework's phantom-tool (when outputSchema is set). The declared tools and their associated grant are not delivered to the LLM. Per FGAP-169: the capability-grant model logically requires that granted capabilities then EXIST as agent-accessible tools; the current implementation clamps then discards. The infrastructure operates correctly as a build-time gate and provides no runtime value at agent dispatch.

evidence.reference: Single use of compiled.tools + dispatch.parentGrant in the dispatch path: computeGrantViolation clamp check; throws GrantViolationError on subset-violation; on pass, tools + grant are not referenced again. The grant has done its only job at this point.
context.tools = [phantomTool] — the LLM receives ONLY the framework's phantom-tool, gated on if(compiled.outputSchema). The capability-granted declared tools are absent from the LLM's tool registry.

FEAT-004 status proposed/completed (composeToolGrant); FEAT-005 status proposed (capability composition: 'subagent receives its OWN tools, JIT-composed per invocation, scoped to EXACTLY the operations the task needs'). The 'subagent receives its OWN tools' commitment is unmet at the dispatch boundary.

DEC-0047 enacted (capability-grant model: spec-declared, default-empty, operation-granular, clamped at dispatch). The clamp at dispatch (line 488) executes; the granted capabilities are then discarded (not delivered to LLM).

TASK-089 completed 2026-05-27 (pi-agent-dispatch extension; FEAT-004/005 composeToolGrant + operation-vocab 55 names). TASK-092 completed 2026-05-29 (FEAT-010 Hybrid-3-v2 composite-tool infrastructure + author-tool-grant). Both shipped infrastructure that operates correctly at build/grant-time but provides no value at agent runtime.

FGAP-169 sharpened body documents the implementation-level fact (line 488/568 wiring); FGAP-177 documents the substrate-contradiction (Body A vs Body B). FGAP-178 (this) names the operational consequence: the capability-grant infrastructure is no-op at runtime.

gaps.impact: The engineering invested in FEAT-004/005, DEC-0047, TASK-089, TASK-092 (composeToolGrant + 55-operation vocabulary + Hybrid-3-v2 composite-tool infrastructure + 5-layer forbidden-wholesale clamp + author-tool-grant Pi tool + per-config.tool_operations[] dynamic composites + operation-granular grant-composition model) produces value at the orchestrator-tier but no value at the subagent-dispatch tier while the implementation strips declared tools at line 488 and never delivers them to the LLM. The author-tool-grant Pi tool authoring affordance lets operators compose grants for subagent dispatch that no subagent can exercise. The orchestrator-tier dispatch (scripts/launch-constrained-pi.sh granting composite tools to the orchestrator-LLM) operates correctly per the substrate. The subagent-tier dispatch (call-agent invoking executeAgent with declared tools[]) is the gap.
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: infrastructure dormancy; no-op capability semantics; agent-tier vs orchestrator-tier divergence
gaps.proposed_resolution: Closure tracked by Phase-3 of the canon-alignment arc: multi-tool dispatch loop in executeAgent per FGAP-169 proposed_resolution. The loop wires compiled.tools (intersected with dispatch.parentGrant) into the LLM's tool registry; the subagent dispatches its granted tools through ExtensionContext; the composeToolGrant + author-tool-grant + operation-vocabulary + composite-tool infrastructure becomes load-bearing at agent runtime. Verification: the existing composite tools (read-real-checks, commit-attested, run-work-order-loop, dynamic per-config composites) become invocable by subagents under their grants, not only by orchestrator-LLMs.

gaps.title: /context init <dir> silently ignores dir argument when .pi-context.json exists; no /context switch family for bootstrap-pointer flip + bidirectional substrate-dir navigation; blocks DEC-0036 cutover + denies operators the substrate-management primitive parallel to git switch
gaps.description: initProject at packages/pi-context/src/index.ts:266 writes the bootstrap pointer ONLY when .pi-context.json does NOT exist (condition: `if (!fs.existsSync(path.join(cwd, '.pi-context.json'))) writeBootstrapPointer(cwd, contextDir);`). When an existing pointer is present, the caller-supplied contextDir argument is completely discarded. The function proceeds with resolveContextDir(cwd) which reads the EXISTING pointer (line 267) and operates against the existing substrate dir, not the user-supplied one. Result message reports skipped against the existing dir while the user's argument named a different dir. 'Project initialized' message is misleading — project was already initialized for existing dir; user's explicit new-dir request was silently dropped. DEC-0036 (enacted 2026-05-17) declares the canonical re-derivation path with consequence #7 'Cutover (bootstrap-pointer flip + .project archive)' as the closing step. The framework has no canonical surface for that flip OR for the broader substrate-management primitive that bidirectional switching would enable. Operators need the substrate-management equivalent of git switch — flip pointer to existing substrate dir; bootstrap new substrate dir and flip; flip back to previous; list discoverable substrates; archive deprecated substrates. The substrate-as-bounded-context property (each substrate internally coherent; cross-substrate references explicit + sentinel-typed) is preserved under multi-substrate navigation. Bidirectional capability unlocks workflow shapes that do not exist today: transitional A/B during cutover, experimental substrate trees parallel to canonical, per-feature substrate scratch spaces, read-only consultation of frozen substrates without ceremony.

evidence.reference: initProject body: `if (!fs.existsSync(path.join(cwd, '.pi-context.json'))) writeBootstrapPointer(cwd, contextDir);` — pointer write is conditional on absence; with existing pointer, caller's contextDir argument is ignored

Function body after the conditional pointer-write proceeds with resolveContextDir(cwd) which reads existing pointer; dir creation operates against resolved path not against the user-supplied argument; returns created/skipped lists against the wrong dir

Enacted 2026-05-17, consequence #7 verbatim: 'Cutover (FGAP-026 Phase 10) is a bootstrap-pointer flip + .project archive, not a data migration.' The cutover step depends on a canonical surface for the pointer flip that does not exist.
Current state: {"contextDir": ".project", "version": "1.0.0", "created_at": "2026-05-10T05:27:24.196Z"} — pointer dates from 2026-05-10; pre-dates DEC-0036; no surface to flip it canonically

Operator-experience target: 'I never guess. I see the agent's health at a glance.' Same discipline applies to substrate operations: an init command that silently ignores its argument and reports success under a no-op violates the same operator-experience contract — the operator can't tell the framework did the wrong thing

Re-survey of /Users/david/Projects/clock-menu-app/.context shows init works correctly there because clock-menu-app's .pi-context.json was created at the same time as .context/ (no pre-existing pointer); the bug surfaces only when an existing pointer is being replaced — the exact DEC-0036 cutover scenario

gaps.impact: Blocks DEC-0036-mandated .context/ cutover for every existing pi-context user-substrate. Operators following the DEC-0036-directed path get silent no-op + misleading success message. Workaround paths violate canonical-authoring discipline: direct .pi-context.json edit bypasses pi-dispatch auth-gate (FGAP-134/138) + dispatch attestation; manual .context/ scaffolding bypasses install-ceremony invariants. Beyond the immediate cutover blocker: absence of a /context switch family denies operators the substrate-management primitive needed for transitional cutover work (substrates side-by-side during transition), experimental substrate design (try new block-kind shapes in throwaway substrate without affecting canonical), per-arc substrate scratch (focused work in isolated substrate then merge back), and read-only consultation of frozen substrates (DEC-0036's archaeological .project/ access pattern). The operator-experience parity with git's substrate-of-record management (git switch + git branch) is the model; substrate-management today lacks even the basic flip primitive.
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: bootstrap-pointer flip; substrate dir switch; cutover canonical surface
gaps.proposed_resolution: /context switch command family modeled on git switch, with bootstrap-pointer flip as one mode among bidirectional substrate-dir navigation operations. Command surface: (a) `/context switch <existing-dir>` — flip pointer to existing substrate dir; validate target is a valid substrate (has config.json) before flipping; (b) `/context switch -c <new-dir>` — bootstrap new substrate dir (writeBootstrapPointer + create dirs via initProject-equivalent) AND flip pointer to it; (c) `/context switch -` — flip to previous contextDir per pointer.previous_contextDir field; (d) `/context list` companion — list discoverable substrate dirs in cwd (scan for dirs with config.json); (e) `/context archive <dir>` separate command — move substrate dir to archive/<dir>/ + write-protect; non-default operation requiring explicit invocation (substrate-delete more dangerous than git branch -d so no `-d` flag on switch). Pointer-shape extension v1.0.0 → v1.1.0: add optional `previous_contextDir`, `switched_at`, `switched_by` fields. Migrated via existing write-schema-migration mechanism per FGAP-136 (identity migration; old pointer shape remains readable). Cross-substrate semantics: edges within relations.json are substrate-scoped; cross-substrate references use sentinel form (`<substrate-name>:<id>` or `project-archive:<id>`); new relation_type `references_cross_substrate_item` carries explicit cross-substrate edges; lazy resolution via consultation tool. Auth-gate routing: every switch operation modifies the pointer = structural substrate change with significant operator-flow consequence; routes through pi-dispatch auth-gate (FGAP-134/138) for writer.kind=human attestation + interactive confirmation; switch history auditable via pointer's switched_at + switched_by fields (potentially extended to switch-history array). initProject argument-handling fix: when existing pointer differs from caller's contextDir, fail loudly with structured guide-message pointing to `/context switch -c <dir>` as the correct command rather than silently dropping the argument. The init + switch commands form a guided-handoff: init bootstraps fresh substrate when none exists; switch handles all subsequent substrate-dir transitions. DEC-0036's cutover step becomes the first use of `/context switch -c .context` (operator-directed, auth-gate-confirmed, attestation-stamped).
